elections, will not answer for the county. Do
not fail, sisters, to put yourselves right in this
matter, so that you may be prepared to cast
your vote, which will be quite an important
factor at the November election. What will ap-
ply to the sisters in this county will also hold
good for the women of every other county in

[Column 2]

the Territory of Utah, Sisters, let us be wise
and act well our part, that there may be no
blame attached to us in this matter, which is of
vital interest. In this grand department of
public weal, in this juncture of affairs, which
calls for united effort, woman bears the same
relation to the subject at issue as man, and
wherever there are serious entanglements or
difficulties, woman is as great a sufferer
as man, and many times more so
The burden of oppression, the horror of
war often falls heaviest on defenceless women
and children. God has given us freedom, let us
be grateful. He has given us the ballot; let us
use it wisely for the protection of those we love
and the maintenance of peace and good order.
Let us act wisely and in concert with our breth-
ren in this matter, for what benefits one
blesses the other correspondingly, and there
must be union of effort with men and women, if
any really great good is to be accomplished.

author: Unknown
title: WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | December | Issue: 10 | Title: WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE. | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE.

	The American Fork Woman's Suffrage
Association held a special meeting at that
place on Wednesday Oct. 28th. Pres. Hannah
Lapish in the chair. Sarah M. Kimball, Ter.
Pres., and Emily S. Richards, 1st. Vice Pres.
of Salt Lake City, were in attendance. A
large number of members were present. The
meeting house was tastefully decorated for the
occasion and presented an elaborate picture
of art and taste, amidst the portraits of Mrs.
Cady Stanton, Miss Susan B. Anthony, sat Mrs.
Kimball, Mrs. Lapish and Mrs. Evans, and
the stars and stripes floated upon every side.
	The best speakers in the Territory were
represented at the meeting, and their reports
of the progress of the Association were very
encouraging. Mrs. Greenhalgh a laborer for
fifty years for the suffrage cause, said the
women should have equal rights with men, and
there were political positions that women
should fill. Utah women should work for
their rights, for they of all women have more
freedom than any other women in the world.
She said she was proud of the work done by
the American Fork women.
	Ter. Pres. Kimball, said she was a suffragist,
but not a lecturer. Was not an advocate for
women's superiority but that women should
stand on equality with men. She then gave
her ideas concerning the mission of women.
	Electa Bullock, county president, com-
mended the American Fork Association upon
the good work they are accomplishing.
	Emily Richards said that women would
make the best jurors, especially where women
are to be tried. She did not wish men to think
that women wanted to rule,—they only want
equal rights with the sterner sex. Woman
need not neglect her household duties while
attending to politics. She thereby can make
home all the more cheerful.
	M. Garff then favored the assemblage with
the association's Sunday song, "Equal Right's
Banner."
	Joanna Patten said if women had the
right to vote there would not be so many dens
of iniquity to allure the young.
	Bishop Halliday then addressed the suff-
ragists in his usual interesting and instructive
manner. He complimented the association
upon the work they were doing. He claimed
if the women were to receive the franchise and
vote they would do much to suppress in-
temperance, said that is the evil of this place.
He hoped the women would be successful in
their attempt.
	Msr. Stewart said the time was at our door
when the women would have the right of
franchise.
	Mary A. Evans sang the very interesting
song, "Where is My Boy To-night."
	After which benediction was offered by
Joanna Patten and the meeting was dismissed.

author: Unknown
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | March | Issue: 19 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: Unknown]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	THE black veil was taken, Tuesday, Jan.
9, by six young women at the convent of
the Sisters of Mercy in Hartford, Ct.
	MRS. CAROLINE A. Soule, in Nebraska,
on Sunday, Dec. 19, organized the First
Universalist parish at Tecumseh with
thirty-two members. "Surely the world
moves."—Ex.
	IT IS stated that the House-committee
on Territories have agreed to report un-
favorably on the admission of Utah as a
state. It is thought nothing will be done
to deprive women in this Territory of the 
the ballot.
	THE NEW Pope, Leo XIII, has been for-
mally installed in office. There are various
reports in circulation as to the future course
of action in reference to the Italian Govern-
ment. It is now believed he will for the 
present remain in seclusion in the Vatican.
	MRS. ISABELLA Beecher Hooker had a
hearing before the senate-committee on
privileges and elections, on the 22d ult.
She said the corner-stone of the American
Republic was God's own doctrine of per-
sonal liberty and personal responsibility.
She defined Liberty as the steam, Respon-
sibility as the brakes, election-day the safe-
ty-valve. She maintained among other
things the assertion that Lucretia Mott
could long ago have settled peacefully some
of the most unpopular and knotty problems
of the day without a standing army.

[Column 2]

	TELEGRAMS OF the 28th ult. state that
the treaty of peace between Russia and 
Turkey is on the point of being signed. It
is thought possible the Grand Duke Nicho-
las will visit the Sultan at Constantinople.
The 26, Vienna Special says: Austria will
not submit to some of the preliminary ar-
rangements for peace between the two
great hostile powers as it will infringe upon
her grounds.
	THE Princess Mercedes, King Alfonso's
future wife, though bred in France, is a 
Spaniard at heart, and insists on being call-
ed Spanish, yet there has hitherto been a 
strong prejudice against her marriage, her
father, the Duc de Montpensier, being wide-
ly unpopular because of the fatal duel with 
his cousin, Don Enrique de Bourdon, and
his pronunciamento of Cadiz, which began
the series of political convulsions that has
hardly ceased to agitate Spain.
	MISS RHODA Broughton, the authoress,
lives the greater part of the time in the
beautiful vale of Clwyd, Wales, and has
doubtless derived her love of scenery and
her power of describing it from constantly
having before her the mountains and vales
of that beautiful country. She is about
thirty years of age, and is described as
"spiritual in expression, with a light, quick,
impatient manner. Her face is an intel-
lectual one, with an appearance of active
imagination. She has a good figure, of 
about the average height.
	THE WOMAN SUFFRAGE HEARING.—On
Tuesday, Jan. 29, the Committee on Wo-
man Suffrage of the Massachusetts Legis-
lature, gave a public hearing to the peti-
tioners, in the Green Room of the State
House, in Boston. The attendance was
very large, and evidently in sympathy
with the petitioners. The first speaker was
Mr. Henry B. Blackwell, who said:—
	Mr. Chairman and Gentlemen:—In be-
half of the thousands of petitioners who 
are already before you, and of thousands
more, who, day by day, are sending in simi-
lar petitions, I desire to state, 1st, What we
ask: 2d, Why we ask: and 3d, By whom
the request is made.
	1. For a change in the law which regu-
lates elections for town, city and county
officers, removing the restriction of sex, so
that, hereafter, women may be enabled to
vote in such elections, on the same terms as
men.
	2. For a change in the law which regu-
lates Presidential elections, removing the
restriction of sex, so that hereafter women
may be enabled to vote for electors of Pres-
ident and Vice-President of the United
States.
	3. For a Joint Resolve for a Constitution-
al Amendment abolishing all political dis-
tinctions on account of sex.
	To make the first and second steps, no
change in the Constitution is needed, but
only a majority of the Senate and House of
Representatives with the assent of the Gov-
ernor.
	To change the Constitution requires the 
action of two successive Legislatures, rati-
fied afterwards by a majority of the quali-
fied voters.
	So much for what we ask; now why do
we ask it?

[Column 3]

	1st. Because the fundamental principle
of American Government affirms, that all
mankind are endowed by nature with cer-
tain inalienable rights of life, liberty and
the pursuit of happiness, and to secure
these rights governments are instituted,
deriving their just powers from the consent
of the governed. Every one will admit
that a woman has the same right to life,
liberty and the pursuit of happiness as a 
man; that to secure these rights govern-
ment exists; that she is governed; why
then should she be forbidden to consent or
dissent like other citizens? What is Suf-
frage? The authoritative expression of an
opinion. What is its essence? Rational
choice. Are rational choice and its ex-
pression a masculine function? Are they
a feminine function? No; they are a human
function. We ask that women may vote,
for the same reason that they vote already
as stockholders in a railroad company or in
a manufacturing corporation. They are
equal stockholders, as citizens, in the great
political corporation called Government,
and it is unjust to deprive them of their
equal expression, since they are equally in-
terested in its safety and prosperity.
	But the principles of our State Constitu-
tion affirm Woman's right to Suffrage.
	All power resides in the people and is de-
rived from them." Women are people.
	"The people...have a right to institute
government and to reform, alter or change
the same." They can do so only by voting.
	Again, we ask Suffrage for women, be-
cause they are different from men. There
are no two classes of human beings in so-
ciety so different from each other as the 
larger classes known as men and women.
Each class has special qualities more highly
developed than the other. I need hardly
stop to enumerate them. The qualities
which specially characterize women are the
very ones in which our government is de-
ficient. Women excel men in gentleness,
temperance, chastity, economy, and respect
for law. They are and always will be the 
wives and mothers of men. Woman Suf-
frage therefore means the representation of 
the Home; the domestic interests directly
expressed by women, just as the business
interests are directly expressed by men.
Instead of an aristocracy of sex, which is 
class legislation, we want human nature
manifested in a truly representative gov-
ernment of men and women.—"Woman's
Journal."
	Most substantial and positive reasous
were brought to bear in this meeting by
both men and women. Mrs. Lucy Stone's
speech was pathetic in parts, and rich with
the pathos of womanly feeling. Miss Ann
C. Garlin's speech was short but contained
some forcible arguments strongly present-
ed. Mr. Garrison dwelt very much upon
reform and what urgent measures it requir-
ed, etc. Mrs. E. H. Webster, George T.
Stevens and Miss Lorenza Haynes were the
other speakers and received great applause
from the audience. They presented such
claims and backed them with such conclu-
sive evidences, illustrations and facts, that
in reading them over one feels that they
ought to convince the most bitter opponents
of Woman's Suffrage.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

author: Unknown
title: LEGAL INCONSISTENCY.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol1 | 1872 | January | Issue: 17 | Title: LEGAL INCONSISTENCY. | Author: Unknown]

LEGAL INCONSISTENCY. 
	
	Some of the lawyers of this city—twenty-six,
indeed—have signed a document, published last
week in the “Salt Lake Herald,” which is at
once an accusation against the Territory and a
memorial to Congress, setting forth some of the
usual charges against the people of Utah which
ought to be too stale now to be rewarmed and
served up again for the thousandth time, by
these gentlemen. The “Herald” has been
handling the document without gloves, and
showing in plain terms its unfairness, weak- 
ness, unjust statements and bitter partisanship;
but one portion of it demands notice by the
EXPONENT. It covertly attacks the right of the
women of Utah to the elective franchise, by
attacking the law conferring the franchise upon
them:  We say covertly, and might add that
the entire document is gotten up in such style,
is not straightforward, nor honorable, but reads
more like the plea of a pettifogger in one of the
lower courts than a composition such as would
be expected from twenty-six individuals who
are entitled by law and usage to be styled
“learned gentlemen.” The document submits
“that the act of February 12th, 1870, prostitutes
the right of suffrage by conferring it upon an
alien woman without even qualification of time
of residence.” The simplest and most complete
way of refuting this is by quoting the act itself,
which being short, we here append in full:
				AN ACT.
Conferring upon women the elective franchise.
	(Approved February 12, 1870.)
	Sec. 1. Be it enacted by the Governor and
Legislative Assembly of the Territory of Utah:
That every woman of the age of twenty-one
years who has resided in this Territory six
months next preceding any general election,


born; or naturalized in the United States, or
who is the wife, widow, or daughter of a native-
born or naturalized citizen of the United States,
shall be entitled to vote at any election in this
Territory.
	Sec. 2. All laws or parts of laws conflicting
with this Act are hereby repealed.
	The act is so plain that it seems impos-
sible any one would attempt to dispute the fact
that it convicts these gentlemen of willful and
deliberate misstatements, with intent to de-
ceive.
	In looking over the names we note some who
were at one time very anxious that the women
of Utah should possess the elective franchise.
They were then, as they are now, aspirants for
notoriety and power; and they imagined that
women were weak, pliable beings, who could
not withstand their wonderfully eloquent
sophistries, but would rush to the polls enthusi-
astically determined to give these wordy
champions the desired elevation to office and
emoluments. But when the women of Utah
received the elective franchise, it was speedily
apparent that they could think and act for
themselves, and that they would vote for the
candidates whom they believed most suitable;
and they actually did vote for the men who had
conferred upon them the legal right to vote.
Then came a change with these before-time
self-proclaimed champion of the  "down-trod-
den women of Utah.” They found they had
made a mistake, and reckoned without suffi-
cient acquaintance with independent woman-
nature; and they have since been more loudly
urgent that Congress should step forward and
annul the Territorial statute than they ever
were that the women of Utah should receive
the elective franchise.
	As it is always well to know our friends, and
as some of our readers or their husbands, fathers,
or sons, may, unfortunately, have occasion to
require the services of a lawyer, we append the
names of the twenty-six who urge upon Con-
gress legislation inimical, to Utah, and the
taking from the women of Utah the right of
suffrage.
	J. B. Bosborough,		R. H. Robertson
	D. Cooper,			C. W. Bennett,
	Wm. Haydon,		C. K.Gilchrist,
	Frank Tilford,			C. H. Morgan
	D. J. Toohy,			Sol. P. McCurdy,
	E. P. Johnson,		J. N. H. Patcick,
	Wm. Hyndman,		Wm. P. Appleby,
	James M. Carter,		M. Kirkpatrick,
	Thomas Marshall,		Jno. R. McBride,
	Jonathan C. Royle,	Warren Earll,
	Wm. C. Hall,			George E. Whitney,
	Jas. G. Spratt,			Wells Spicer,
	W.W. Gee,			R. N. Baskin.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.				141

author: Unknown
title: WOMEN IN POLITICS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | August | Issue: 4 | Title: WOMEN IN POLITICS. | Author: Unknown]

WOMEN IN POLITICS.

	Almost every day and sometimes more than
once in a day we are asked the question, "What
are you women going to do in political matters in
Utah?" Occasionally one adds "are you going to
organize on national lines?" I suppose people
think, newspaper women are the ones who ought
to talk or have an opinion on the subject.
	As for doing, studying politics to begin with
and finding out how few rights women have com-
pared to men, and how far they are protected in
those few rights, might do to begin with, con-
sidering, the Edmund's Tucker law dealt such a
sweeping blow to all women voters, that there is
scarcely a shadow of a chance to have the fran-
chise restored until very radical changes take place
in the condition of things in this Territory.
	It is possible, to be sure, for women to decide
in their own minds, whether they lean more to the
Republican or Democratic party, but it is an old
saying "the proof of the pudding is in eating it,"
and as neither the one or the other of these or-
ganizations seem disposed to recognize women
in any positive way, women are not very likely to
do more than to look on, and see those who have
it in their own hands, appreciate all the golden
opportunities to benefit themselves.
	It is sometimes a good thing to stand and wait,
and as waiting seems to be about the only thing
women can do at present in a political way, per-
haps its just as well to keep silence too, though
"they do say" that is impossible for women.
There is an opportunity to look on and learn and
if failures are useful lessons, there may be some-
thing gained.
	The Woman Suffrage Associations throughout
the Territory ought to be wide awake in plans
and methods of study that will give them practi-
cal as well as theoretical information, that will
be available at any and every point where wo-
men can help in securing good and honest gov-
ernment for all even if they do have to pay taxes,
and have no voice in saying what shall be done
with the money.
	There are some places in which woman's help
may be asked for, and certainly many places
where it is needed, even if she has no vote. The
way matters stand here with reference to women
it would be best to work for the Sixteenth
Amendment to Congress. In fact we always
have believed in the Sixteenth Amendment, that
would be killing a good many birds with one
stone. That would help all women and when
they had the ballot secured to them by an act of
Congress, it wouldn't be possible for the Legis-
lature or Governor of any State or Territory to
say it wasn't legal and whisky rings couldn't
buy up the votes and enter protest. Our idea is
the sixteenth amendment, and by that time we
will have studied the national questions and
[Column 2]
policies pretty thoroughly, and be prepared to
decide. In the mean time if there is any open-
ing for women, any possible chance to give her
political equality, there are those shrewd enough
to observe it, and report all along the line; so
that immediate action could be taken.
	Women are sufficiently interested in each other
to watch the progress of affairs politically as well
as spiritually, and to take advantage of any
suggestion that will promote the advancement
of their interest. The signs of the times signify
the onward progress that is being made through
organized effort, and the effective help women are
likely to render each other and all humanity in
the great upward movement. The increase of
love towards one another through these united
efforts is one of the most hopeful signs of the
age. The gathering together of women of so
many Nationalities and assimilating their thoughts
and ideas is almost a marvel. Even here in Utah
how many different nations are represented in
Womens' Associations. It proves conclusively
that there is a power and influence binding women
together, such as has not been heretofore; this
spirit pervades the most highly civilized and en-
lightened portions of the earth. No one can
doubt this who reads of the great organizations
of women all over the United States, Canada,
Great Britain, France, Scandinavia, and many
other countries of the old world. While all these
great events are transpiring outside, we at home
in Utah will assuredly be occupied with important
matters, civil, political, educational, and spiritual;
therefore it behooves the women of Utah to study
and find out the great underlying principles of all
good government, and note how these principles
are applied in the national parties, but it does not
yet appear to be the time of woman's choosing in
political issues until further developments of diffi-
cult questions now pending are made in this Terri-
tory.


[Column 1]

author: Unknown
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol15 | 1886 | December | Issue: 13 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: Unknown]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	The New York Bureau of Labor Statistics
reports that 200,000 women in New York City
represent ninety-two trades.

	The nineteenth annual Washington conven-
tion of the National Woman's Suffrage Asso-
ciation will be held Jan. 25, 26 and 27. Head-
quarters at the Riggs house.

	The House of representatives of Vermont
passed the bill granting municipal suffrage to
women by a vote of 135 to 82. Rhode Island
has also passed a bill of the same character.

	Dr. Newman has permitted the introduction
of a novelty in his Washington church, in the
shape of a reply from the members of the con-
gregation to the statements of the preacher.
Much good might come from such a plan in
many churches.

	The Liberal ladies of England recently held
a great demonstration at Cheshire, at which
time it was decided to form a Woman's Liberal
Association to combat the work of the Prim-
rose League and to contribute to the return of
Mr. Gladstone to power at the next election.

	The National Woman's Suffrage Association
will celebrate at Washington, D. C., in 1888,
the fortieth anniversary of the first woman
suffrage convention ever held. At this time a
World's Woman Suffrage Association will be
formed, and advocates of this reform in all
countries are invited to participate.

	A Vermont correspondent informs us that
women in that State must own taxable property
in order to vote for school committee, but need
not own two hundred dollars’ worth. The pay-
ment of a tax on proporty, however small,
qualifies women to vote. Men, on the other
hand, can vote upon payment of a poll-tax.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

author: Unknown
title: N. A. W. S. CONVENTION.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol19 | 1890 | April | Issue: 19 | Title: N. A. W. S. CONVENTION. | Author: Unknown]

N. A. W. S. CONVENTION.

	I am from the first Territory that by its
 laws recognized the ballot as the natural birth-
right of woman as well as man; and a near 
neighbor of the only State in the Union that
recognizes in its Constitution that the consent
of woman is essential to the just exercise of
power in the government to make laws for
her guidance.
	I beg you will not deem it egotism when I
remind you that the Eagle, emblem of our gov-
ernment is indigenous to a mountain country,
and that there is a something in the rarified
atmosphere of high altitudes that impels the
human soul to pant for greater heights of
human liberty than is today enjoyed by the
masses. It is not strange then that the practi-
cal disenthrallment of woman, should first be-
come crystallized into organic laws in the wilds
of the Rocky Mountains.
	The branches of the Woman's Suffrage As-
sociation in Utah Territory are in a flourish-
ing condition, our meetings are frequently held
and numerously attended, and I believe a close
scrutiny of the statistical reports will show
that a larger porportion of the women of Utah
are enrolled as members of the Association
than in any State or Territory of the United
States.
	The peculiar circumstances surrounding
Utah and her political and social relations,
are such as cause the questions of the extension
and limitation of the right to vote to possess
peculiar and grave significance to all classes
of her citizens, and to elicit extensive discus-
sion by her people, about the basic principles
underlying the rights or privileges of the elec-
tive franchise. The people of Utah of all
classes, are driven by the force of circumstances
to think on this subject, and it is very gratify-
ing to note, that the more people think about 
it, the larger are the numbers of those who
advocate Woman Suffrage as an abstract
right, and as a proposed practical amelioration
of the evils which now infest society, which
man alone without the aid of woman has
hitherto been unable to successfully grapple
with and overcome.
	The fact that deep, earnest thought on this
subject generally makes converts to our theory
is all but conclusive proof, that the advocacy
of Woman Suffrage is one of those revolutions
of human society that never go backward,
hence, although we in Utah have once pos.
sessed, but we are now deprived of the fran.

[Column 2]

chise, we cannot but hope that as agitation of
this question is increased, those who now pos-
sess power may be induced to think on this
subject, and confidently expect that calm,
sober thought by the statesmen of our nation
will lead them to adopt such measures as will
permit all the women of our nation to exercise
this high privilege of citizenship.
	The fundamental principle upon which rep-
resentative Government is based is "that all
just Governments derive their powers from
the consent of the governed." Can a Govern-
ment truly be said to be just, when it deprives
one-half of the independent intelligences it
assumes to govern, of the only measure now
known by which their consent can be ascer-
tained or expressed? We think not, and so 
insist upon the theory that a truly representa-
tive Government is based upon an expression
of the free will of every individual member
who is capable of intelligently expressing his
or her will.
	It is freely conceded that insane persons do
not possess intelligent wills, hence they
cannot express them, and that children whose
minds need training and development cannot
but be dependent upon their tutors, hence their
consent is implied by the laws of existence, but
there is no law of being that makes woman's
will dependent upon the will of man. The
sexes complement each other, they travel upon
parallel lines, side by side with each other,
naturally endowed with similar aspirations,
each in its own sphere with equal intelligence,
amenable to the same laws, both human and
divine, each with individual responsibil-
ities, each entitled to personal liberty of body
and what in the near future must be con-
sidered as a higher natural freedom, the right
to express their individual choice through
casting their ballots just as we do.
	Holding these views we meet often together
to discuss social and political questions, desir-
ing that we may be fully able to discharge our
responsibilities when we shall be permitted to
exercise our rights, fondly hoping that the time
is not far distant when intelligent women will
be permitted to vote for men and measures
that will tend to elevate society; when a wo-
man's vote will be at least equal to that of a
man, who may have cast his ballot when tem-
porarily insane by intoxication.

author: EMILY SCOTT
title: WHAT A GENTILE WOMAN THINKS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol12 | 1883 | May | Issue: 2b | Title: WHAT A GENTILE WOMAN THINKS. | Author: EMILY SCOTT]

WHAT A GENTILE WOMAN THINKS.

	Well, this is election day, and I am out of
temper; there is so much to stir up all the sar-
casm, contempt and anger in one's nature. The
elections are for township and corporation offic-
ials. We have in near vicinity a settlement
of colored folks who really, by their numbers,
carry the election.	*	*	*	*
	There! there goes an old colored man who
has been in the workhouse all winter and is not
much more than half-witted, yet he is being
taken over to the polls to cast his vote.	*	*
Last year the Democrats played a joke on
this old negro; he happened to go to their Pri-
mary, through mistake, and they told him it
obligated him to vote the democratic ticket,
So, on election day the old man hid and did
not venture out all day for fear he would have
to vote the democratic ticket. Such intelli-
gence is required to be a voter! Woman nev-
er can understan politics.
	Here goes a wagon with five colored men,
two Germans who are never sober, and the bal-
ance Americans. They are all men, at least,
wear men's apparel; thank goodness, they are
not women, so they must be men, therefore,
capable of having a say in the making of laws
for our corporation. Do I wish to vote? No,
and yes. Do I think it will improve matters
to allow women to vote? I am not sure, for
there are so many bad and so many noodle-pa-
ted women who would go to the polls for no
higher purpose than to "create a sensation;"
and their vote would count as much as the one
cast by a thoughtful, intelligent mother, who
prayerfully watches the body politic as she
watches the physical, moral and mental devel-
opment of her own loved children. Centuries
ago the question was asked, "What must I do
to be saved?" Bob lngersoll has made a bur-
lesque of the question, and people smile, for-
getting the jailer who fell down at the feet of
Paul and Silas, and think of the eloquent in-
fidel orator. But others, aged parents, wives,
sisters and sweethearts, many thousands are cry-
ing in their hearts as they see their loved ones
going to destruction, swift unto ruin, and see
the laws of the land made by saloon keepers,
profligates, drunkards and their dupes to suit
themselves, they say, "What must we do to be
Saved?"
	In the early days of the Colonies, when
men's lives were threatened at all times by the
Indian foe and they found their ideas of war-
fare must change; that they could not choose
a leader by his orthodox belief in infant bap-
tism, that although they wer equipped
"corselets, muskets, bardaliers and portable
rests from which to shoot, and swords," the In-
dians got the better of them, killed them while
they were getting ready to shoot. They did
change their ideas and laid aside the old rules
of warfare.
	When the Constitution of the United States
was framed, the inhabitants of the country and
everything that pertains to the country were
very different from the present. Again it is
time for ideas to change; evils of various
kinds, as sly and devilish as the wily Indian,
creep upon the honest law maker and cut him
off. Every man, woman and child who desires
to save the country from destruction, who have
the good of the community at heart, must be on
the alert. Women in old times snatched up
the guns of their dead husbands and killed the
foe, they bore all hardships and dangers by his
side and were an equal and helpmeet. Women
did not vote then? No; nor do women need to
shoot Indians now; but she needs to stand side
by side with her husband, and have the right
to defend herself and her children against the
foes which beset them on every side.
							EMILY SCOTT.
	Ohio.

author: Unknown
title: SUFFRAGE.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol19 | 1890 | March | Issue: 18 | Title: SUFFRAGE. | Author: Unknown]

SUFFRAGE.

	In the early history of our country, suffrage
was limited to church members, then to
property owners, and later all restrictions were
lifted from the citizens of a free Government,
who were endowed with sufficient intel-
lectuality, to become competent judges at the
age of twenty-one. The last advancement
removed a much greater obstacle, and this
time it was decreed that race or color should
form no consideration. We now wish to
insert only the one word "sex," to make it
read race, color, or sex, then there will be no

[Column 2]

limit to the broad platform of freedom upon
which our glorious Constitution stands.
	This word should have been inserted by the
signers of the Constitution, but it was not and
the omission has caused and is causing a great
deal of unnecessary trouble.
	Why do we want suffrage you say? Why
did our forefathers wish to be represented in
the English Parlament, and have a voice in
the law-making power? Because one person
cannot justly represent another without
having experienced similar feelings. And for
the same reason we desire to place a candidate
in the halls of Congress, and aid in the making
of the laws, for if they materially conflict
with a majority of the governed, they cannot
be classed as good, or be upheld by the
populace, hence it is of vital importance that
representatives of the people should formulate
them. As it now stands only one half of the
citizens of the United States are represented
in Congress.
	One class rules the other by the hand of
tyranny. They have no voice in the laws, the
amount of taxes they shall pay, or who shall
fill the public trusts.
	In Massachusetts those who wish to vote
must first work a poll tax. Were it possible
for women to gain suffrage by paying a poll-
tax, there would be hundreds and hundreds
seen at the polls every election day. And
why would the test be unjust? The paupers,
infirm, demented and vagabond, would be
debarred. Men or women who shared not in
political enthusiasm, would not care to pay a
dollar to secure a privilege that was not
valued.
	The polls are not necessarily a place of
uncouth conduct and boisterous language.
If women were expected and continually
appearing, quiet would reign; men would
not so far forget themselves as to indulge in
abusive language or amuse themselves by
fist fighting in the presence of ladies. It is
just as Henry Ward Beecher says, that "rude-
ness at the polls is due to woman's absence,"
that "her office is to carry with her whatever
is seemly and decorous."
	We do not want to fill man's sphere, or
rule over him as Victoria does over her subjects.
We believe that man was placed at the head
by God, but that woman's place is at their
right hand. We would like to be represented
in all political circles, to let merit, not sex,
regulate the amount due for services rendered,
to be allowed to fill some appropriate offices
under the Government, and to have a voice
in the election of the same. If, as some say,
it would be like giving every man two votes to
allow each woman to cast one, as he would
be his wife's dictator, it would pay them just to
please and satisfy the women, for do they not
make for us a heaven, or a hell? And there
could be no harm in simply doubling the
number of votes, as the ultimate result would
not be changed. But who would dictate to
the bachelor's wives, the widows and the un-
married? Oh! that is not the point, they fear
that women would not always be influenced by
their husbands, and that wicked and immoral
men would not be elected. As one writer
says "Women are too scrupulous and exacting
in their choice of a candidate to be successful
in practical politics." Would that more of
the men were afflicted with the same disease!
	Why are women not as competent to vote as
men? They are oftener sober, a greater
number of them are honest, fewer commit
murder, suicide, burglary, robbery, etc., a
less ratio become insane, beggars, or visitants
of the poor house and gambling den.
Woman has the early care and training of the
child, she plants the first seeds, makes the
first and lasting impressions; and the in-
fluences that she exerts over the child's mind
remains with it to its dying day.

[Column 3]

	It is impossible for man to go very far in
advance of woman, or for woman to far
surpass man in intellectuality, but the develop-
ment of the one means the onward march of
the other, and a systematic development of
both, for do they not have the same origin?
	We cannot impart to others what we do
not possess; and when it becomes an
acknowledged fact that she is best fitted to
perform the duties of a wife and mother,
and to have the care and training of the
representatives of our future government, who
is best informed upon political, civil, religious,
and to have the care and training of the
representitves of our future government, who
is best informed upon political, civil, religeous
and domestic affairs, the zenith of woman's
glory will have been reached, and great things
may be expected from the offspring of
such a people.
		LEXIA HARRIS.

author: Unknown
title: CONCLUDED.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol8 | 1879 | March | Issue: 19 | Title: CONCLUDED. | Author: Unknown]

CONCLUDED.
 Last winter the legislature of Massachu-
setts passed a bill allowing women to vote
on school questions--and already, thousands
of the best and most intelligent  women in
that state are  registering,  and  many have
already assisted at school elections.  In the
Mo. Legislature last session, several women,
of St. Louis, were sent as delegates  to  the
Assembly to present arguments in  favor of
amendments, giving the  right  of  suffrage
to women.  They were courteously received
and listened to.  Several gentlemen  spoke
in support of their arguments.  Among the
number,  Major  Merwin,  editor  of  the 
American Journal of Education and Rev.
Dr. Eliot, of St. Louis,  President  of the
Washington University.  Last winter Con-
gress recived  petitions for a 16th  Amend-
ment to the Constitution, giving the ballot
to women, signed by tens of  thousands of
honest and intelligent  women from every
state in the Union--and they  were not all
sour old maids  either--but most of them
loving, home-keeping mothers.
 For over four years the women of Wyom-
ing have voted, and the experiment seems
to be  attended with no startling  results.
Judge Kingman says, "That since the wo-
men  vote  in Wyoming that rowdyism
and drunkeness are banished from the polls,
and that the best classes of women take an
interest in the elections  without creating a
ripple in the home circle."  As a temper-
ance measure the same high authority con-
siders the ballot in woman's hand indispen-
sible.
The able minority report of Senators Hoar,
Mitchell and Cameron may be regarded as
a very marked indication  of the  progress
of our work.  There is no shutting  your 
eyes to the truth, that the thinking women
of America, from Abagail Adams, of nearly
a hundred years ago, down are not satisfied
with the old order of things, but insist that
the very spirit of our so called free institu-
tions, demands for our sex, perfect equality
without which is mockery to talk of a free
country, or a free people.
 Twenty years ago the enthusiastic Aboli-
tionist who predicted  the  emancipation of
the colored men on  this  continent, in less
than a decade, was considered insane and 
today many of you may think the  cause I
advocate equally hopeless, but I a mfirmly
convinced, that the dawn of the new cen-
tury will find the question settled and wo-
man clothed  with  the  full  powers,  and
privileges of citizenship.  You men of Holt
County, will soon have an opportunity to
place yourselves on record.  See to it  that
you come with clean hands, clear heads, and
pure motives to try our case, remember we
can really do nothing without  your assist-
ance and if you use the reason  God  has
given  you,  to  weigh  and  measure  our
claims, you must see that only one course is
open to you.  Mothers train your sons to
feel that their first vote will be but another
blow for your enfranchisment.  Teach your
daughters to struggle now, and forever for
the God given right, to life liberty and the
pursuit of happiness in her own way, bear-
ing in mind that we can  have no power to
protect ourselves  from  unjust  taxation, or
unequal laws, until we have our political
disabilities removed.  We can never realize
the full idea of a representative government,
while such  women  as  Maria Mitchell the
astornomer or Anna Dickenson the  orntor,
may be represented by a bundle  of  newly
arrived foreign  ignorance, or a drunken
negro outvotes  the queen of the  rostrum,
and so on down to the millions  of quiet
home loving women who want a voice at
least on the whiskey question.  That the
revolution we ask  for, will bring  some
changes no one can doubt, but knowing in
whom, and what they have believed  new
seas of agitation can have no perils for those
who sail  by the compass  of truth.  The
same law which holds oceans in its bed, and
planets in their courses will inspire  those
two mysterious sovereigns we call man and
woman to transact their gayest, and gravest
affairs with no jar or hurt, but mutual bene-
fit to each other and perpetual help to all
mankind.
The coming woman will be no masculine
femininity or the opposite, but with greater
physical vigor of finer tiber, she  will  bring
melting tenderness, mental force, and moral
rectitude such as our  country  vainly sighs
for elsewhere.
Then friends of the sterner sex, read and
ponder well, before you cast your vote on
this great question and give us a fair ver-
dict.  We are not trying to buy, or beg  or
steal your influence, but as you already pos-
sess  and  enjoy  the  highest privileges of
citizenship, we ask  you not  to  perpetuate
our subjection, through wilful ignorance.


[Column 1]

author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT
title: OUR FRANCHISE.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol11 | 1882 | May | Issue: 24 | Title: OUR FRANCHISE. | Author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT]

OUR FRANCHISE.

	If the Legislature of the Territory of Utah
had the right to grant the franchise to the
women of the Territory, (and they having vio-
lated no law of the Constitution of the United
States) by what right, political or otherwise,
shall Senator Edmunds, or any other man, say
it shall be taken away? He says it is because
they vote as a unit, "because they vote as they
are compelled by their husbands." I ask, why
he should complain, if we, as the "poor women"
of Utah, choose to vote for men whom we
have proven to be men of God; because 
vote for men who have proven themselves to
be honorable and just, who conceive it to be
their duty to maintain the principles of true
liberty, to promote the welfare of their con-
stituents; and because we claim the right of
Americans, to vote according to our own con-
victions of right; we claim the right to vote
for freedom of thought and speech, and to wor-
ship God according to those convictions; we, as
a community, claim the right to vote for men
that are God-fearing, that will perpetuate the 
principles of virtue, of holiness, of honesty, of
integrity, and good will to all men, that will 
implant in the minds of the community at
large that it is by unity we stand; it is by
unity that any enterprise (whether political,
social, or moral) will become a success. The

[Column 2]

whole world is striving for a unity of action;
the Democrats are striving with all their might
for a unity of action in the interests
of their party; and who shall say, if they suc-
ceed they shall be dispossessed of the right of
the ballot? The Republicans are exerting all
their powers for the same object; and shall
they also lose their right to vote, if by chance
they should become united? Every society
seeks the unity of its members as a necessity
to its success; and shall all such be debarred
the rights of freedom, or is it because we are
women, the weaker sex, that man, proud
man, exalted to a little brief authority, seeks to
deprive us, the "poor women" of Utah, of our
rights? Because what, forsooth? Because we
are one with our husbands in the maintenance
of the principles of freedom, do they think
that we, as the honorable wives of honorable
men, having had the franchise for so long a
time, (with a secret ballot) would not, ere this,
have thrown off the yoke if we were in bond-
age? If (as Senator Edmunds says) we were
living under the influence of a Mormon hier-
archy, such as he represents, we could at any
moment have thrown it off and voted against
our brethren. How did our commissioners
find out the minds of the "women of Utah?"
How could they judge of the sentiments of the
people, when they never traveled through the
Territory to find out the true feelings of the
people? They, like others, have been satisfied
to draw their own conclusions from a few of 
our enemies, or those who have been excluded
from good society, hence they are not capable of
judging.
	Senator Edmunds is as much in the dark as
are our commissioners, with regard to the
feelings, impulses, and sentiments of the women
of Utah, and I can say that I have enjoyed
the right of franchise for twelve years, and
have never failed to exercise that privilege,
(until it was taken from me) and I was not at
any time compelled to vote, only as I felt, in
my own mind, was right. Living under the
law of the Lord, it was to my interest to work
for the good of the people that were living
under the divine inspiration of God, our hea-
venly Father. The Almighty has condescend-
ed to reveal His Gospel to men on the earth in
this the last dispensation, and has instructed
His servants in this our day in all the ordi-
nances that He did the Ancient Saints; and
among other principles, He has revealed the
holy order of marriage, or a plurality of
wives, and I am proud to say that the Lord
has blessed me with the privilege of living in
the same. I honor all who practically honor
this principle, as it is only those who love this
law that will be found among the noble and 
exalted, and will reign forever a kingdom of
kings unto the Most High, as we find by read-
ing the Scriptures, that holy men of God whom
our Father loved, were men who lived in the
practice of plural marriage or were descendants
from those who lived in it. We find that
Abraham, Jacob, Solomon and David were
all favored of God, and were not reproved for
their many wives, but the Lord sanctioned the
practice, and blessed those who lived in that
holy order. By reading in Genesis xvi and
xvii Chapters we find how the angels of the
Lord appeared unto Hagar and blessed her,
and promised that she should become the
mother of a "great nation," also after that,
Abraham married Keturah, Genesis xxv
Chapter and 11 verse, yet he was the "father

[Column 3]

of the faithful and the friend of God." Also
the Lord blessed Jacob with his own voice,
and he had more wives than one. And Leah,
Rachel, Bilhah and Zilpha became the
mothers of the Twelve tribes of Israel.
	And was not David a man after God's own
heart? Jehoida, the Lord's High Priest, took
two wives for Joab, the king of Judah, who
redeemed Israel from idolatry. (Chronicles
xxiv, 3.) Solomon had many wives, and was
blessed of the Lord, until he ran after idola-
trous women; also Hosea was commanded to
take a second wife, and in fact, if we study
the Bible, we will find abundant evidence, to
convince a candid mind, that God not only
sanctioned the institution of the Patriarchal
order, but that he commanded his servants in
many instances to obey that law; therefore I
say as before, that it is an honor to belong to
such an institution, and how much higher is
that pure principle than such associations as
those existing in the world, where a man only
acknowledges one wife, but where many,
women are prostituted by the lusts of men who
look with horror over the snow-capped moun-
tains to the happy women of Utah and pity
their forlorn condition, while all around them,
on every street, in every corner you find hordes
of women who were once blooming in inno-
cence, the fairest of their sex, sunken to the
lowest grade of degradation. While the hus-
band (whom the confiding young wife has
trusted her life, her happiness and her all, in
his keeping) is secretly decoying to his treach-
erous heart some unsuspecting one, whom he
can, after having gratified his unhallowed pas-
sion, send forth into a cold-hearted world, un-
protected by the name of wife, or by the friend
who had betrayed her, a prey to the sting of
remorse and shame, to die, perchance, un-
cared for. When men think that these things
will escape the eye of an Almighty God they
are mistaken. I tell you the judgments of the
Almighty will fall on the nations of the earth
and sweep such wickedness from the face there-
of; we say to all who pity the women of Utah,
look at home, and cleanse the nation from the
stench of corruption that is sapping away the
foundations of virtue and happiness from the
land; and close up the places and dens of in-
famy in your own states, and then you can
look to your neighbors. The Lord, ere long,
will come out of his hiding and destroy those
that have rejected the Prophets and persecuted
his Saints, and those that have shed the blood
of the innocent and sought to deprive them of
their rights, they will be as stubble in the
time of God's judgment. We put our trust in
the Lord, knowing that we will be judged ac-
cording to our works (whether they be good or
bad) with a righteous judgment, and inasmuch
as we live faithful to our works (whether they be good or
bad) with a righteous judgment, and inasmuch
as we live faithful to our integrity and our
God, we fear not to trust ourselves in His
hands.
	 The wicked and ungodly we leave in the
hands of our Heavenly Father, knowing that
He will deal with them according to their de-
merits.	SARAH A. FULLMER.
	Orangeville, Emery Co., April 9th, 1883.


It is much safer to obey than to govern.
Who is so wise that he can fully know all
things? Be not, therefore, to confident in
thine own opinion, but be willing to bear the
judgment of others.—Thomas d Kempis.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

author: Unknown
title: ONE WOMAN'S OPINION.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol11 | 1882 | February | Issue: 17 | Title: ONE WOMAN'S OPINION. | Author: Unknown]

ONE WOMAN'S OPINION.

	Another attempt is being made in the Con-
gress of the United States to disfranchise the
women of Utah. The Woman's Convention
recently held in Washington, passed resolutions
denouncing these attempts and have been be-
fore the House proclaiming against it as an evil
display of power. While these and thousands
of other noble women throughout the land, and
hundreds of liberal minded men are laboring in
the interest of securing the franchise to all
women, here are a few narrow-minded politi-
cians trying to deprive a handful of women,
away in the far west, of that privilege which
they had exercised already twelve years, and no
one has ever been able to say, the least evil re-


[Column 1]
﻿sult had followed. These liberal minded per-
sons who are so anxious to better the condition
of the women of Utah, and who made such
boasts of what they would do for them, during
the late campaign, when electioneering for Dale-
gate to Congress, are now urging the lawmakers
of the nation to annul the act of the Utah Leg-
islature confering upon women the right to vote.
It is plain as possible that it is a little game of
subtraction they are trying to play, and at the
expense of women; they have scarcely the
courage yet to say, take the ballot from men
because they believe in a certain religion, but
women are "weak creatures," and it will be an
easy matter to make Congress believe women
ought not to have the privilege of voting, and
that will take away from the People's Party a
few thousand votes weakening their ranks a
little, and equalizing the two parties somewhat.
	Plural marriage does not enter into the ques-
tion at all, for no woman on whom a suspicion
has fallen of entering into such relations, is
allowed to vote under the Edmunds law as in-
terpreted by the Commissioners. Consequently
that settles that matter decidedly. It is not
because they vote for men to hold office who
have more than one wife, for such men cannot
hold any office, well why is it? Simply because
they want to obtain a balance of power, by
working away at the rule of subtraction. They
would take away every right and privilege that
the men and women enjoy who settled this
desert land without the least conscientious
scruple Mormon women are entitled to some
privileges for the part they took in opening up
this new country. Many of them drove cattle
over the plains, yoking and unyoking the team
themselves, cooked for their children, made
their own fires, etc., while the husband and
father was in the United States service. This
people, driven from their homes in Nauvoo, and
compelled to go out into the wilderness, and
afterwards called upon to furnish five-hundred
able-bodied men to go to Mexico, a most un-
common request, yet cheerfully complied with;
undergoing numberless privations and hard-
ships, in which women bore their share uncom-
plainingly, have had comparative peace and
prosperity for a number of years here in the
mountain fastnesses.
	The toil and labor consequent upon such long
and tedious journeyings, and the many difficul-
ties and trials which are incidental to pioneer
life have been shared by the mothers and
daughters equally with the fathers and sons.
Realizing this fact and having the utmost con-
fidenee in the integrity and capacity of women
for the public good, the Legislature of Utah
gave woman the suffrage. Believing it
would be for the best interests of all, and that
woman's intelligence would thus be brought in-
to exercise for the benefit of all. Again and
again have the liberal party sought to wrest the
ballot from the women of this Territory, but
their repeated attempts have each time signally
failed. No doubt this last will be as futile as the
others. It is strange considering how anxious
the liberal party were, previous to the last elec-
tion, to obtain the votes of "Mormon" women
that they should so soon seek to deprive them
of the ballot. Had they cast their votes for the
"Liberal" candidate he might have proved him-
self more "Liberal." Alas for the frailty of the
liberal party, who promised so much for woman
in her "down-trodden condition," and now seek
to deprive her of that which gives her political
independence and prestige. What a pity it is
to be sure that all the democratic candidates
who have been beaten by republican votes can-
not appeal to Congress to disfranchise republi-
cans! It would be quite as just, as for the lib-
erals to cheat the women of Utah out of their

[Column 2]
political privileges; but possession is nine points
of the law, the old adage saith, and the women
of Utah have the suffrage and it will be a hard
matter to persuade Congress that any particular
benefit to the Territory can be derived by the
annuling of the act which granted woman the
right to vote, when even those Governors, who
have been most opposed to woman suffrage on
general principles could see no evil result arising
from its exercise in this Territory.
	However, if the liberal party push women to
the wall in the political struggle as they seem
determined to do, they will not achieve the ob-
ject of their ambition, and will most assuredly
get the ill will of all women everywhere who
are demanding political recognition. In these
mountain vales there are hosts of young men,
as well as old, who are by every right of birth
and intelligence entitled to citizenship, who will
uphold the Constitution of the United States
and stand by the flag even under difficulties, and
they will always out vote such men as are now
trying to get Congress to take from the women
of Utah that privilege which they hold most
sacred; and have exercised for twelve years con-
tinuously with the most satisfactory results to
all parties concerned, except a few dissatisfied
office seekers, who are striving to build them-
selves up at the expense of the territorial treas-
ury, and not by the will of the whole people,
but the desire to rule by right of might, taking
advantage of the prejudices of the people
against the "Mormons," and resorting to all
sorts of trickery to carry on the campaign. But
this move to disfranchise women is in keep-
ing with the entire proceedings of the "liberals,"
and is only one more outrage perpetrated in the
interest of a few who desire to arrogate to them-
selves the control of this little portion of terri-
tory, and to oppress the rightful owners of the
soil and the pioneers of the desert, who have
done the hard work of searching out settlements
have built the roads and bridges, made peace
with the red man and destroyed the wild
beasts and reptiles, until it was easy for others
to come here and acquire wealth and position.
The fortitude and endurance of the early set-
tlers of these valleys is deserving the recollec-
tion and gratitude of posterity. Those who par-
ticipated in the hardships and endured the pri-
vations incident to settling this country, know
well how to appreciate the blessings of prosper-
ity that have followed their untiring exertions;
and are certainly the best guardians of their
own freedom and the public welfare, and will not
surrender the fort for any political whim or in-
trigue, not while they have God and right on
their side.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 133

author: Unknown
title: WOMEN'S SPHERE.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | May | Issue: 24 | Title: WOMEN'S SPHERE. | Author: Unknown]

WOMEN'S SPHERE.

	As I said Betsey Bobbet is awful opposed
to wimmin's having any right only the 
right to get married. She holds on to that
right as tight as any single woman I ever
see, which makes it hard and wearyin' on
the single men round here. For take the 
men that are the most opposed to wimmin's
havin' a right, and talk the most about its
bein' her duty to cling to man like a vine
to a tree, they don't want Betsey to cling
to them, they won't let her cling to them.
For when they would be goin' on about
how wicked it was for wimmen to vote—
and it was her only spear to marry, says I
to 'em, which had you rather do, let Betsey
Bobbet cling to you or let her vote? and
they would every one of 'em quail before
that question. They would drop their heads
before my keen grey eyes, and move off
the subject.
	But Betsey don't get discourajed. Every
Time I see her, she says in a hopeful wish-
ful tone, "That the deepest men of minds
in the country agree with her in thinking
that it is wimmin's duty to marry and not to
vote. And then she talks a sight about the
retiring modesty and dignity of the fair
sect, and how shameful and revoltin' it
would be to see wimmin throwin' them
away, and boldly and unblushin'ly talkin'
about law and justice. Why to hear Betsey
Bobbet talk about wimmin's throwin' their
modesty away, you would think if ever
they went to the political pole, they would
have to take their dignity and modesty and
throw them against the pole, and go with-
out any, all the rest of their lives.
	Now I don't believe in such stuff as that,
I think a woman can be bold and unwoman-
ly in other things besides going with a 
thick veil over her face, and a brass mount-
ed parasol, once a year, and gently and
quietly dropping a vote for a Christian
president, or a religious or noble minded
pathmaster.—My Opinion's and Betsey
Bobbet's.—English Woman's Review.


It is repo[-]ted that ex-Queen Isabella is
about to make London her residence.

	WHAT letters of the alphabet are most
valuable to a man in business? N. R. G.
(energy).

his preference for a quack doctor—"I
vouldn't call him iv mine cat vas dead."

author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT
title: ADDRESS TO W. S. A. AT OGDEN.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol18 | 1889 | July | Issue: 4 | Title: ADDRESS TO W. S. A. AT OGDEN. | Author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT]

ADDRESS TO W. S. A. AT OGDEN.

Mrs. President Ladies and Gentlemen:
	I congratulate the women of Ogden on
having organized an Association for political
study and advancement in the science of
government.  I realize fairly perhaps how
much prejudice and opposition there will be
to encounter, and difficulties to surmount and
overcome in the pursuit of a purpose which to
many seems unwomanly and out of place.
But women are in reality amenable to the laws
of the country in which they dwell, and if
property owners, as many are, they are taxed
to help maintain the laws, while perhaps
totally ignorant on all these subjects, having
no representation and getting no practical ex-

[Column 2]
perience, except to pay taxes; such women are
scarcely prepared for a sudden change of which
perchance they had never thought or dreamed,
and of which they had no fore-knowledge.
But the women of Utah and many of you
whom I see here to-day have had an experience
in the practical use of the ballot.  But in our
day many changes are being brought about,
and one of these is woman's place in the great
world of thought and action, in the home, in
the state, and in the nation for suffrage has
become a national question.  We sometimes
speak of it as woman's era—so much has been
and is being done to elevate and help woman-
kind.  The world is growing wiser every day
in regard to woman's position.  In fact there
has been a vast improvement in the last fifty
years,  in woman's status educationally, in-
dustrially and politically.
	The laws of several of the states in this great
Republic of which we Americans boast so 
much, have been essentially modified in regard
to women and children since the women them-
selves have been agitating the question of politi-
cal equality.  I do not say, nor do I expect, 
that when women vote, all the evils that 
afflict society will be remedied or removed, nor
that all oppression will cease; but I do believe
that women have a right to use their influence
towards making the laws that are to govern
them and their children, and should have the
privilege of having a voice as to who shall
execute the laws of the land, and how the taxes
shall be applied and in no other possible way
can woman exert as direct an influence for
her own and the public good as with the ballot
in her own hands; and I do most firmly believe
that woman's moral and spiritual nature will
impress itself with a recognized force upon all
questions in which she is permitted to par-
ticipate, thus helping to refine and purify the
political atmosphere which envelops the nation.
	I am aware that it is said by men that
women vote now, indirectly through their
husbands, brothers and sons, but who would
not rather wield the power herself even were
such the case, and especially when, as a literary 
lady of wealth and influence informed me in
Washington, the only vote she had at the polls
was through her colored coachman;  her
husband had been killed in the war of the 
Rebellion, and she, a lady of culture and re-
finement as well as wealth—was denied rep-
resentation though she paid heavy taxes—and
she was not even sure her servant voted on her
side of the question.  Such cases and similar
ones are very common.  Woman likes best to
act her own part and if there is a point to be
gained in political matters,  her vote is one
more and might turn the scale.
	John Quincy Adams said, in defending the
political petition of the women of Plymouth, 
that "women are not only justified, but exhibit
the most exalted virtue, when they do depart
from the domestic circle, and enter upon the
concerns of their country, of humanity and 
of their God."
	In the days when women voted in Utah, it
was very commonly remarked by us, that our
individual vote was equal to that of any man's,
no matter what his ability, or  his political
position.  And to all those who understood
and appreciated the franchise, it gave a feel-
ing of independence, that was in it-self an
elevating power.  Men will assuredly respect
women's opinions more, when they know they
can give them expression at the polls.
	American men are everywhere reputed to
be more tender and indulgent to women than
those of any other land; socially, they elevate
them to a very high plane, even giving them
the title of "queens of society," though these
same men will  not admit their wives to their
club rooms, and are shocked to hear of women
voting;  but will this social distinction out-
weigh  the ballot, and give them influence,

[Column 3]
such as equal suffrage will give?  Verily no?
Who does not know better, that has watched
the tide of human affairs carefully?  What
woman of common sense, would not rather be
the equal of her husband than to be called
pet names?  Besides, every honorable man
respects a woman more, who is his equal in
intelligence,  than one who is his inferior.
The old rule of woman's weakness and man's
power, will be done away with many of its
seducing influences, and attendant  miseries,
when women have equal rights with men.
	There are many men everywhere yet to be
converted, to this new, or comparatively new
doctrine, of woman's equality or emancipation,
and would like her to  believe still, that she is
sweeter,  and dearer, and more lovable, when 
she keeps in the old beaten track of domestic
life, and never aspires to anything beyond the
boundary of her own domicile.  But this will
scarcely answer for the mothers, whose children
are growing up, and about to take their part in
the world's great drama.  Then, there is some-
thing to be done; and more things to be thought
of than  looking sweet and demure; and what
man would not wish, at  such a time, that his
wife were really prepared to take up a goodly
share of the burden and obligations.  And
how many women are there in the world who
must even take them all; widows, and those
worse than widowed, whose husbands have,
notwithstanding all the sweetness given, all the
deference shown them,  given themselves up to
the demon, alcohol? Then,  in such a home, is
it not absolutely necessary that the wife be
strong-minded, as well as physically strong,
to carry the load of life along?  And who is
to represent her and her interests at the polls?
Her vote is needed then against the dramshop.
Such instances are not rare; they may be found
by the thousands in America, as well as in for-
eign lands,  They are too sad to dwell upon,
but they are solemn facts nevertheless.
		*	*	*	*	*
	And when women sit at ease in luxurious
homes and say they have all the rights they
want, and are disgusted with those who are not
content,  and who are interceding  for the
ballot, they simply shut their eyes and ears, to
the cries of the wronged and oppressed; they
forget, or ignore, the Savior's words, "love thy
neighbor," but to many, even of these de-
luded ones, an awakening comes, sometimes
suddenly,  and too late,  they find perchance,
they too,  are left, by death or misfortune,  to
battle with the  world.  How much better to 
have been prepared.
	*	*	*	*	*	*	*
	If women are fortunate enough to marry a
man who supports them,  and gives them all
the rights they want, for the services they
render in  love, it  is well.  But if he fails to 
do this, would it not be  better were they pre-
pared to be self-sustaining?  Especially tf they
have equal pay for equal work, which, it seems,
they never will have, until they have the ballot
in their own hands.  That is one of the im-
portant matters to be adjusted, and working
women themselves do not seem to be aware of
this fact.  Many of them are afraid of the
question of Woman's Rights, and will say, "I
have  a perfect dread of women who are
always wanting men's places," but they  are
quite willing  to accept men's wages, neverthe-
less.
	However were men and women all agreed
in every relation in life, and the world march-
ing on with flying colors, as it were, to victory,
even then, woman's word should be as potent
as man's, and her voice in all matters of  the
home, the state, and society of equal impor-
tance. And there need be no fears of collision
because men and women both go to the polls,
any more than because they go to the theatre
or church together; for so long as men retain


[Column 1]
their magnificent physical organizations and
proportions, and as long as women possess
their sensitive, sympathetic, maternal tem-
peraments, just so long as these distinctions of
sex exist, that great primal force which
binds men and women, will continue to at-
tract them to each other. It has been said,
and truly, "If there be one thing of which
nature is careful, she is careful of her types,"
and while "that likeness in unlikeness sub-
sists which is at the base of all physical at-
traction, there is little fear of sexual rela-
tions being either reversed or annulled,"
but let woman as well as man, know how to
protect herself, and stand on an equal platform.
	Lucy Stone, one of the foremost women of
the age, once said, "Woman's nature is stamped
and sealed by the Almighty, and there is no
danger of her unsexing herself, while His eye
watches her. 
	Margaret Fuller, one of the earliest advocates
of the higher education of women in America,
said, "One hour of love will teach a woman
more of her true relations, than all your
philosophizing. These, said a great writer of
our own day, are the testimonies of women
who had studied Greek, and were only the
more womanly for their study,—illustrating
the idea that the greater includes the less.
	The Duchess de Fontanges is described as
being, "as beautiful as an angel and as silly
as a goose." Men worshipped her beauty, but
despised her in their hearts, and she certainly
could not have arisen to any greatness upon
her own merits.
	What we want most at the present time, is to
understand political questions and privileges,
and make ourselves better acquainted by
study and discussion, with the laws of the
land, and the government which taxes us, and
protects us in our rights, or professes to do so.
	It is said, if women are strong-minded, that
is if they should vote, because that is the sum
and substance of the matter, that they will
neglect the home, and will not cook the dinner
and that is said to be a necessity for man; at
least, the poet tells us so, and by-the-bye that
poet was a man; that man can do without
everything except cooks and dining—he
give it beautifully in these words.

	We may live without poetry—music and art,
	We may live without conscience, and live without heart,
	We may live without friends’ or may live without books
	But civilized man, cannot live without cooks.

	He may live without books, what is knowledge but
		grieving?
	He may live without hope, what is hope but deceiving?
	He may live without love, what is passion but pining?
	But where is the man—that can live without dining!"

	For the seventeen years that the women of
Utah voted, I never once heard it stated or
argued that the bread was poorer, or the home
neglected, or the husbands shirts without
buttons, or less economy in the household, as a
consequence. In fact, everything went on
much the same, only a little better. Primary
meetings and Conventions were more interest-
ing, and there was a fairer chance of getting
good men in office, and not nearly so much
worry over elections as there is now-a-days;
therefore, it must have been a great relief to
men, instead of a disturbing element.
	In arguing the question of woman's equality
the inference is usually drawn from the family,
and mainly the husband and wife, utterly ig-
noring the fact, that men and women were
human beings before they were husband and
wife, and that they had formed ideas, views,
and opinions, and arrived at an age when they
exerted an individual influence, known and
felt in the great world of mankind, if only
among their immediate circle; but in speaking
of equal suffrage, this fact is very seldom

[Column 2]
taken into consideration. It is invariably the
plea that it will create domestic broils, pro-
mote dissension, provoke quarrels, etc. We
all know very well that there are thousands of
women in the United States, who never have,
and very likely, never will marry, for various
reasons unknown to us, and which we have
not time to discuss; who is going to represent
the interests of all these single women? cer-
tainly not the single men, they have too many
interests of their own. In my own native
state, Mass., there are thousands of them.
They have lives to live, work to do, interests
at stake and they need the ballot as a lever
to help them in their struggles for bread-
winning or other pursuits, or for protection.
	What does Emily Pfeiffer say to the members
of Parliament in Great Britain. "Necessity
has presented itself to the women of our gen-
eration with talons and beak more formidable
than those of the eagle, who drives the young
one from the nest. They have not sought the 
shelterless strife with opposing prejudices and
interests, but have been forced into it by the
incontrovertible, law, which pushes the tribes
of men over barren continents, and out upon
stormy seas. It is hunger the mighty maker,
which is urging our women upon new paths,
and driving them upon a way which they would
not, to the fulfillment of a destiny they know
not. With this force behind them it is im-
possible, that they should turn back, impossi-
ble that those before should resist their impul-
sion. They have been crowded by their own
numbers out of the penfold in which their ac-
tivity was enclosed, and forced to seek the
equivalent of their labor in an ever widening
sphere. In making the experiment of their
fitness for untried work, they have had to face
odium and abundant ridicule from those they
hold dear. Their efforts to train themselves
for higher and more remunerative labor has
encountered the opposition of a jealously
guarded monopoly, and the claim for citizen-
ship now formulated though enforced inde-
pendence has rendered it a right—may be
met,—seeing that it lacks the element of
material force which, still enters largely into
human affairs, on many sides with indifference
and on some with scorn. But the stream of
tendency which sets in the way of woman's ad-
vance is irresistible, and the vital rational prin-
ciples incorporated in her claim could in the
end win alone in the struggle with material
resistance."
	Other remarks were made beside those em-
bodied in these notes, but the main points are 
included herein.
						July, 1st, 1889.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[column 1]

author: a lady in the Southern Province, who
title: WOMAN'S RIGHTS IN JAPAN.—A wo-
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol7 | 1878 | May | Issue: 23 | Title: WOMAN'S RIGHTS IN JAPAN.—A wo- | Author: a lady in the Southern Province, who]

	WOMAN'S RIGHTS IN JAPAN.—A wo-
man's rights d[-]monstration has been made
by a lady in the Southern Province, who 
protests against paying taxes unless she is
allowed the privilege of voting. Her let-
ter which is pertinent and logical even in 
Japanese estimation, has been referred by
local authorities to the central government
and awaits decision at the capital.—EX.
	ON the outside of the April number of 
the "Womah's Suffrage Journal" published
in Manchester, Miss Lydia E. Becker,
editor, is an earnest entreaty to the women
of England.
	VOTE! vote! vote!—Women Ratepayers
are reminded that Elections of poor-Law
Guardians, Members of Local Boards,
Church wardens, Overseers, Auditors and 
other Officials take place during April.
Vestry meetings for the Elections of 
Church wardens usually take place on the 
Tuesday in Easter week. Women Rate-
payers have the right to attend and vote in 
all local elections, and they are earnestly
exhorted to use the suffrage, which the 
law confers upon them. Elections for 
Guardians and Members of Local Boards
are conducted by means of voting papers,
which are left at the houses of the Rate-
payers, and are called for before the election.
Women ratepayers should see that their
papers are duly filled up and signed ready
to be given up when called for, and in case
of failure to collect the papers they should 
themselves forward them to the proper
officer, who is bound to notify by public ad-
vertisement the time and place when he
will receive papers which have not been 
called for.
	It is of the utmost consequence that wo-
men should exercise their votes on every
lawful occasson.
	Dorcas, drawing-room, and other meetings
have been held in various parts of England
to awaken an interest and prepare Parlia-
mentary petitions for the member Hon. Mr.
Courtney in support of his resolution. The
question was submitted to the House in
March under new conditions, and in a 
slightly altered form. This question was a 
resolution offering the principle once laid
down by Lord Beaconsfield that it is anoma-
lous that the Parliamentary franchise at-
tached to a household or property qualifica-
tion when possessed by a woman should
not be exercised, though in all local elec-
tions, when similarly qualified she exerci-
ses this right; and that this anomaly is in[-]
jurious to the best interests of the country.

author: Unknown
title: CONTESTED.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol9 | 1880 | October | Issue: 9 | Title: CONTESTED. | Author: Unknown]

CONTESTED.

	We publish in this issue some particulars con
cerning the attempt recently made to take the
suffrage from the women of this Territory. All
at once these "wise men of Gotham" have de-
clared the law conferring the franchise upon
woman null and void, so positively, that they per-
emptorily order the names of certain women and
all other women to be erased from the registra-
tion list. The Supreme Court issue a writ of
mandamus to enforce this demand. Is it not a
grave reflection upon these legal gentlemen so
thoroughly acquainted with matters pertaining
to legislation, and the construction of law, to
have passed over in silence a matter which has
been so loudly talked of everywhere its merits
and demerits so extensively discussed, and have
allowed to remain a law for ten years without a
demur,and now to emphatically declare as Judge
Sutherland did in his argument on Wednesday
evening last, "We deny the validity of the act
giving women the right to vote." According to
this positive statement women here have never
cast a lawful ballot? Of what inconsistency will
they accuse women next? In the name of reason
and justice we would ask as Mrs. Blake did at
a convention at which we were present "why
don't you take women to the end of the world
and jump them off?"
	One of the principal features of the objections
urged by the plaintiff's lawyers was that the law
of 1870, now in question, did not require women
to pay taxes, and consequently stamped the act
as special legislation; and as the act of 1859 pro-
hibited any male from voting unless he was a
tax payer, that was general legislation, and the
two conflicted, but were not contradictory. This
question about tax paying seems to be a disease
with men, and yet they know that every woman
who holds any property whatever in her own
right is required by law to pay taxes, and that
thousands of women, both wives and widows,
pay taxes in this Territory. Poll tax is the only
tax from which they are exempt, that we are
aware of, and all this furore about a privileged
class of voters, is all bosh and nonsense.
	There were several interesting points in the
arguments, which are of great consequence to
all who have the vital question of woman suf-
frage at heart. We shall publish all the argu-
ments made, in full, in our next issue, October
15, as the paper was nearly ready for the press
when the question which involves woman's right
to vote was raised.
	Meanwhile, we would say, this is not the time
for men or women to sleep upon their oars, but
watch the enemy's rank and file well, and never
lose an opportunity to defend the right. Let
those who wish to maintain civil and religious
liberty, stand firm in the hour of danger, and

[Column 2]
trust in the living God, Who knows the secrets
of all hearts.
	The defense affirm that the Supreme Court
has not the power to issue mandamus. If this
demurrer could be strenuously maintained, then
those who are so anxious to do away with women
voters would have to raise some other question,
or attack from another quarter. But it is to be
hoped the women will give the enemy "no quar-
ter," and they will be obliged to succumb to a
law which has been practised and observed for
ten years, and which is calculated, in the very
nature of things, to foster good institutions and
practises, promote industries among the people,
elevate society, and eventually lift woman to a
much higher plane than that enjoyed by her in
the past.
	We attended the sitting of the Court on Thurs-
day afternoon, according to adjournment from
Wednesday evening, when the arguments were
