made upon the merits of the question, but Chief
Justice Hunter explained that the Court was
not prepared to render a decision in the case of
mandamus, and therefore the court adjourned,
without transacting any business, until four
o'clock p. m. to-day.
	We have no idea what the legal opinion in
this matter will be, but we know that woman's
cause is in the hands of a higher tribunal than
that of a Supreme Court, and that it will contin-
ally triumph over every obstacle that prejudice,
tradition, envy or malice may place in the way
of woman's advancement and political equality.
"Man proposes, God disposes."


[Column 1]

author: Unknown
title: SWEET IS LIBERTY.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol11 | 1882 | March | Issue: 19 | Title: SWEET IS LIBERTY. | Author: Unknown]

SWEET IS LIBERTY.
"Sweet to us is Liberty, and Bondage odious."

	How appropriate are these words to the peo-
ple of Utah.  How significant at the present
time.  Every one who has in his veins the
blood of freedom must feel keenly the thraldom
and oppression of the enforcement of the Ed-
munds law as interpreted by the Commision-
ers.  The very men and women whose ances-
tors helped to found this great government
and establish republican institutions, to be de-
prived of the franchise without judge or jury,
or any opportunity of defence whatever; such a
policy pursued by those who profess to be ad-
ministrators of law and justice, is sufficient to
make men feel the utmost indignation in their
hearts and to protest strongly against such in-
justice, and exclaim "free we were born," and
there is no power to subdue the free-born soul,
though they may disfranchise and thus punish
men and women for something unknown and
unsubstantiated.  "Kings themselves hold no
dominion over the free will.”
 The people of Utah have rights that ought
to be respected by the general government,
but how has it been?  What aid has been
given to them in settling the great American
Desert?  This is what has been done, to cry out
against them, to beseech Congress to stop their
proselyting, to hinder them from gathering peo-
ple from other nations, seeking to make it an
international question; to take from them every
local position of trust or emolument, so that
they shall have no voice in the disposal of their
own taxes, or any revenue from their own in-
dustries; to put the men who settled this
country and opened up its agriculture and
manufactures under the feet of these who
would despoil them of their worldly possessions
break down their institutions, and deprive them
of every right that a free people are entitled to
enjoy.
	The clamor has been so great and so general,
that without inquiring into the facts, newspa-
pers will publish and republish the most auda-
cious falsehoods against the "Mormons," and if
any are bold enough in the face of public opinion
to defend their cause and tell the truth, they are
immediately assailed and upbraided as having
joined the "Mormon" Church or been bought
over, etc.  Every scheme and cunning plan has
been resorted to, it would seem, to deprive this
people of liberty of conscience, and yet they are
not conquered, much as their enemies have
have conspired against them; though many of
them were not permitted to vote at the last
election.  And because it happened there were
a few women who could take the registration
oath (bad as it was made) and cast a vote for
the man of the people's choice, these would-be-
friends and protectors of women, great in
their own esteem, and loud in their demonstra-
tions of freedom to womankind, set to work to
petition Congress to annul the woman's suf-
frage act passed by the Legislature of Utah.
What nobility of purpose!  How magnanimous! 
How anxious to protect women they must have
been! They excuse themselves by saying,
"these women vote just as their husbands do!"
Well, what of that, if they vote as they wish to?
It sometimes happens that whole families vote
for one man in Massachusetts and in other
States.  Is that a crime?  Not there! In con-
tradiction of the assertions made that wemen
are compelled to vote as the husband does,
we can positively assert that no man ever
asked us a question of this kind, nor have we
ever heard any lady of our acquaintance say
her husband or any other man ever sought to
influence her vote.  Women have their own
free agency in Utah and are quite as indepen-
dent in their opinions as any women on the
face of the earth.  The officials sent here
by the United States government know this
is true.  Moreover, they know that woman
suffrage has worked no evil whatever, and not
one of them can truthfully make an assertion
to that effect.  Yes, women are influenced
to vote by their own ideas of right and justice.
They want men to fill public offices that they
know to be honorable and upright, and not those
who would take from them every vestige of
liberty.  A woman who goes to the polls and
deposits a ballot, feels her political indepen-
dence and that she is virtually part and parcel
of the great body politic, not through her father
or husband, but in her own vested right.  It is
better to represent yourself than to be repre-
sented by another, is most true in this particu-
lar, and applies with quite as much force to the
ballot as upon any other question.  If anything
will give woman a desire to think for herself
and form opinions of her own, and make her
feel the need of informing herself on subjects
connected with the laws and government of her
country and interested in the general welfare,
it will be the practical exercise of the ballot.
	The mother, it is very generally conceded;
forms most essentially the character of her sons
as well as her daughters, and according to her
superiority of endowments, she imparts strength
and force to their capabilities; the im-
portant responsibilities of the ballot will most
certainly add to her attainments and give tone
and balance to her femininity of character, and
thus will she be able to impart to her offspring
thoss requisites and that higher home education
that will help them to reach a higher standard,
and who knows but through the advancement
of women, and their political emancipation,
the laws may be better adjusted and a broader
plane may be reached for all humanity.  At
any rate the experiment is worth trying.
	So far Senator Edmunds has not succeeded in
debarring the women of Utah of their franchise,
although he and others sought to deprive them
of this inestimable privilege.  One would think
Judge Black's logical and magnificent argument
for Utah ought to have put an end to this ever
recurring quibble, whether "Mormons" should
be tolerated or not, on account of their religious
belief; but although the Judge is a most learn-
ed and able lawyer, yet many people will not
listen even to the soundest argument, because
their prejudice is so great.  They do not com-
prehend liberty for all people of whatever creed
or nation, vouchsafed by the Constitution, nor
that Congress shall make no law abridging re-
ligious liberty.  Judge Black's argument com-
mends itself and will certainly be read with
great interest by the honest and intelligent
who are not blinded by bigotry and a desire to
crush the "Mormons."  Those who voted for
the passage of the Edmund's bill perhaps were
not aware that they were striking a deadly
blow to religious liberty in the United
States, To pursue the policy towards this peo-
ple that the anti-Mormons recommend would
be to change the laws and ordinances of the
local government, and overrule and tread under
foot law and order as maintained heretofore,
once the pride and boast of this beautiful and
quiet city, and arrogate to themselves every
vestige of the power of office and make the peo-
ple of Utah a set of serfs and vassals.
 But there is still some power vested in the
people of Utah, they are not all disfranchised
and they appreciate the meaning of the word
liberty, and they are undoubtedly capable of
maintaining the rights of freemen.  They know
they have the right on their side, and God and
the right are a great majority.  Honor to Judge
Black and all such noble minded men, who
dare stand up boldly and plead the cause of the
weak and down-trodden.

author: Unknown
title: THE ELECTIVE FRANCHISE IN
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | July | Issue: 4 | Title: THE ELECTIVE FRANCHISE IN | Author: Unknown]

THE ELECTIVE FRANCHISE IN
			UTAH.
SUGGESTED BY MRS. LIVERMORE’S RE-
	MARKS AT THE NEW YORK CONVEN-
	TION.

 I don’t doubt Mrs. Livermore is an ex-
cellent woman, but she does not understand
the women of Utah. She says that in Utah
women vote.  To be sure we do; every sen-
sible and just man knows that taxation
without representation is tyranny and op-
pression. Don't women have to pay taxes
if they have any property? Ask the tax
collector, you know what his answer would
be. Mrs. Livermore says we are ticketed.
This is indeed news. She says both men and
women receive a number. Do we?
Mrs. Livermore seems to know more about
those tickets than we do. I have never
seen one of them, although I have voted
ever since the women were given the right
of Franchise in this Territory. Where
shall I look fot those tickets, on the backs,
on the hats or where? And echo answers
where! When I have voted it has been be
-cause it was my honest conviction it was
for the best man. Although we are only
women we will not be driven like a flock of
sheep at the will of the driver. When
next I vote I shall be looking for my num-
ber. Every man in the states votes ac-
cording to his honest convictions, don't he?
Especially the working men and the de-
pendents on another man's smiles or
frowns? They are not ticketed, Oh, no!


They are all free !! Yes free to go without
their daily bread if they don't vote as their
employers wish. In Utah gentlemen (?)
don't go about buying up the votes of the
lowest rabble, and the half-idiotic inebre-
ate, the pay "free drinks" Is this the
style in Utah? No it is not. Men or wo-
men vote just as they please. Any decent
person passing the places where the polls
are held in the states are shocked and dis-
gusted with the sights and sounds,and will
hurry away as fast as possible.
	Mrs. Livermore says, "the women don't
want to change." What can she mean? Is
it our husbands? If so, we beg to inform
her we are not "Free Lovers," but believe
most emphatically in chastity. She asserts
we women "do just as the men want us to."
Does she mean to imply we are not capable
of doing our own thinking? She also says
we obey our religious convictions" as
though she pitied us. Is it not right that
we should? Is it any sign of imbecility?
The Roman Catholic nun follows her re-
ligious convictions when she takes the vail,
yet she is not considered an idiot. I have
lived in Utah eighteen years and never
known any woman to die of a broken
heart. I will tell you what I have seen;
noble, brave, strong-hearted women in po-
lygamy living the lives of saints in deed,
and dying the death of the righteous, leav-
ing the world better for having lived in it.
	Well although so much has been written
and said about the Women of Utah, we are
still a "sealed book" to the world.
	MARY.
Grantsville, July 2, 1876.


[Column 1]

author: Unknown
title: WOMAN SUFFRAGE IN BOMBAY.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol10 | 1881 | April | Issue: 22 | Title: WOMAN SUFFRAGE IN BOMBAY. | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN SUFFRAGE IN BOMBAY.

	A correspondent of the Boston Woman's
Journal writes:—
	Now that a special effort is being made to
secure municipal suffrage for women in Massa-
chusets, it may interest you to know that wo-
men in Bombay, British India, already have a
right to vote for municipal officers.
	Four years ago, my landlord, a well known
Parsee gentleman, was a candidate for member
of the municipal corporation, from the Mazagon
ward. He appealed to my friends to use their
influence to persuade me to vote for him, and
finally sent his agent to me with the necessary
papers and the blanks filled out with my name.
The agent assured me that I only needed to re-
gister my name at the Mazagon police station,
in order to become a legal voter.
	On the day of polling, one Parsee lady pre-
sented herself at the police station to vote for
this gentleman; and in the Girgaum ward one
Hindoo lady voted for the Hindoo candidate of
that ward.
	This was noticed in the daily papers, but
apparently did not attract much attention. At
that time any person, male or female, paying
taxes on property, whether as owner, trustee, or
agent, was entitled to vote for municipal of-
ficers. Since then the law has been changed,
making any person who pays a municipal tax
a legal voter.
	At the last election, one year ago, several
Parsee and Hindoo ladies, one Mussulman, and
one European lady, voted in their respective
wards. Considerable attention was attracted
to this by the daily papers, and a certain
amount of interest manifested itself, so it is
likely that when the next election comes
around, two years hence, a large number of
ladies will present themselves as voters.—Ex.


SUSAN B. Anthony appeared March 28, be-
fore the woman suffrage committee of the U. S.
Senate, and argued in favor of granting the
ballot to women.

	THERE is no reason, in our judgment, why
women should not be allowed all the rights and
privileges of citizenship. but at present the law
does not recognize them as such—Christian
Union.
	"THE Governor of Massachusetts has named
Mrs. Robert C. Winthrop a member of the
Advisory Board on State institutions for wo-
men; Mrs. Henry F. Durant is a Commissioner
on Prisons; Mrs. Anna B. Richardson a Trustee
of the State Industrial School."

	"The first magizine in this country conducted
solely by women was the Lowell Offering. It
originated in an "Improvement Circle" in one of
the churches in Lowell, Mass. Operators in the
Lowell mills were its first editors, from 1842 to
1849. Its first motto was;

The worm on the earth
May look up to the star.

The articles were all written by factory girls,
and printed as written. Miss Lucy Larcom
was one of its frequent contributors.
	"The whole Lowell factory community in
1840 was filled with the idea of self culture and
a better education." But when foreign-born
operatives came in, and labor was cheapened,
the whole tone of factory society was changed,
and the Offering had to be abandoned. But it
was a marked power for good while it lasted."


[Column 1]

author: Unknown
title: AN APPLICATION FOR MANDAMUS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol9 | 1880 | October | Issue: 10 | Title: AN APPLICATION FOR MANDAMUS. | Author: Unknown]

AN APPLICATION FOR MANDAMUS.

	Boreman, Justice, delivered the following
opinion:
	As an original question, I have uniformly
been of the opinion thatthe Supreme Court had
no jurisdiction in such cases as this, except in
aid of its appellate powers. I have considered
that the Supreme Court had no distinctively
original jurisdiction, except in cases of habeas
corpus. The issuing of the mandamus as prayed
is an exercise of original jurisdiction. In the late
case of Emeline Young and others vs. George
Q. Cannon et al., this Court, after exhaustive
argument, declared that it had jurisdiction to
issue the writ of certiorari, which is an origi-
nal writ of the same class as that ofmandam-
us, and coming to us from the same source, the
King's Bench. I assumed that that decision was
to settle the practice, and that in this class of
cases, to which both certiorari and mandamus
belong, the Supreme Court would take jurisdic-
tion. I think it our duty now to stand by that
decision and not again unsettle the practice, as
I deem would be done if the writ were now de-
nied.
	Upon the merits of the case I cannot agree
with a majority of the Court. I deem this to
be a proper case in which to issue the writ. The
Legislature had no authority to allow anybody
to vote who were not citizens or who had not
declared their intentions to become such. It
has never enacted that parties who had declar-
ed their intentions to become citizens might
vote. Therefore the registering officer is not
authorized to allow anybody to vote who are
not citizens. The statute granting suffrage to
women allows them to vote without being citi-
zens if they are "the wife, widow or the daugh-
ter of a native-born or naturalized citizen."
Such a provision is utterly void, in my opinion,
and it is the duty of the registering officer to
obey the law of Congress and not that of the
Territory, when they conflict.
	The act conferring the elective franchise
upon women is unjust, as granting the franchise
to women upon easier terms than to males.
Men are required to be tax-payers by the stat-
ute, but not so with women; the men are all
required to be residents, but not so the women,
if they be the "wife, widow, or daughter;" and
all men who ask to vote must be citizens,or they
will be rejected, but not so with all women. This
matter of citizenship is important, when we
consider that the bulk of the population of this
Territory is of foreign birth, or children born in
this Territory of foreign parents. The statute
granting the elective franchise to women des-
troys the uniformity and impartiality which
should exist in regard to the qualifications of
voters, and the act which will do this is unjust
and ought not to be upheld. I do not think that
it will do to say that the requirement as to male
voters, which is not found amongst the require-
ments of the female voters, will be nugatory.
We have no right to conclude that this is so.

[Column 2]

The Legislature has expressed itself to the con-
trary. It first passed the statute allowing males
to vote, requiring them to be citizens, etc. It
afterwards passed the statute granting the
elective franchise to women, and subsequently it
enacts the registration law, wherein it retains all
the qualifications originally required as to male
voters. It certainly, therefore, had no intention
of repealing any part thereof. The two laws in
regard to suffrage show great unfairness and
lack of uniformity between the requirements
of male voters and those of female voters, but
as the Legislature so intended,what authority
have we to say that the one repeals the other.
This certainly does not exist by implieation as
they are statutes regarding different classes. The
two laws are not inconsistent further than that
one is unconstitutional, unjust and unfair to
the body of voters mentioned in the first,
and being so should not be upheld. If the Legis-
lature had power to make one set of qualifica-
tions for one class of voters and another set
for another class of voters, then the two laws
can stand,but if the Legislature has not such pow-
er, its attempt to do so is nugatory and void.
	For these reasons thus hastily stated I am un-
able to agree with my associates in denying the
writ in this case.


WOMAN'S  EXPONENT.

author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT
title: MISS BECKER ON WOMAN SUFFERAGE
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol2 | 1873 | January | Issue: 15 | Title: MISS BECKER ON WOMAN SUFFERAGE | Author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT]

MISS BECKER ON WOMAN SUFFERAGE

	On Tuesday evening, Oct. 1st, at Oakfield,
England, Miss Becker delivered the follow-
ing interesting address on Woman Suffrage.
Mr. Thomasson occupied the chair.
	Miss Becker commenced with a few com-
plimentary remarks, and then referred to
the political position of women, and said
that if they considered for a moment the va-
rious propositions that were made for the
welfare of the nation, they could not fail to
be struck with the omissions of all distinct
reference to women in many important
branches of legislation. There were certain
matters—such, for instance, as those rela-
ting to food and sanitary science—in which
the reference to women was not material,
because it was universally admitted that
in such matters the welfare of women was
included in that of men. But when they
came to consider the moral, intellectual, and
political welfare of the country, they would
find that all reference to women was omit-
ted, not because women were included, but
because they were excluded. It was admit-
ted that women had the same physical wants
as men, but as far as their intellectual and
social needs were concerned, it was thought
that what was desirable for the one was un-
desirable for the other. It was the object of
the movement they had the honor to repre-
sent to secure for women an equal share
with men in moral, intellectual and social
needs. All women labored under certain
political disabilities. They had no votes in
the election of the Government under which
they lived. It was true that very many
classes of men had no votes, but their dis-
qualification was very different indeed from
the disability under which women suffered.
At one time all who professed the Roman
Catholic faith were excluded from represen-
tation in Parliament, and were subjected to
a series of severe penal laws, but in the
course of time doctrines of civil and religi-
ous liberty prevailed and then Catholics
were enfranchised, because it was thought
that nothing short of that would secure
them such liberty. There was no mention
in the old Acts of Parliament of the franchise
being limited to men, the first enactment
in which it was limited to male persons be-
ing the Act of 1832. In the Act of 1867
the word "man" was used, and she contend-
ed that the word had a higher meaning than
was attributed to it by many, and included
humanity at large. In saying this, she was
not without authority, for in an Act of Par-
liament which had been brought in by the
late Lord Brougham it was expressly de-
clared that words imputing the masculine
gender should be taken to include females
in all the clauses of that Act. Before the
passing of the Act of 1867, it was stated in
the House of Commons that it was intend-
ed to give the franchise to all householders
rated for the relief of the poor, and many
poor women were hardly able to pay the ex-
tra rates imposed under this bill, but when
they applied to the courts in 1868, to claim
the votes given under the Act, they found
that the words imputing the masculine gen-
der applied to them in the clauses imposing
the burdens, but excluded them from the
clauses conveying the privileges. There
were certain classes of men, viz., minors,
idiots, lunatics, and criminals that were not
permitted to vote, and surely it was not
very wonderful that women should desire to
escape from such a category. But when 
they enquired into the question, they found
that the condition of women was worse. A
minor would be entitled to the franchise in
a few years, but a woman was never able to
acquire the right of voting. A learned


[Column 1]
judge had laid it down that "infants cannot
vote, and that women are perpetual infants."
She would ask them to bear in mind the
influence of women in the civilization of the
world, and say if that judge was right in
thus speaking of women, was it not true
that the most important lessons a man could
learn were learned at his mother's knee?
And was it right that the law should teach
him when he grew up, to regard his mother
as a perpetual infant? It had been proposed
in the House of Commons that when a man
was convicted of felony and sentenced to
penal servitude he should be disfranchised,
but Mr. Gladstone replied that it was not
right to punish a man by giving him "life-
long electoral incapacity." But although
this was thought too severe for criminals, it
was inflicted without compunction on a class
of beings who had never broken the law.
In effect, therefore, a man might rob his
mistress, go to prison for the offence, and
then come out of gaol and help to make
laws to govern the mistress he had robbed.
It had been laid down by authority that if
a lunatic had a lucid interval at the time of
an election, and was sufficiently "compo-
mentis" to distinguish between the candi-
dates, he was entitled to vote, but the law
regarded women as never having a suffi-
ciently lucid moment to vote; so that the
law placed them intellectually below luna-
tics, and morally, below felons. As to the
illiterate voter, they all knew the wonder-
ful pains that had been taken to secure his
vote. Yet, strange to say, an educated wo-
man was not allowed to record her vote.
There was an old maxim that taxation and
representation should go together, and that
was all she asked for. In 1868 it was found
that there was so much corruption in the
borough of Bridgewater that a Royal Com-
mission was sent down to report on the sub-
ject, and the committee found so much cor-
ruption that they disfranchised the borough.
Now the people of Bridgewater discovered
that a Royal Commission was a very expen-
sive luxury, and a rate of 3s. in the pound
was levied on the rate-payers. This rate fell
very heavily on a number of poor ladies in
the borough who were rate-payers, and they
sent a memorial to the Home Office, stating
that as they had no votes they should not be
called upon to pay for the faults of the men.
But Mr. Bruce replied that it was not in the
power of the Government to release them
from the imperial and local taxation to which
their property was liable. That perhaps was
a right answer to give, but what they asked
for was that women, who owned or occupied
property, should have the vote the proper-
ty would carry with it if they were men, in
addition to the monetary liabilities. It
might be said that their claim was a just one,
but that it was necessary to show more rea-
sons than the one that it was just. She could
not admit that. When a thing was clearly
put they might rest assured that good would
accrue from giving it, and that evil would
follow its refusal. Miss Becker then referred
to the speech which Mr. John Bright deliv-
ered in 1868, on the first occasion on which
he addressed a public meeting after his re-
covering from a severe illness. Mr. Bright
said: "I do not wish to endanger or remove
any of the ancient landmarks of our Consti-
tution. I do not wish to disturb this ques-
tion of the franchise beyond what has al-
ready been sanctioned by Parliament and
the country. I do not want to introduce any
new principle or theoretical opinion which
it may be found difficult to adopt.	*	*
*	*	*	*	*	What have we at
present in the way of franchises? We have
the parish franchise. For generations, for
ages past, there has been an extensive fran-

[Column 2]
chise in all our parishes. We have poor-
law unions which have worked on the whole
in a manner satisfactory to the country. We
have a franchise in our poor-law unions.
We have a corporation franchise, and that
franchise may be said to have worked to the
satisfaction of the country. I will ask any
man here whether he believes that in all
parishes, all the poor-law unions, and all the
corporations, men have not conducted them-
selves with great propriety, and managed
the affairs of their parishes, the poor-law
unions, and the corporations, women had
not conducted themselves with propriety;
and whether he would object to have the
same franchise conferred upon them for the
election of the members of the House of
Commons? She argued that in the days of
open voting the influence of women in an
election was greater than it was now when
a man voted secretly, according to his own
will and pleasure, and was responsible to no
one. The idea that the franchise was a
trust, had been done away with by the prac-
tice of secret voting, and it was, therefore,
only just that each class of the community,
who used formerly to influence an election,
should have the secret vote. The passing
of the Ballot Act had removed a great ob-
jection to women's suffrage. Mr. Gladstone
said that his objection to it was that it would
involve women in all the turmoil of contest-
ed elections, but the objection had now been
removed, for whatever else the ballot had
done it had introduced order into elections.
Women were very much addicted to com-
mitting matrimony, but when they did so
the law treated them as if they had com-
mitted felony. A criminal could not hold
property, and a woman from the day of her
marriage became incapable of possessing
property. There was not a large town in
England in which some woman was not
shamefully robbed every day. By the Act
of 1870, a woman, when she married, trans-
ferred all her liabilities to her husband, but
the House of Lords made this arrangement;
they gave the husband the property and the
wife the debts. Miss Becker then proceed-
ed to make a few remarks on the present
position of the movement. The Govern-
ment in encouraging women to come for-
ward as School Board candidates, distinctly
recognized the right of women to share in
the government of the country. How then,
she would ask, could women who sat at
School Board meetings and assisted to solve
such knotty points as the payment of de-
nominational fees, &c. be considered as
unfit to choose between two Parliamentary
candidates? It was too great an anomaly to
last. It was proposed to give the franchise
to agricultural laborers; but could it be said
that women had less political experience and
less capability for the franchise than agri-
cultural laborers? Why was the franchise
to be given to laborers? It was thought
they needed it as a protection. The very
same reason, she need hardly add, applied
to women. The extension of the Municipal
and School Board franchise to women had
worked well, and there was no reason what-
ever why the Parliamentary franchise should
be withheld. If men advance women must
advance. Man and woman must go hand in

[Column 3]
hand, and if man will not bring her forward
she will draw man back, for man cannot ad-
vance himself from woman. After sketch-
ing forth the programme of the Women's
Suffrage Society, Miss Becker concluded her
address by eloquently appealing to the audi-
ence to use whatever influence they possess-
ed to remove an anomaly which was inju-
rious to the best interests of the country.— 
“Bolton Guardian.”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

author: the chaplain Mrs. M. A. Comer
title: LEHI W. S. A.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol18 | 1889 | January | Issue: 16 | Title: LEHI W. S. A. | Author: the chaplain Mrs. M. A. Comer]

LEHI W. S. A.
	Pursuant to a call the ladies of Lehi met
in the Relief Society Hall Thurs. Dec. 19,
1889 to organize a branch of the W. S. A
Mrs. Rebecca Standring was elected tempo-
rary chairman and Mrs Sarah T. Evans Secy.
Mrs. Standring announced the object of the
meeting.
	Singing. “My country ’tis of thee." Prayer
by the chaplain Mrs. M. A. Comer.
	The secretary then read the call after which
the following officers were elected. Mrs. Mary
Ann Webb, Vice Pres. Mrs. Sara T. Evans,
Secy. Mrs. Laura Cutler, Chairman, Mrs. M.
M.	Gaddie, Josephine Brown, Mary Ann
Standring, Sarah A. Karrew, executive com-
mittee.
	The secretary read the constitution of the
N. W. S. A. which was unanimously adopted.
The by laws and regulations as adopted in
Utah Co. were also read and adopted. 
	Vice Pres. M. A. Webb thanked the assembl-
age for the honor that had been conferred upon
her. It was one duty to convert the men as
well as the women to the necessity of the
women having the franchise. We in Utah
have had the franchise and can appeal to his-
tory to prove that we never abused it, but used
it wisely and intelligently always standing
bravely for the right. As mothers it is our
duty to understand politics. We should be
able to instruct our sons and daughters that
they may understand their rights and privi-
leges as American citizens. Believed the day
was not far distant when the women would en-
joy their rights of the ballot.
	The following address was then delivered
by Mrs. Mary M. Gaddie:—
	Mrs. President, Ladies and Gentlemen:—
I stand before you to address you on the sub-
ject of women suffrage. I am proud to see
the women feel that they have rights. Let us
go to work with a determination never to give
up until we gain those rights.
	I have studied this subject and asked my-
self the question. “Why are women not
allowed to vote as well as men.” There is but
one answer. The women have never asked
 for the right. It is true that a few noble
women in the nation have been working for
it many years but a large majority have

[Column 2]
thrown their influence against it, and said "I
can trust to men to do my business.” “What
can women do anyway?” The reason they
talk thus is that they do not seem to under-
stand that they have any rights only those
which men give them. I assert that women
have done as much towards making this
nation what it is to-day as men have. This is
a broad assertion but I expect to substantiate
it before I get through. When Columbus was
inspired with the belief that there was a
country on the other side of the ocean men
scorned the idea, but a noble woman grasped
it and helped with her means to give him an 
outfit to go on his exploring eqpedition. There-
fore, we give women the credit for the dis-
covery of this good land. When the discov- 
ery was made men began to gather here from 
all the European world, and as they could
not get along without the women, they came
too. They suffered all the privations and
hardships that men did. They went to work,
the women with the men, and soon began to
prosper; as they prospered they became inde-
pendent, growing stronger as the years rolled
by. All these years they had been paying
taxes to England, but they became tired of
this as they were not represented in the mother
country, and “taxation without representa-
tion,” was their war cry. Ladies, that is one
condition to-day and has been for over a hun-
dred years taxation without representation.
The colonists went to war and gained their in-
dependence. When war the was over the men
formulated a constitution for the government
of the people and declared that all were created
free and equal, and the rights of life, liberty
and the pursuit of happiness were guaranteed
to them.
	Then emigration took its march toward the
setting sun. Woman had been declared free
and equal with man. She must go with him
through the wilderness, across the deserts,
through a country filled with savages. She
 was equal with him in all the trials and hard-
ships of pioneer life. They never stopped their
onward march until they landed on the
Pacific coast, by this time women had helped
to build up a great nation. The men had
held the reins of government with a firm and
steady hand. They made all the laws and
administered them, created all the offices and
filled them all with men. And what else has
woman done besides sharing the hardships.
She has reared presidents, senators, congress- 
men, legislators, governors, judges, lawyers,
and warriors to fight the nation’s battles, in
fact every other class of men that compose
this great nation, and yet she has never been
allowed to cast a vote for the sons she has
reared to fill these high and exalted stations.
Yet this is a great and powerful nation there
is none other on the footstool of God that
equals it. Its glory and grandeur shine far
and wide. All nations are invited to come to
this "land of the free and home of the brave,”
and yet at least one half of her citizens have 
never been allowed the right of franchise, and
there is a dark spot on the horizon of its great-
ness. The women of the nation have been
looking at that spot. They find it is a star
with a veil over its face. That star is woman’s
suffrage. We want to remove the veil and
let the star shine forth with all its brightness
and prove to the world that this is indeed and
in truth a land of the free and a home of the
brave women as well as men. The sovereign-
ity of this nation is rested in the people.
Women as well as men are its sovereigns. The 
lawmakers are their servants.
	*	*	*	*	*	*	*	*
	As women have shared equally with men in
all the trials and hardships of building up a
nation until its greatness knows no bounds, is
it not fair and just that she should share  in
the honors and emoluments?


Ladies:— 
	Our cause is so just, that conquer we must.
 Let this be our motto, in God is our trust.
	Mrs. Laura Cutler was heartily in favor of 
woman suffrage.  As woman was in the be-
ginning created as a help-meet to man, it was 
only reasonable to suppose that she was en- 
dowed with equal intelligence that she might 
be an able assistant.  It has been said by 
some misanthropist, "a woman was always at 
the bottom of all trouble.” She is most cer-
tainly at the head of that which is honorable.
Without her whence would have come the pil- 
grim fathers, the patriots of the revolution, the
framers of our glorious constitution?  Through
all those trying times when our Independence
was in its infancy, who went hand in hand
with those patriots as they fought for their 
rights?  Who comforted and sustained them
and prayed while they fought?  Who endured
the hardships of those perilous times side by 
side, with the men traveling across the track-
less plains and bravely bearing their share of 
the burden.” The women all honor to them. 
Since the first woman's convention which 
was held at Senecca Falls N. Y. in June 1848 a
few have been struggling for the right of 
suffrage until now the movement is general.
It is said, a woman will always have her way. 
If this is the case, when the women of a nation 
are united they stand a good chance of getting 
what they ask for.  We want the right to say 
who shall hold office and make the laws we 
are governed by.  As we in Utah have held 
the franchise, we feel most keenly on the sub- 
ject.  If we expect to rear sons who will be
able to uphold and sustain our great and 
glorious constitution in all its purity we must 
prepare ourselves so to do. 
	Hon. Geo. Webb said “It is evident we 
must concede the point that in intelligence,
women are the equal of men, as we have seen 
by, those who preceeded me.” If the ladies of
the nation are united in this movement and
each converts her husband the victory is won.
That women will have their way is fully rec-
ognized in the lobbys of Congress.  Women
succeeding where men fail.  If mothers wish 
their sons to become politicians, they should be 
able to teach them.  A mother’s influence
over her child is boundless.  Many of our
prominent men and eminent politicians ac-
knowledge that their success is due to the in-
fluence of mother or wife. 
	Franchise is conferred by law and can be 
withdrawn by law.  Congress holding that 
power over us as a territory.  If Utah were a 
state (with the leading men we have to-day) 
the ladies of Utah would enjoy the franchise. 
	Mrs. Josephine Brown considered the sub- 
ject of Woman Suffrage a very important one. 
We should understand our positions and be 
united on this movement.  Let our motto be 
“United we stand, divided we fall.” 
	Mr. John Woodhouse spoke upon the con-
dition of women in past ages and of their 
present advancement, until now we find them 
equal with men in almost all the walks of life. 
It is claimed that women would have a purify-
ing effect on politics, but he considered 
American politics so rotten they were past 
redemption.  A different and better state of 
affairs must soon prevail and when that time 
came women would undoubtedly obtain their
rights.
	Bishop T. R. Cutler was proud of this or- 
ganization.  In all departments of life he be- 
lieved women were as reliable as men. They 
had proven themselves capable in various 
positions.  Had heard of female cashiers in
banks but not yet of a female defaulter. It 
was the duty of the ladies to maintain their
rights.  Had never heard an intelligent reason
advanced why women should not vote.  If the
women held the franchise many evils that
now exist in our midst would be abolished.

[Column 2]
	Mr. William Southwick was in sympathy 
with this movement and endorsed the senti-
ments that had been uttered.  Spoke upon the
condition of politics in the United States. 
	Through the industry of the ladies they 
could accomplish anything.  Believed the day
was not far distant when the ladies would 
have the franchise. 
	The meeting adjourned for one month.
	No of members enrolled 137.
		SARAH T. EVANS, Sec. pro. tem.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


The Ex-Empress of Brazil died suddenly of
heart disease at Oporto, on December 28. and
it is said that all Europe is in mourning for
this gentle spirit, whose only sin was the
crown she wore. Her heart was broken when
she was driven from Brazil, and change of air
and scene could not save her, she had been
Empress forty-seven years, and had spent her
long married life in assisting her husband in
contributing to the charities of her adopted
country, and watching over the education of
her daughters and their children. She always
held aloof from the affairs of the State; was
greatly loved by her people, for her charities
and good works were innunerable. In, every
way she was an exemplary woman and Prin-
cess. The news of her death came as a great
shock to the people in Brazil. Monarchists
and Republicans alike mourn deeply over her
untimely death. The regret is very general as
she was so greatly loved.-

author: Unknown
title: ADOPTDD BY THE ELEVENTH CONVENTION
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol7 | 1878 | Unknown | Issue: 18 | Title: ADOPTDD BY THE ELEVENTH CONVENTION | Author: Unknown]

ADOPTDD BY THE ELEVENTH CONVENTION
OF THE NATIONAL WOMAN SUFFRAGE
ASSOCIATION, HELD IN LINCOLN HALL,
WASHINGTON, D. C., JANUARY 9TH AND
10TH, 1879.

	Whereas, in the recent elections for the
popular branch of the National Legislature,
the question of woman suffrage was wholly
ignored by each of the great political par-
ties, even our most zealous friends not risk-
ing a vote in behalf of this principle, and
	Whereas, the result of said elections, in-
stead of being the overwhelming victory for
either party which each anticipated,has been
to more evenly balance them, so that if an
election for the next president should be
thrown into the House of Representatives,
the result may turn upon a financial issue
to which neither of the old parties is com-
mitted, and which is now represented by a
third party:
	Therefore resolved, Tbat we press upon
the National Party the vital question of 
national citizenship secured by constitu-
tional amendment as a question underlying
the financial and all other national issues;
	And further Resolved, That we respect-
fully remind all political parties that we
cannot have honest money until we have
honest men.
	Resolved, That the Forty-Fifth Congress,
in ignoring the petitions of more than three
hundred women citizens of high social
standing and culture asking for the remov-
al of their political disabilities, while
promptly enacting special legislation for
the removal of the political disabilities of
every man who presented to them a similar
petition, furnishes a clear illustration of the
total indifference of this Congress to the
rights of citizens belonging to a class de-
prived of political power.
	Whereas, a Sixteenth Constitutonal
Amendment for Woman Suffrage is now
pending on a tie vote in the House Judici-
ary Committee;—Yeas, Lapham, N. Y.;
Lynde, Wis.; Frye,. Me.; Butler, Mass.;
Conger, Mich.;—Nays, Knott, Ky.; Hart-
ridge, Ga.; Stenger, Penn.; McMahon, O.;
Culberson, Texas;—Absent Harris of Va.,
who declares he has never ihvestigated the
subject;
	Therefore Resolved,That it is the duty
of Harris of Va. to remain absent when a
vote on this question is taken, until he has
given it as much consideration, as if the
rights of all men were therein involved.
	Whereas, as announced by Senator
Blaine, it is the very essence of tyranny to 
count any citizens in the basis of representa-
tion who are denied a voice in their laws
and a choice in their rulers,
	Therefore Resolved, That counting wo-
men in the basis of representation, while
denying them the right of suffrage, is
compelling them to swell the number of
their tyrants and is an unwarrantable usur-
pation of power over one-half the citizens
of this Republic.
	Whereas, in President Hayes' last mes-
sage, he takes a truly paternal review of
the interests of this Republic, both great
and small, from the Army, the Navy and
our foreign relations, to the ten little Indi-
ans in Hampton, Va., our timber on the
western mountains, and the switches of the
Washington railroads; from the Paris Ex-
position, the postal service the abundant
harvests, and the possible bull-dozing of
some colored men in various southern dis-
tricts, to cruelty to live animals, and the
crowded condition of the mumies, dead
ducks and fishes sn the Smithsonian Insti.


[Column 1]
tution; yet forgets to mention twenty mill-
ion women citizens robbed of their social,
civil and political rights;
	Therefore Resolved, That a committee
of three be appointed from this Convention
to wait upon the President and remind him
of the existence of one-half of the Ameri-
can people whom he has accidentally over-
looked, and af whom it would be wise for
him to make some mention iu his future
messages.
	Whereas all of the vital principles invol-
ved in the thirteenth, fourteenth and fif-
teenth constitutional amendments have been
denied in their application to women by
courts, legislatures and political parties;
Therefore Resolved, That it is eminently
logical that these amendments should utter-
ly fail to protect even the male African, for
whom said courts, legislatures and parties
declare they were expressly designed and
enacted;
	And further, Resolved, That thus, ever
above all the short-sighted schemes of poil-
ticians will the inexorable law of justice
bind humanity together and vindicate the 
equil rights of all.
	Resolved, That the Senate Judiciary
Committee, in its report on the bill to allow
women to practice law in the courts of the
United States, in which they declare that
"further legislation is not necessary," evad-
ed the plain question at issue before them
in a manner unworthy of Judges learned in
the honorable profession of the law, and
thereby sanctioned an injustice to the wo-
men of the whole country.
	Resolved, That the judges of the Supreme
Court of the United States, in denying ad-
mission to its bar of Belva A. Lockwood,
who was entitled under the law and under
its rules to admission therat, violated their
oath of office.
	Resolved, That the policy of this govern-
ment in appointing agents to educate and
civilize the Indians, to obtain calico dresses
for squaws and aprons for papooses, and a 
comfortable salary for their own pockets out
of money justly due the Indian tribes, is in
harmony with man's treatment of woman
in appropriating her property, talents, time
and labors, and using the proceeds as he 
pleases in the name of protection.
	Resolved, That the oft-repeated declara-
tion by women, "I have all the rights I
want," is due to the same feeling of intimi-
dation and subserviency to those who feed,
clothe and shelter them (out of their own
unpaid earnings) which prevents the colored
men in the South from expressing an opin-
ion or casting a vote contrary to the wishes
of their former masters, unless the army of
the United States is at hand to protect them
from the consequences.
	Whereas the general government has re-
fused to exercise federal power to protect
women in their citizen's right to vote in the
various States and Territories, it should for-
bear to exercise federal power to disfran-
chise the women of Utah, who have had a
more just and liberal spirit shown them by
Mormon men than Gentile women in the
States have yet perceived in their rulers.
	Whereas the proposed-legislation for the 
Chinese women on the Pacific slope and for
outcast women in our cities, and the opin-
ion of the press that no respectable woman
should be seen in the streets after dark, are
all based upon the assumption that woman's
freedom must be forever sacrificed to man's
license;
	Therefore Resolved, That the ballot in
woman's hand is the only power by which
she can restrain the liberty of those men
who make our streets and highways dan-

[Column 2]
gerous to her, and secure the freedom that
belongs to her by day and by night.
	Resolved, That the National Woman
Suffrage Association urge the women citi-
zens of the United States to send petitions
not merely in names 40,000 strong, but by
the hundreds of thousands to the Forty-
Sixth Congress, asking for a constitutional 
amendment prohibiting the several states
from disfranchising United States' citizens
on account of sex.
	And further Resolved, That we urge each
woman who signs a Sixteenth Amendment
petition to send an individual petition to
the Forty-Sixth Congress, asking for the
removal of her political disabilities, that
the coming Congress may be educated to a 
just recognition of the rights and demands
of women citizens.
	Resolved, That we urge the women citi-
zens of the several states to go before their
own state legislatures during the present
winter with petitions and arguments for
the passage of a resolution urging Congress
to submit to the states the pending Six-
teenth Amendment to protect women citi-
zens in the right to vote.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

author: Unknown
title: PRACTICAL POLITICAL STUDY.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol19 | 1890 | February | Issue: 16 | Title: PRACTICAL POLITICAL STUDY. | Author: Unknown]

PRACTICAL POLITICAL STUDY.

	A Political Club was organized in Farming-
ton, Nov. 24, 1890, with J. F. Miller, a rising
young politician, as president; the members
being composed of both sexes. They meet as
a Legislative Assembly and as a House of
Representatives. They frame bills and
pass laws as systematically and earnestly as
though the fate of the Territory or nation de-
pended on their labors.
	A committee, appointed by the Speaker, on
Woman Suffrage, consisting of Lucy Clark,
Althalia M. Steed, Charles C. Miller, reported
the following bill, which, after an animated
discussion for three evenings, passed the Legis-
lature on its third reading by a vote of 26 to
22. The minority objected only to the clause
in the bill granting to women the right to hold
office. (The bill has not been sent to the
Governor yet.)
	"Be it enacted by the Governor and Legisla-
tive Assembly of Utah: That it shall be lawful
for women to vote at any and every election
held in the Territory of Utah, and their votes
shall be counted as valid and given effect.
And that they shall be entitled to hold any
office to which they may be elected."
	Lucy A. Clark, chairman of the committee
on Woman Suffrage, in presenting the bill,
said: Mr. Speaker, Members of the Legislature,
and Ladies and Gentlemen present: It is with
profound gratitude that we thank you for the
courtesy extended to us, the ladies who were
by special invitation from our honored presi-
dent, solicited to attend and take part in these
political deliberations. We consider that they
are educational and, therefore, accept of the
invitation, and trust that the moments we
spend here may be moments of pleasure and
profit. It is with pleasure that we present to
this honorable body of intelligent lawmakers
a bill asking that all political rights and privil-
eges may be guaranteed to women. We trust
that this honorable body will exhibit the same
degree of justice exhibited at the State Con-
stitutional Convention in Wyoming, when an
act, conferring equal rights upon all citizens,
passed the convention without an opposing
vote, and therefore became a commendable
plank in the platform, whereby they were ad-
mitted into the glorious union of States. The
Honorable Mr. Warren, Governor of Wyoming,
in his speech to the women, said: “Ladies, you
have builded well in the past, which is a suffic-
ient guarantee that your future actions will be
commendable.” It was ably proclaimed by the
champion of human rights, C. W. Penrose, that
if we would have a great race of men, we must
educate the mothers in politics, as well as other
branches, and as they had to pay taxes they
should have a voice in political affairs.
	Through a misguided and fanatical jealousy,
a board of political tricksters succeeded in
depriving the women of Utah of the suffrage,
after having intelligently and successfully exer-
cised the right for seventeen years. This un-
just measure was unsuccessfully opposed by
many intelligent men, and the leading women
suffragists of the nation.
	Some prejudice is being removed, and the
barriers that have stood in the path of woman's
progression so long are being obliterated one
by one. Their abilities are beginning to be
better appreciated by some. In twenty-four
of the forty-six States and Territories in the
Union, women exercise the suffrage on some
points. In Montana, twelve of the eighteen
county superintendents of schools are women.
Women have been placed upon the committee
of arrangements for the World's Fair, to be
held at Chicago in 1893, the highest position
of responsibility ever placed upon woman in
America.
	There is to be a pavilion constructed in hon-

[column 2]
or of Queen Isabella of Spain, whom we can
proudly and appropriately call the mother of
America, who, after Columbus had spent ten
years of toil and hardship, pleading with the
crowned heads of Europe for assistance to car-
ry out his plans, came to his relief and fitted
him out with ships and money, without which
he could not have made the discovery of this
renowned and glorious country. A lady archi-
tect, Miss Minerva Parker, has drawn the
plans for the pavilion to be erected in honor of
this renowned lady, and they have been ac-
cepted by the committee.
	Hamilton Wilcox, New York, said: “States-
men! politicians! editors! behold our move-
ment’s strength! Be wise in time; hasten,
while yet you may, to join the hosts who
struggle for the right!"
	We sincerely trust that there is not one gen-
tleman in this beautiful town of Farmington,
within the borders of this enlightened and
progressive Territory, or within the borders of
these United States, who could say to the Afri-
can slave, “step upon the platform with me,
share the emoluments of office with me,” and
at the same time say to his sainted mother,
his devoted wife, his beloved sister, or his angel
daughter, “remain below; live, breathe, and
pay taxes, but you shall have no voice in say-
ing who shall handle those taxes.”
	That all prejudice, fear and selfishness may
be ground to powder in the mill of human
progress, and that our bill may become a law,
we earnestly pray.				L. A. C.

author: Unknown
title: WOMAN FOR OFFICE.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | October | Issue: 10 | Title: WOMAN FOR OFFICE. | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN FOR OFFICE.

	In the great cry against Woman's Rights,
the greatest fear of some men seems to be
that women might obtain some office that is
now held by man. Have no fear on that
score, brethren. Among all my female ac-
quaintances I do not know of one woman
who wishes to hold the office of sheriff;
but many of my male acquaintances have
made themselves unhappy because their
friends did not see fit to elect them sheriff,
or at least to nominate them for that office.
I know of no woman who wishes to be State
representative, but I know of a number of
men who feel chagrined and indignant be-
cause their friends thought them unfit to
be legislators, and voted for the "other
man." Who knows a woman who would
be willing to go before a convention for any
mere office of state or nation? And who
does not know that there are other things
for women to think about? No, no, it is
not the desire to hold office (although there
are many offices that women might hold
without losing the respect that is accorded
to woman) that engages so many female
minds, but the right to hold their own
children, and to hold what they earn by
the sweat of their brow, and the right to
say whether a man, or a mere apology,
should sit in the legislative halls and make


the laws whereby they are governed. In
the days of slavery it was the slave mother
and not the slave father that held the child.
The slave has been emancipated; given the
right to vote, and made custodian (so to
speak) of his wife, her earnings and her
children. While the uneducated and super-
stitious male negro is made of as much im-
portance at the ballot-box as the president
of the United States, woman, with the
companionship of books and papers, is
deemed unfit to vote. Such is the law of
our nation; we make no war upon that law.
We only wish to suggest to man that he
pause upon his road of usefulness, and con-
sider how much (or rather how little) he
would have accomplished had it not been
for the companionship and counsel of wo-
man. Where is the man noted for his wis-
dom and goodness; but will say that "a good
woman made me what I am?" Woman
guides the destinies of man; acknowledge
her worth by recognizing her rights of citi-
zenship.				MRS. E. C. 
	—Woman's Words.

author: Unknown
title: VENTION.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol17 | 1888 | January | Issue: 15 | Title: VENTION. | Author: Unknown]

			VENTION.

	The National Woman Suffrage Association
will meet in Washington, D. C., January 21st,
22nd, and 23rd, 1889, at the Congregational
Church, corner of G. and 10th streets.
	Delegates from all the states are invited to be
present. The question of uniting the two so-
cieties will be again under consideration, as the
resolutions passed at the recent annual meeting
of the American society, held at Cincinnati,
demand some action on the part of the Na-
tional Association. For this and other reasons
a large gathering of the friends of this move-
ment is desirable.
	For twenty years in succession these conven-
tions have been held at the national capital,
with hearings before committees of the Senate
and House judiciary, and able minority reports
thereon, which have been extensively circulated
throughout the nation, and thus a great educa-
tional work has been accomplished. We see
the effect in the extension of the suffrage to 
woman, and a growing interest on her part in
public affairs.
	In the presidential canvass just ended, women
have taken a more active part than ever be-
fore, and shown that they are by no means a
unit on political questions. For the first time 
large delegations of women have marched with
banners, in various processions, and spoken on
the platforms of different political parties.
	Neither among politicians, nor women them-
selves, is this in any sense a party movement.
While the prohibition party incorporated
woman suffrage in its platform, the Republicans
of Kansas made it a fact by extending munici-
pal suffrage to the woman of that state. The
Democrats of Connecticut on several occasions
voted for woman suffrage while Republicans
voted against it. In the New York Legislature
Republicans and Democrats have alike advoca-
ted and voted for the measure. In Congress
the last vote in the House was eighty Repub-
licans for woman suffrage, and four Democrats
against it, and not a single Democrat voted in
favor of it on the floor of the Senate. Both 
the Labor and Greenback parties have uni-
formly recognized woman suffrage in their
platforms. 
	There are movements now in the political


horizon pointing to the formation of a national
party in the near future, combining the interests
of labor, woman, temperance and other reforms.
Our strength for future action lies in the fact
that woman suffrage has some advocates in all
parties, and that we, as a party, are pledged to
none.
	The denial of the ballot to woman is the
great political crime of the century, before
which tariff, finance; land monopoly, temper-
ance, labor, and all economic questions sink into
insignificance, for the right of suffrage involves
all questions of personal and property rights.
	While each party in power has refused to
enfranchise woman, being sceptical as to her
moral influence in government; yet with strange
inconsistency they alike seek the aid of her
voice and pen in all important political
struggles, as shown in the late presidential
campaign. While not morally bound to obey
the laws made without her consent, yet we find
woman the most law-abiding class of citizens
in the community. While not recognized as a
competent part of the government, she is most
active in all great movements for education,
religion, philanthropy and reform.
	The magnificent convocation of women from
the world over, held at Washington last March,
a council more important than any since the
Diet of Worms, was proof of woman's marvel-
ous power of organization; and her clear com-
prehension of the underlying principles of all
questions of government. With such evidence
of her keen insight and executive ability, we
invite all interested in good government to give
us the inspiration of their presence in the com-
ing convention.
		ELIZABETH CADY STANTON, Pres.
		SUSAN B. ANTHONY, Vice Pres.
		MATILDA JOSLYN GAGE, Ch. Ex. Com.

author: Unknown
title: CONCLUDED.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol8 | 1879 | June | Issue: 1 | Title: CONCLUDED. | Author: Unknown]

CONCLUDED.

	Let me show you one good that has come
to woman through her ballot in Wyoming.
The payment of men and women teachers
has been adjusted upon the basis of the
same pay for the same qualifications, and
been thus equalized by direct statute for
political power always benefits the parties
holding it. In reading ancient history we
find political power shown by one person,
re-acting upon a whole class. For instance,
the ancient Roman women worshiped the
goddess Viriplaca, whose special mission
was to appease husbands; but after Volum-
nia had shown power to stop the conquer-
ing march of her son Coriolanus, at the very
gates of Rome, the whole sex were treated
with more consideration, and a temple was
erected to Woman's Fortune.
	Milman calls attention to the disrespect
with which Greek women were treated,
because of their total want of political
power, their "political insignificance."
	Now we women of the states know that
the women of the Territories of Wyoming
and Utah are not secure in their political
rights, because the women of the nation
have no political rights. Scarcely a session
of Congress but some political busy-body
introduces into that body a bill to disfran-
chise the women of these territories. We
are tired of it! we are tired of so much at-
tention being paid to the wrongs of men

[Column 2]
and so little to the wrongs of women.
When such a speech as that of Mr. Blaine
is made, we wonder where political consis-
tency has flown.
	Then there was another point of Mr.
Blaine's speech that was bitter to the think-
ing women of the nation. That is, his
pointing out the fact that members of the
House hold their office in defiance of the
rights of representation.
	We know and feel that every Representa-
tive and every Senator, the Hon. Mr. Blaine
himself not excluded, hold their seats in
defiance of woman's rights of representa-
tion. Women have been counted in the
basis of representation, and are not allowed
to exercise political power.
	Mr. Blaine, in his argument, quoted that
portion of the 14th Amendment which
says, "Representatives shall be apportioned
among the several states, &c., &c., but when
the right to vote, &c., is denied to any of 
the 'male' inhabitants, &c.
	Mr. Blaine declared the patent undenia-
ble intent of this provision was, that if any
class of voters were denied, or in any way
abridged their right of suffrage, the class
so denied or abridged should not be counted
in the basis of representation.
	The women of the nation notice this an-
nulling provision, because through it they
are cunningly made to count in the basis of
representation.
	It is an underlying principle of true
statesmanship that constitutions should be
general in their character, securing in a 
general way the rights of all members of 
the nation. But the women of the nation
are indebted to the Republican party for a 
principle in opposition to general rights, by
the introduction of the word "male" three
times in the constitution.
	I shall make use of Mr. Blaine's very lan-
guage, and say that "wherever a feeble pro-
test is made against this injustice," to one-
half the people the taunt comes, "What are
you going to do about it?" and "how do
you propose to help yourselves?"
	As Mr. Evarts so graphically said in the
great Tilton-Beecher trial, "man holds wo-
man in the hollow of his hand to protect or
to crush," so the great political parties seem
to say to the women of the nation, "We
hold your rights in our grasp; we can pro-
tect you or crush you. What are you going
to do about it? and how do you propose to
help yourselves?"
	That they hold power we acknowledge—
that they hold it rightfully we deny.
	Let us look at a few other ways in which
the United States has touched the rights of
women where protection has been secured
her by legislation outside of itself. One in-
stance that has come to my knowledge
since I have been in your city, is in the
case of pensions for colored women. The
United States not only secured the ballot to
the black male citizen outside of state
authority, but it has touched the family re-
lation with its powerful hand. It has as-
sumed that the woman with whom a col-
ored soldier was living at the time of his
death was his wife, notwithstanding he
may have lived for many years in recog-
nized married relations with another wo-
man, and become the father of children by
her during this period. In one case com-
ing under the cognizance of our Washing-
ton woman lawyer, Mrs. Lockwood, a pen-
sion was by United States authority thus
granted to a woman living with such col-
ored soldier at time of his death, although
she had no other claim upon it. This sol-
dier had, during the period of slavery, been
married in his master's house by a regular-
ly ordained clergyman to another woman,

[Column 3]
and by that wife had become the father of 
five or six children. This woman was his
lawful widow, according to state and re-
ligious law. These children were his law-
ful children, according to state and religi-
ous law, but the United States stepped in,
and made of this married woman a thing I 
will not name, and cast her children into
the world with the brand of illegitimacy.
	What a strange thing is law! what a
strange power the United States wields !
and how unjustly it always manages to
touch women, for now comes up Utah with
similar complaint.
	I am not careful to touch the religious
aspects of this Utah question. I do not
care for it only so far as it touches, or is
touched by woman's political rights, al-
though I do know that woman's political
wrongs and her religious wrongs have been
very closely intermingled in the past. I
recall a Papal Bull of Urban II, in the 12th
century which compelled priests to discard
their wives, making of thousands of wo-
men in England, wives who were not wed,
of children, offspring who had no recogniz-
ed fathers.
	We of the National Woman's Suffrage
Association have nothing to do with the 
religious rights of women in Utah, except
in so far as they intermingle with, and
touch woman's political rights. But the
Utah question, which now comes up again,
is not simply a religious question. The 
government is continuously striving to
touch the political rights of the women of
this territory. Its Governor is a United
Statas officer, and in his last report to the
Secretary of the Interior, he so far trans-
cended the duties of his office, as to sug-
gest the disfranchisement of Utah women.
Almost every session of Congress sees some
bill of similar import introduced.
	The general government did not confer
this right, did not secure even the exer-
cise of this right. The territorial legisla-
ture the same as in Wyoming, secured to
women the exercise of the right of suffrage;
the United States according to its own
theory, has nothing at all to do with the suffrage
question. It proposes to disfranchise those
women as a punishment for their religious
belief; it proposes to make social outcasts
of them, as it has already done with the
wives of some of its black soldier voters.
	Looking back through history we find no
act of the Romish Church more vile than
that which compelled its priests to disown
their wives and legitimate children—none
which so utterly demoralized society, and
destroyed its tens of thousands of women.
And although as a body of reformers, I
again say we do not touch religion except
where it, and politics together, infringe up-
ou the rights of women, I do not hesitate
to say for myself individually, that I have
no faith in any form of religion, be it what
it may, Christian, Mohammedan, Buddhist,
that receives revelation only through some
man; or farther than that, I will say, I have
no faith in any form of religion that does
not place man and woman on an exact
equality of religious rights. Two forms of 
religion of the present day which have
risen through women, or as revelations to
her, namely the Shaker and the Spiritual,
do give us equality in religious rights, for
men and women. But I close by again
calling your attention to the inconsistency
of United States laws, and their especial
injustice to women by interference with
those rights secured them by state or terri-
torial laws, as in case of the colored soldier's
wife, as in case the assumption that the


United States had a right to prohibit the
exercise of the suffrage by a woman in
New York, although New York itself did
not interfere: as in case of the virtual pro-
hibition by the United States of jury rights
to the women of Wyoming; in case of the
presumptuous suggestion of the Governor
of Utah that its women should be disfran-
chised; as in case of such bills so often in-
troduced in Congress.
	And Senator Blaine with his eye on the
Presidency—with his primal admission
that the right of suf[-]rage belongs to all citi-
zens of the United States, devoting his
recent great speech entirely to the denial
of black men's rights of citizenship, his
talk about the black men being the "un-
witting instrument" of increasing the po-
litical strength of a party inimical to him;
his wish that the basis of representation
should be lessened in those states where
the black man is deprived of his right to
vote, exhibits an inconsistency that all
thinking women notice. In this speech he
said not a word about lessening the basis of
representation on account of woman's being
deprived of her right to vote.
	The country was wild over Mr. Blaine's
speech. All Washington was agog to hear
it. The gallery and floor of the Senate
Chamber were filled; the diplomats were
present; Democrats and Republicans were
alike disturbed by it; but really it was a 
very small matter in comparison to the de-
nial of representation to women. Woman
is not only denied the right of representa-
tion, but she is actually counted in the basis
of representation, and thus to use the words
of Mr. Blaine, is the "unwitting instru-
ment" of electing men to make laws against
her. She is doubly powerless, because not
only has her own actual representation been
taken from her but a forced representation
is given her by her being counted in the 
basis of representation and she has never
been allowed to select her own representa-
tives. The only analogy I have ever been
able to find, is the old three-fifths representa-
tion of slaves; a system which allowed
masters to use slaves as a means of tighten-
ing their own bonds.
	But to borrow Mr. Blaine's form of ex-
pression, let me tell Mr. Blaine and all poli-
ticians, that I know something of the 
opinion of the women of the nation, and I 
know they intend to be recognized as citi-
zens of the nation secured in the exercise
of all the powers and rights of citizens of 
the nation. If this security has not come
under the 14th Amendment, it must come
under a 16th, for woman intends to possess
"equal personal rights and equal political
privileges with all other citizens." She
asks for nothing outside the power of the 
United States to secure, she asks for noth-
ing outside the duty of the United States 
to secure.
	Politicians may as well look this fact
squarely in the face and become wise after
the wisdom of the world, for in just so far
as they ignore and forget the women of the 
country, in just so far will they themselves
be ignored and forgotton.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol15 | 1886 | April | Issue: 21 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	The large numbers in which women are
registering to vote under the new municipal
suffrage law in Kansas ought to set at rest the
fear that women would not use the suffrage if
they had it. The women voted at the primary
elections on March 6, and there was not the
slightest disturbance. One thousand of the
leading women of Leavenworth registered
within two days.
	The U. S. District Attorney for Washington
Territory recommends the women to go on vot-
ing as usual, and if refused, to take out a man-
damus to compel acceptance to their votes.
The validity of the woman suffrage law having
been repeatedly affirmed by several Supreme
Court decisions before the adverse decision by
only two of the judges. There is great doubt
whether the adverse decision will be sustained
by the full bench.
	Twenty-seven years ago, Henry Ward
Beecher, in a powerful lecture on woman
suffrage, predicted that one of the first good
results of the reform would be the removal of
the polling places to more respectable quarters,
and the increased order and civility of elec-
tions. The Republican Convention, which met
at Topeka, Kansas, on March  7, expressed a 
cordial welcome to the newly enfranchised
ladies, and adopted a series of resolutions, en-
titled, "For the new voters," favoring safety
and convenience of voters at the polls, and
recommending their removal to more respect-
able locations. They also declare that no
woman whose political duties call her on the
street on election day shall be subjected to any
inconvenience, or have any ticket thrust into
her hand unless she asks for it. The Boston
Traveler says: "This view of what is really
the practical effect of woman suffrage is inter-
esting, and the advocates of political enfran-
chisement for women will be more than mortal
if they can refrain from airs of triumph in
this fulfilment of their long prophesies as to
the better state of affairs when women go to
the polls."—Ex.


They talk about a woman’s sphere
	As though it had a limit:
There’s not a place in earth or heaven,
There’s not a task to mankind given,
There’s not a blessing or a woe,
There’s not a whispered yes or no,
There's not a life, or death or birth,
That has a feather’s weight of worth,
	Without a woman in it.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

author: Unknown
title: FRAGE.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol1 | 1872 | March | Issue: 20 | Title: FRAGE. | Author: Unknown]

				FRAGE.

	Congress adjourned without the bill directed
against Utah becoming law, although it passed
the Senate in a modified form, but still retain-
ing the provision depriving the women of
Utah of the ballot. This is a point in which
all the women of the United States should be
interested, as far, at least, as they desire to
occupy a political equality, not simply with
men, but such men as are to be found among the
most degraded class of the colored race,
or the most ignorant foreigners that may seek
our shores and desire citizenship. Does any
one think that if Congress were to deprive the
women of Utah of the elective franchise by
repealing the Territorial statute conferring it
upon them, that the same body would imme-
diately turn around and undo its own work,
by again conferring the suffrage on them
through any measure extending that right to
all American women? Those who are agitat-
ing the question, who are working for the
extension of the elective franchise to women
all through the United States, are directly
interested in this matter, for they may rest
assured that the Congress that strikes at the
principle of woman suffrage, by depriving the
women of a Territory of the right to vote, will
not stultify itself by passing or recommending
any measure, nor any Constitutional amend-
ment, recognizing woman’s right to this
political status.	
	The Woman’s Suffrage Association of New
York saw this, and passed a resolution, which
was presented to Congress, protesting against


the blow aimed at woman's rights in the Frel-
inghuysen bill. And advocates of woman’s
cause throughout the entire Union should be
alive to the fact that, if a similar attempt is
made next session of Congress, it will have a
double object, as it had before: to further the
plans for plunder of political adventurers in
this Territorty, and to strike a blow at
the woman’s suffrage cause.
	Most of the men who urge with the most
strenuousness the crusade against the Mormons,
are avowed enemies to the woman’s rights move-
ment in its every aspect; and while they are
without influence to effect anything against it
in the States, they know very well that if they
can get any measure through Congress in
opposition to it and attacking it in a Territory,
they will have successfully made a point.
	Every lecturer, every editor, every person
advocating or interested in the cause of woman,
should make this matter his or her own, in
the event of another attempt to deprive the
women of a Territory of the right of suffrage.

author: Unknown
title: NATIONAL AMERICAN WOMAN
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol19 | 1890 | December | Issue: 13 | Title: NATIONAL AMERICAN WOMAN | Author: Unknown]

NATIONAL AMERICAN WOMAN
		SUFFRAGE ASSOCIATION.
	The twenty-third annual convention of the
National-American Woman Suffrage Associa-
tion will be held in Washington, D. C., in
Albaugh’s Opera House, Feb. 26—March 1st,
1891.
	Each annual convention has recorded an
increasing public sentiment in favor of woman’s
legal and political enfranchisement. Largely
as a consequence of our movement, the in-
dustries and learned professions have been
opened to women, laws have been changed
to secure protection for her personal and
property rights, and she has been welcomed
to a partial participation in government, as a
school voter in twenty-two States, as a munipal
voter in Kansas, and a holder of important
public offices in nearly every State in the
Union. But the coming convention will have a
fuller significance than any that preceded it.

	The admission of Wyoming into the Union

[Column 2]
as a State with equal rights for women
guaranteed in its organic law, not only sets its
sovereign seal of approval upon woman suffrage
after practical experience for twenty-one
years, but makes women a recognized factor
in national politics. Hereafter the chief
executive and the members of both houses of
Congress will owe their election partly to the
votes of women. The injustice and absurdity
of allowing women in one State to be sovereign
rulers, and across the line in every direction
obliging them to occupy the position of a
subject class, taxed without representation and
governed without consent, and this in a nation
which by its Constitution guarantees equal
rights, to all the States, and equal protection
to all the citizens, must soon be manifest even
to the most conservative and prejudiced. We
therefore congratulate the friends of woman
suffrage everywhere that at last there is one
spot under the American flag where justice is
done to women. Wyoming, is all hail ! the
first true Republic the world has ever seen !

	The present is a time of upheaval in politics.
New ideas are pushing to the front. The
brotherhood of humanity, the establishment
of justice, the protection of the weak, the
value of the individual, the right of all to
equal privileges and opportunities, are now
not only the rallying cry of moral and social
leaders, but are pressing forward to the ballot-
box as the expression of the popular will.
These all include and hinge upon the en
franchisement of women. Let us, therefore,
gather to this convention as those who know
that their cause is the outcome of all the heroic
struggles for freedom which have been made
in the past, and the forerunner of a more
