title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | May | Issue: 23 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: Unknown]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	MRS. CAMPBELL a lady lecturer known
to fame, has been lecturing during the past
month in some of the most conservative
old towns of Mass., on Woman's Suffrage.
The Woman's Journal in alluding to the
fact, and speaking of Somerset, said it was
at first doubtful whether the lecture would
receive even respectful consideration. At
Taunton, Mrs. Campbell lectured under the
auspices of the Woman's Christian Temper-
ance Union. "The lady was self-possessed,
spoke eloquently, and commanded close at-
tention." She believes that the ballot would
be better used by women than by men, and 
thinks in the good time coming, when wo-
men help make the laws there will be some
improvement.
	A MEETING in support of the extension
of the franchise to women was held in the 
Temperance Hall, Leceister, England,
March 21st. A large number of ladies
were present. * * The Chairman
in opening the proceedings said they were
there to discuss a serious and interesting
question, which must arise in a highly civ-
ilized state of society, namely the proper
position of women.
	It was moved by one of the leading cit-
izens that a petition in favor of the Wo-
man's Suffrage Bill should be signed by the
Chairman in behalf of the meeting and for-
warded to Mr. Taylor, M. P. for presenta-
tion to the House of Commons. Another
gentleman seconded the motion, and Miss
Helen Taylor, member of the London
School Board, supported the motion as fol-
lows: The main interest of the question
to her was from the point of responsibility.
The Suffrage should be used by women not 
as a matter of right but of duty, and a wo-
man who had been successful in life, and who
said she did not want to vote, had a very
low idea of the responsibility attaching to 
her. There were questions affecting a large
class of women in the country, which wo-
men could understand better than men,
however noble they might be, and upon
which they should be allowed to exercise
their opinions. The interests of the whole
nation would profit by the franchise being
extended to women, and she hoped the
time was not far distant when their prayer
would be granted. Meetings of a similar
character to this have been held recently in
many of the large towns in England and
Scotland. These items were taken from
the April No. of the Woman's Suffrage
Journal of that country.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

author: Unknown
title: WOMAN BEFORE THE LAW.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | January | Issue: 14 | Title: WOMAN BEFORE THE LAW. | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN BEFORE THE LAW.

	Advocates of equal rights have many annoy-
ing things to contend with, many exasperating
arguments to meet, many disagreeable people
to answer, and these are all embodied in the
woman who "has all the rights she  wants."
There is some excuse for the opposition of
men who are unwilling to share their long-ex-
ercised power. Some allowance must be made
for those men who are conscientious in th be-
lief that the lines will be made harder instead
of easier for women when they possess political
equality, and who oppose it for these kindly,
although misguided, reasons. But patience
reaches its limit when it encounters a woman
who would deny all other women the suffrage
because "she has all the rights she wants."
There is only one person more narrow, selfish
and ungenerous, and that is the woman who
signs a "remonstrance" against granting the
ballot. A few women have done this, an in-
finitesimal number compared to those who have
signed petitions asking for the privilege of
voting, and their posterity will blush to read
their names. The woman of a few generations
hence will be utterly at a loss to understand
the spirit that would inspire one of her sex to
ask that freedom and independence might not
be conferred upon her.
	But let us consider for a moment the con-
dition of these women who "have all the rights
they want." We will glance at the events of
one week, that chanced to get into the daily
print, only a small part of what is happening
in this respect all the time. We find Miss
Irene W. Coit passing with honors the severe
examination for entrance to Yale College and
refused admission because she is a woman.
We see a number of Methodist Assemblies
of ministers voting against permitting women
to sit as delegates in the General Conference
simply because they are women. We read of
the Presbyterian Conference of Alabama vot-
ing that women shall not be permitted to speak
in public. In the city of Toledo a street must
be cared for by two commissioners who own
property on the street. Upon one it was found
that every property owner on the street was
a woman but one. Therefore, instead of ap-
pointing this man and one woman, they
selected a man from another street for the
other commissioner. In New Hampshire a
drunken Canadian farmer stole their child
from his wife and gave it to his parents in
Canada. The mother, protected by a detective
secured the child, which she found Covered
with dirt and rags. The father carried the
matter into court and, sustained by the law, a
constable took the child away from its mother
and returned it to the husband's parents in
Canada.
	In Iowa a man and wife lived together eight
years and had two children, but whether they
were married or not, nobody knew. Finally
the man deserted her and left her to provide
for the children by her own labor, which she did.
One night he stole the children. The law de-
cided that, if she was a wifethe husband


should have the children, if she was a mistress,
she might have them. A fine distinction and
encouraging to matrimony! The man swore
that they were married, and the judge gave
him the children. Down in Kentucky a poor
woman worked three months with her needle
to buy a cow in order that she might have the
milk to raise her three little children. Her
husband “traded the cow for a gun, filled up
with whisky, and started on the war-path.”
The woman was helpless because in that State
the wife’s earnings belong to the husband.
These are mere straws, drift upon the current.
Similar cases are occurring every week out of
the fifty-two year after year. And yet well-
protected, happily-situated women, having no
thought for their less fortunate sisters will lean
back in calm complacency and say, “I have
all the rights I want and 1 am not willing that
the burdens should be lifted from the shoulders
Of other women.”
Do they know what are the laws regarding
Women in their own State? There are only
three or four States in the Union where a
mother has the right to her child. In the
oth rs, either the child belongs absolutely to
the father where he has not forfeited his right,
or where both are proved to be equally capable
of taking care of it, the law gives it . to the
father.In New York the father "may-will his
children away from the mother and at .his
death they will be taken from her and dis:
posed of'as directed. He may will away an
unborn child and at its birth it will be taken
from the mother’s arms. In every State in
the Union the joint earnings of the two belong
absolutely to the husband. In many States
the wife’s property, real and personal, becomes
the possession of the husband at marriage. In
others, if the wife dies without children the
husband takes all the property if he dies the
wife gets but one-third, and in some .instances
only ,a life interest in this. In a number.of
States if the woman earns furniture, clothes
arid food by her own labor, the husband may
sell them and pocket the money. If the wife
breaks an arm or leg she cannot iue for
damages, but the husband sues the cause of the
jnjury for the loss ofher services, and the
money belongs to him. In more than half
of the States little children, from seven to
twelve years of age, may consent to their own
ruin, aUd the mothers are powerless because
the law sanctions it.
in forty-three States women are forced to
pay the full amount of their taxes and are per-
mitted no representation, not even the privi-
lege of voting for the assessor. They-are
compelled to obey laws they have no voice in
making and, when accused of crime, they are
refused trial by a jury of their peers. Space
is too brief to mention all the inequalities of
the law. It is true there have been many
mprovements since the day when the old
commonlaw of England everywhere pre-
vailed, which gave a man the same ownership
of his wife as he had of his horse or cow, but
these changes have represented the labor of
years. It will also be found that almost with-
out exception, the champions who secured the
repeal of the obnoxious laws were pronounced
Woman-suffragists, and they will testify that
the difficulty has been infinitely greater be-
cause’they were working for a disfranchised
class. Our law makers represent their con- stituents, the ones who placed them in power.
They have an eye single to the interests of
those who posse33 the influence of the ballot.
They are not legislating for Chinese or Indians
or idiots or women. Women have the right of
petition, but legislators scorn a petition .which
3 not signed by voters. Massachusetts wo-
men petitioned twelve years for the right to
own the clothes upon tfieir backs. While the
natural progress of public sentiment may, in
the next fifty years, be sufficient tp striko.out
most of these unjust laws, is it necessary to
await the slow process of half a century? Can
any one doubt that if women were enfranchised
it would be one-fourth that time until there
would be an absolute equality of the laws?
it will be observed that in considering this
question only the equity has been noticed.
The social side, the effect of woman’s ballot
upon education,-temperance, morality, and
kindred matters, has b?en entirely omitted,
becnji3e there may be a variety of .opinions
upon these subjects but it does not seem pos-
sible that there can be a difference of senti-
ment upon the point that, in matters of justice
all should have the same privilege and the
laws should bear upon all alike. We ask our
readers of both sexes to' lay aside prejudice
and consider this subject from the standpoint
ef reason, logic and broad humanity,' and ,
answer the question whether a government can
be truly republican, which makes these insid-
ous distinctions.
Locomotive Firemen’s Magazine.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

author: Unknown
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol7 | 1878 | Unknown | Issue: 14 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: Unknown]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	THE AMERICAN Woman Suffrage Asso-
ciation held its Annual Convention in In-
dianapolis, Nov. 13 and 14. We gather a
few important items in reference to the
Convention from our Exchanges. There
were many leading suffragists present. The
Committee on Credentials reported thirteen
States represented. Dr. Mary F. Thomas
of Richmond, Ind., presided. Lucy Stone
read the report of the Executive Commit-
tee. Wm. Loyd Garrison, President of the 
A. W. S. Association, was unable to be
present, but sent a letter “able and elo-
quent,” which was read by Mr. Hinkley of
Rhode Island. Miss Matilda Hindman of
Colorado said if the suffrage workers of the
East would contribute money, speakers and
workers, the next report from the Centen-
nial State would be woman suffrage trium-
phant. The speakers of whom particular
mention was made were J. Ellen Foster of
Iowa, H. B. Blackwell of Boston, Margaret
W. Campbell, Hannah Tracy Cutler, Hon.
George W. Julian and Mary F. Eastman.
	Resolutions were adopted, from which
we extract the following:
	Whereas, Theoretically governments de-
rive their just powers from the consent of 
the governed; and whereas, practically,
women are the greatest sufferers from de-
fective legislation, therefore,
	Resolved, That a government of the peo-
ple, by the people, for the people, must be
a government equally composed of men and
women; and that the co-operation of the 
sexes is alike essential to a happy home, a
refined society, a Christian church and a
Republican State.
	*	*	*	*	*	*	*
	Whereas, The United States courts have
affirmed that “the regulation of suffrage
belongs exclusively to the States,” and
that “women are citizens, and as such may
be made voters by appropriate State legis-
lation.” And whereas a sixteenth amend-
ment to the federal constitution, abolishing
political distinction on account of sex, al-
though just and necessary, can be more
easily obtained when several States have
set the example; therefore,
	Resolved, That we urge every existing
State association to renewed effort upon 
the next and each following State Legisla-
ture, and in every State where no such as-
sociation exists we urge individual effort,
and the immediate formation of a State
society


THAT gallant sailor, the British Admiral
Ramsey, says that on the coast of Africa he
once saw a regiment of rifle-women—and
black as they were, he must say he never
saw a finer regiment. All the officers were
women, and there was not one man in the
whole regiment. They were most courage-
ous, and fought bravely; in fact, a friend
told him that when they went into action
they fought like so many tigresses.
	DON'T refuse your wife a little extra spend-
ing money just now if she should ask it;
you will get it all back again in slippers
and dressing gowns next Christmas.—
Washington Post.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿

author: Unknown
title: THE NEW CONSTITUTION.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol17 | 1888 | March | Issue: 20 | Title: THE NEW CONSTITUTION. | Author: Unknown]

THE NEW CONSTITUTION.

	The following is the Constitution proposed
for the new National-American Woman Suf-
frage Association. This constitution has been
approved by a majority of the committee on
union, appointed by the American W. S. A.,
and is now submitted to a vote of the members
of the Association, the vote to be taken by
mail, in accordance with the resolution passed
at the last annual meeting of the Association,
held at Cincinnati, O.
	HENRY B. BLACKWELL,
		Cor Sec, A. W. S. A. 
	ARTICLE I. The name of this Association
shall be the National-American Woman Suf-
frage Association.
	ARTICLE II. The object of this Associa-
tion shall be to secure protection in their right
to vote to the women citizens of the United
States by appropriate National and State legis-
lation.
	ARTICLE III. Section 1.—All citizens of
the United States subscribing to this Constitu-
tion and paying not less than one dollar
annually into the treasury of the Association,
shall become members thereof and shall be en-
titled to attend all its meetings, to participate
in all discussions that may arise, and to receive
reports and other documents published by it;
but such membership shall not carry with it
the right to vote.
	Section 2.—The payment of fifty dollars
($50) into the treasury of this Association
shall constitute any citizen of the United
States a life member of the Association with
all the privileges belonging to the regular
annual members.
	ARTICLE IV. 	Section 1.—The officers of
this Association shall be a president, a vice-
president at large, a recording secretary, a cor-
responding secretary, a treasurer, a chairman
of the executive committee and two auditors.
The officers named in this section shall be
nominated by an informal ballot at a busi-
ness session of the annual convention of the
Association. The three persons receiving the
highest number of votes for any office, shall
be considered the nominees of the convention
for that office, and the will of the Association
shall be taken for a formal ballot.
	Section 2.—Wherever State and Territorial
associations, auxiliary to the National Ameri-
can Association exist, the presidents of such
associations shall be considered vice-presidents
of the National-American Association, repre-
senting therein their respective States and Ter-
ritories.
	Section 3.—Each State or Territorial auxil-
iary association shall elect from its members-
ship one person to serve on the executive com-
mittee of their National-American Association.
	Section 4.—The president of the National-
American Woman Suffrage Association shall
appoint a vice president to represent on its
executive board, any State or Territory where
no State or Territorial Association exists auxil-
iary to the National-American Association.
	Section 5.—The officers enumerated in the
preceeding sections of this article shall con-
stitute the committee of this Association: of
these officers, fifteen shall constitute a quorum
for the transaction of business.
	Section 6.—The executive committee shall
elect annually from the veterans of our cause
ten or more honorary vice presidents.
	ARTICLE V. This Constitution may be
amended by a majority vote at any annual
meeting, notice of the amendment having
been given at any preceeding annual meeting.

author: Unknown
title: UTAH WOMAN SUFFRAGE ASSOCIA-
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol19 | 1890 | April | Issue: 19 | Title: UTAH WOMAN SUFFRAGE ASSOCIA- | Author: Unknown]

UTAH WOMAN SUFFRAGE ASSOCIA-
TION.

	Madame President and Members of the
Convention:—Since our last report a petition
was presented to the Legislative Assembly of
Utah asking that the Deseret University,which
admits male and female students, be put under
the control of male and female Regents, instead
of, as at present, twelve make Regents. The
petition was read and, like the petitions of our
revolutionary forefathers to the British
Parliament, laid on the table.
	There are auxiliary to the territorial
organization seventeen county organizations,
and they have many auxiliary branch organiza-
tions. The paid up auxiliary territorial
membership is over fifteen hundred (1,500.)
	There have been two territorial conventions
held in Salt Lake City, at which,in addition to
the general routine of business, reports have
been received, and such instructions given as


seemed requisite. The number of meetings
held in the branches not reported.
	In some parts of the territory, notable in
Sanpete, Millard, Utah and Davis Counties,
much zeal has been manifested.
	The cause of woman's wages, woman's moral,
social and political needs have been discussed;
also the best methods of helping woman to
rightly understand her present degree of help-
less dependence, and to intelligently assert her
selfhood in a manner that will enable her to
labor more effectively for the general good of
humanity.
	Classes in civil government have been
formed; the duties of city and county officers
have been considered; mock legislative
assemblies have been organized and bills pass-
ed. These lessons have been among the most
instructive of our general exercises.
	In July, the Territorial Woman's Suffrage
Association gave a lawn fete as an Inde-
pendence Celebration, attended by over four
hundred, at which the principles of the suffrage
cause were presented from a political and
scriptural standpoint. In the same month we
held a grove Jubilee in honor of the admission
of our neighboring State, Wyoming, with her
model constitution guaranteeing equal political
privileges to men and women.
	The cruel enactment of our Government
which deprived the women of Utah, without
trial, of their vested and treasured right of
franchise, we understand to be a grievous
wrong, which calls on the administrators of
justice in our nation for redress.
	Our mountain vales are not free from
obstructions; but one by one the advancing
waves of better thought silences them,and once
timid friends of our cause come more and more
to the front.
	Women workers in the suffrage cause in
various parts of the Territory report that they
find many clear brained men who are helpful to
them in various ways. They give their names
and pay their dues,adding words of encourage-
ment, and wishing us success in the good cause
of women's full enfranchisement.
	Our publishing committee have issued a
collection of songs, which help to make our
meetings varied and interesting.
	We watch with interest the reports of the
labors of our Sister Suffragists abroad and they
have our sympathy in all their laudable efforts
in helping to raise the standard of equal
human rights.

author: Unknown
title: PROGRESS OF WOMAN’S RIGHTS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol2 | 1873 | July | Issue: 4 | Title: PROGRESS OF WOMAN’S RIGHTS. | Author: Unknown]

PROGRESS OF WOMAN’S RIGHTS.

  Miss Anna Nichols, of Massachusetts, has
recently been appointed assistant examiner
of the Patent office. The lady has for some
time very creditably fulfilled the duties of
clerk; and on the occasion of some vacancies
in the examinerships, she was one of several
ladies who competed for places.   All of the
candidates were subjected to general scien-
tific examination as to their capabilities for
the position, and four ladies  passed the
ordeal with much credit.  The Commis-
sioner, however, concluded to appoint only
one of them for the present, as a sort of
experiment.
  There are few duties connected with the
operation of the Patent Office but may be
efficiently performed by intelligent women
It is all indoor work, mostly of a fixed,
clerical nature, for which petticoats are
admirably adapted; and if the Commis-
sioner would make a more general use of
them, he would set free a large number of
pataloons to be usefully employed in develop-
ing the more direct outdoor industries of
the country, for which men -are, by nature,
so especially prepared.
   At Canandaigua, in this State, Miss Susan
B. Anthony, who insisted that she had as
good-a right to vote as any other man, and
who did vote at the last election, has been
tried, and, we regret to say, found guilty,
and fined for violating the law.   Judge Hunt
decided that, although women were entitled
to the general rights of citizens, there were
certain special privileges which the law of
New York, as it stands, did not give them,
one of which was the privilege of voting.
The law must, in the opinion of Judge
Hunt, be changed before our feminine fel-
low citizens can enjoy themselves at the
ballot box.
    In the meantime, the Commissioner hav-
ing wisely decided in favor of the eligi-
bility of women as patent examiners, we
shall hope to see his decision sustained and
ratified by the appointment of Miss Anthony
as his successor when he shall retire—and
that day we understand, is not far distant.
The lady in question is a female steamboat,
so far as untiring energyand useful capacity
are concerned.   She is, undoubtedly, com-
petent to manage a dozen or two sleepy
institutions like the Patent Office. We
nominate, for Commissioner of Patents, Miss
Susan B. Anthony, of New York, and Miss
Anna Nichols, for Assistant Commissioner.
— Scientific American,” July 5th.

author: Unknown
title: AND OTHER PLACES.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol8 | 1879 | February | Issue: 17 | Title: AND OTHER PLACES. | Author: Unknown]

AND OTHER PLACES.

To the Honorable Council and House of Rep-
	resentatives of the Legislative Assembly of
	the Territory of Utah:

	Your petitioners, residents of the Territory,
of Utah, would respectfully show unto your
Honorable Body, that by an act of the Legis-
lative Assembly of said Territory, approved
Feb. 12, 1870, the right of suffrage was con-
ferred upon the women of this Territory, but
eligibility to hold office was withheld. That
if it was the intention of the lawmakers of
this Territory to confer upon the women of the
Territory equal political rights with the men
thereof, said act falls short of the intention.
That the women of the Territory, as a class of
voters are discriminated against—they have
the shadow of citizenship, without the sub-
stance. That your petitioners consider that
the right of suffrage and the right to hold office
should be inseparable.
	Your petitioners, therefore, respectfully ask
that your Honorable Body, at the session
thereof for the year A. D. 1880, enact a law
conferring upon the women of the Territory of
Utah, who are qualified voters, eligibility to
hold office.
	And your petitioners will ever pray, &c.

author: PUBLISHER WOMAN’S EXPONENT,
title: QUEEN ISABELLA.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol18 | 1889 | November | Issue: 11 | Title: QUEEN ISABELLA. | Author: PUBLISHER WOMAN’S EXPONENT,]

QUEEN ISABELLA.
			————
	If asking will secure it, women and their
work will have abundant representation at the
observance of the four hundredth anniversary
of the landing of Columbus.
	The women of Chicago met Aug. 26, and
appointed Mrs. Myra Bradwell and Mrs. M. M.
M. Wallace a committee to consult with the
national committee to ask for official recogni-
tion as an auxiliary. This action was approved,
and the Chicago Woman’s Department has
been incorporated.
	A little later Miss Susan B. Anthony made
a similar application to the mayor of New 
York. Now the Woman's Industrial Associa-
tion of Washington D. C., puts in its claim.
No doubt some recognition will be made of
women and their work. But whether it be
adequate and fitting or not, women have only
to "bide their time" to receive all that belongs
to them. It has taken four hundred years to
find out that Columbus was worthy of a
world's celebration. Women are only coming
above the horizon. When they are in full
sight, and secure in the possession of equal
human rights, it will be their own fault if
Queen Isabella, the Pilgrim and Puritan
mothers, the women of Revolutionary times
and the noble women all along the line do not
have their proper place wherever common
interests are represented. To this end let
women unite to secure their right to vote. The
power that resides in the ballot is the one
strong lever by which to secure equal rights.
To-day women are helpless for lack of the
ballot. We cannot command what we ought
to have, and must take what is offered.
							L. S.
	Woman's Journal'


Celia Logan is editor of Belford's Maga-
zine.

author: Unknown
title: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TIMES.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | July | Issue: 1 | Title: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TIMES. | Author: Unknown]

SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TIMES.

	Wherever the ballot has been given to woman, 
whether for special purposes or equal suffrage, the
best results possible have been obtained, and 
opponents to woman suffrage are unable to point
out disastrous consequences, because of woman’s
vote. It is without doubt a time in the history of 
nations, when woman’s voice, her influence and 
power is absolutely necessary, to accomplish the 
grand achievements and reformations, morally and 
politically that are on the tapis. The time is fast 
approaching when men will find woman’s help
necessary and they will undoubtedly ask for it,
and then let woman be prepared to maintain the
position, and advantages that will be offered un-
derstandingly. So far all along the way of pro-
gress she has had to contend for her just rights,
and demand recognition, but it will not always be 
so; men will see that it will be for their own good
and the uplifting of humanity to acknowledge
woman’s equality, and even already they are 
slowly awaking to this supreme fact, and eventu-
ally they will reach out the “olive branch of
peace,” to woman and whenever this transpires, 
as it surely will, we trust woman’s intuition will
lead her to accept gracefully the proffered favor,
[Column 2]
even though she may think it only her rightful
heritage.
	The sleep that has fallen upon the race, women 
as well as men, has been so deep and ab-
sorbing that they only awaken as from a night-
mare, alarmed at the growth of ideas and it will 
take time to bring them out of the stupor back
to their primeval condition. Some have awakened 
early in the morning of these changes, and these
are the pioneer workers men and women bringing
to pass the revolution in politics and morality,
that is to redeem society from the evils under
which it has been laboring so long. 
	Co-operation will be essential, the united and
organized effort of both men and women. This
will be the lever that will move the great ob-
stacles out of the pathway of legitimate progress
in all that will tend to the advancement of the
state or the nation and the purification of the
home, and of society. Men and women do their
best work together, for as the Scriptures tell us
“Man is not without the woman nor the woman
without the man in the Lord.” Neither can one
do any important work without the other, the 
physical and the spiritual forces must both har-
monize and be properly adjusted, if any great
struggle is on hand.
	Our readers will remember in this connection 
the story of the deliverance of Israel by Borak
when he refused to go to battle without Deborah
and said unto her “If thou wilt go with me, then I
will go; but if thou wilt not go with me, then I
will not go.”
	‘‘And Deborah went up with him.” All who
read the Bible know the result of this enterprise
and how they praised the Lord in song afterwards
and it was called “the song of Deborah and
Barak.”
	It is true the argument is brought forward that
this was an exceptional case, and that Barak was 
a coward to want a woman to go up with him to 
battle, but she prophesied unto him nevertheless,
and the Lord inspired her to do this great work;
in the 7th, verse of the 5th chapter of Judges it
reads “The inhabitants of the villages ceased,
they ceased in Israel, until that I Deborah arose
that I arose a mother in Israel.”
	Who does not know at a later time the history 
of Joan of Arc?
	And yet men in this nineteenth century bring 
up the paltry plea against woman suffrage, that
they are the protectors of women.
	In thousands of instances men need women to
protect them, and prevent them from committing
grievous wrongs?
	It is the restraining influence from evil that
women possess that protects men and it is an ele-
ment that is needful in political as well as
social life. It is not necessary to go into details to 
prove this fact, the proofs are all around if one is
not blind to see.
	As to the significance of the times when in
twenty-six states of this union women are allowed
to vote on some questions, and almost every day
brings news of some victory for woman in the 
direction of progress, what may not be expected
and what will be the ultimatim? It will be
equality before the law, and that will bring about 
many radical changes for the better, and woman’s
place will be by the side of man, his counselor,
his helpmeet, his companion, as much in the
political as in the social world.
	The day is hastening on, and although the
women of Utah may deem the time afar off be-
cause of the peculiar conditions existing here,
yet in the time when “a nation shall be born in a
day” what may not transpire in other respects?
There is something important expected of woman
in this day, or she would not have wakened to
the conflict and felt the need of pushing forward
to the issue, as it is manifest she has done. So
we conclude there is special work for women, and
their best efforts must be brought into exercise to
[Column 3]
accomplish it. This spirit to educate the masses
to the idea that a few have received, as it were by
revelation, is fast gaining ground; slowly and 
surely the seed is being sown and the harvest
will be a surprise to those of little faith.
	Meantime women should work diligently, and
silently if necessary, to prepare themselves for
the issues at hand whether local or national, and
seek to comprehend the great questions that are
agitating the political world. Many women are 
ignorant of the simplest laws relating to their own 
individual rights and the rights of property.
Somebody brings them a paper to sign asks them
for their signature, and even without reading the
article or agreement and regardless of the conse-
quences the pen is taken and the name written,
when perhaps by simply witholding the signature,
the most serious consequences might have been 
averted.
	This is not an idle tale but a stern reality, a 
stubborn fact. Women do not look into these
matters as they should, the writer knows of an 
instance where the husband and father of a family
when on “a drunken spree” sold the cow one day
and the next day the home; the wife was sitting
milking the cow when the man who had bought
her came to take her away and she was utterly 
powerless to prevent it; the next day, men came 
to tell her that she must leave the house, they 
had bought and paid for it, she remonstrated, but
to no purpose, and before that man (husband and 
father) had come to his senses again, the wife and 
children were homeless; in the state where this 
circumstance transpired the law has been materi-
ally changed through the intercession and peti-
tioning of woman suffragists. And yet with
these facts staring them in the face, women will
say, they do not want the ballot, they are willing
to leave such matters in the hands of men.
Whenever we hear women reason thus, we know
that they are ignorant of the conditions of women
at home and abroad; such women should be edu-
cated if possible in that which pertains to the
social and moral well-being of womankind, and
they would then see the actual necessity that
exists for woman’s help in the great stride that is
being made to advance in the scale of human 
progression.

author: Unknown
title: WOMAN'S VICTORY.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol8 | 1879 | December | Issue: 13 | Title: WOMAN'S VICTORY. | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN'S VICTORY.

	An interesting convention of the Woman's
Suffrage Association was held in the Empire
State, at Poughkeepsie, Oct. 24, commencing
at 10 o'clock A.M. Able and eloquent speeches
were delivered by Matilda Joslyn Gage, Lillie
Devereaux Blake, Helen M. Slocum, Albert O.
Wilcox and Clara Neyman, One of the princi-
pal features of this convention was the protest
against the election of Lucius Robinson for
Governor of New York, and although the wo-
men citizens of that State have not a vote they
have a voice, and they protested with a will
against Robinson as Governor, not only because
he vetoed the woman's school-bill in 1877, but
by his frequent pardon of criminals guilty of
the grossest outrages against their sex, he made
himself specially obnoxious; and also because
"they were impressed with the fact that the
questions before the country are far larger, of
wider scope, and more difficult to deal with
than at any previous time in the history of this
nation; and that the New York campaign had
far reaching effects for national weal or woe."
	Immediately after the convention Pough-
keepsie, Mrs. Gage went down to New York
and aided in the Anti-Robinson campaign.
	Mrs. Susan A. King, Lillie D. Blake, Helen
M. Slocum and Dr. Clemence S. Lozier worked
faithfully to obtain Robinson's defeat. Wo-
men's Anti-Robinson meetings were held in
every part of the city of New York and suburb-
an towns.
	Thousands and tens of thousands of the wo-
men's printed protests were circulated, not
only in every part of the city of New York, but
all over the State.
	Besides having defeated Robinson, they have
got Cornell's promise to sign the woman's
school-bill vetoed by Robinson, and to sustain
the Woman's Presidential Suffrage Bill, which
will secure to women the right to vote for elec-
tion of President and Vice-President.


Altogether, we think the women of New
York have stormed the castle and forced an
entrance that will give them firmer foothold
hereafter.

author: Unknown
title: AMERICAN FORK W. S. A.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | May | Issue: 20 | Title: AMERICAN FORK W. S. A. | Author: Unknown]

AMERICAN FORK W. S. A.
	Dear Editor: If you will give us a little space
in the Utah woman's paper I will try and
give your readers a little knowledge how the
suffrage cause is prospering here. We have a
paid up membership of one hundred and eleven
members. We hold meetings the last Friday
in each month in the Meeting House, by kind-
ness of Bishop Halliday. Meetings are fairly
attended; we have a Civil Government class of
eighteen enrolled students. Visiting Suffrage
members who take no part in the exercises but
who are interested listeners, three to eight.
Our class is becoming very interesting, and
much honor is due Mrs. Ellen Jakeman of
Provo, who kindly offered to instruct these
classes. We also petitioned the Am. Fork City
Council for the use of City Hall for W. S. civil
government class. Our petition was granted
unanimously. Mayor Cunningham instructed
the Sheriff to have a fire made in the Hall
and have it lighted every Monday evening for
the use of the Am. Fork W. S. civil govern-
ment class. Now we feel very proud and ap-
preciate the magnanimity of such men and it is
for such men we would vote to keep in office
who can accord to women such privileges; surely
the movement for equal rights of women is
advancing steadily. We have also made some
praiseworthy appropriations to the amount of
seventy-five dollars; we have a small bank
account of sixty dollars, and in the Treasury
nine dollars for incidental expenses we have
been very successful socially and financially
in giving entertainments; the association has 
property on hand to the amount of a little
over thirty dollars. I will honorably mention
our national flag, twelve feet long, with golden
star for Wyoming upon it, and as each State
gives to woman her emancipation we shall
make the stars golden, until the blue field
shall be covered with the grand galaxy of
golden States. We have the life size pictures
of E. C. Stanton, Susan B. Anthony and the
Susan B. Anthony of Utah, Sarah M. Kimball,
all in gilt frames, with other paraphernalia
for suffrage occasions. We have international
council of women for 1888 and 1891 which we
loan to members on time. We have fifty-one
subscribers to WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Woman's


Tribune. Women's Column, and Woman's
Business Journal. Also in looking over the tax
list we find in Am. Fork precinct one hundred
and nineteen Women who own property to the
amount of sixty-eight thousand dollars on
which they pay taxes, this is taxation and no
representation. Praying that women will
never quit nor get weary in the suffrage cause
until they are both heard and possess the great-
est boon ever given to humanity—the ballot.
	Yours for suffrage.
		HANNAH S. LAPISH, President.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


[Column 1]

author: Unknown
title: WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE IN UTAH.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol15 | 1886 | July | Issue: 4 | Title: WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE IN UTAH. | Author: Unknown]

	WOMAN'S SUFFRAGE IN UTAH.

	Mr. Edmunds presented the following peti-
tion of Mrs. Angie F. Newman:

To the honorable the Senate and House of Rep-
	resentatives of the Forty-ninth Congress:

	GENTLEMEN: The undersigned respectfully
represents that on the 6th of March, 1886, 2,-
000 Mormon women assembled in mass meeting
in Salt Lake City to protest against the inva-
sion of their rights by Federal interference.
The spirit of protest crystalized in the appoint-
ment of a committee to memorialize the Presi-
dent and Congress of the United States in the
form of a "Declaration of grievances" of the
people of Utah.
	Said committee drafted the memorial, pro-
ceeded to Washington. and presented it to the
national Congress.
	It has been read in your hearing.
	The following preamble and resolutions are
embodied therein: 
	Whereas, the rights and liberties of women are placed
in jeopardy by the present cruel and inhuman proceedings
in the Utah courts, and in the contemplated measure in
Congress to deprive the women voters in Utah of the elec-
tive franchise: Therefore, be it
	Resolved by the women of Utah in mass meeting assem-
bled, That the suffrage originally conferred upon us as a 
political privilege has become a vested right by possession
and usage for fifteen years, and that we protest against be-
ing deprived of that right without process of law, and for
no other reason  than that we do not vote to suit our politi-
cal opponents.

	These resolutions are the expression of Mor-
mon sentiment.
	There are Gentile women in Utah Territory.
On this basis of civil equality their "protest"
should have equal consideration from your
honorable body.
	As an eye witness of the ceremonies at the
Theatre meeting; as a long and close observer
of the effect of female suffrage in Utah; having
definite personal knowledge of the Gentile
sentiment touching this question; as a member
of the committee who drafted and circulated
the petitions (mentioned in the following) ask-
ing the repeal of the act conferring the elective
franchise upon the women of Utah, the under-
signed, in defense of the great principles of
civil equity, respectfully submits the following
memorial, asking the passage of section 7 of the
Edmunds bill, now pending in the national
Congress.
	All law, human or divine, is the exponent
of sovereignty.
	The duty of individual or national recog-
nition of or obedience to law is measured by
the nature of that sovereignty.
	Divine law is unconditional. From it there
is no appeal.
	Human law may be only a synonym of des-
potism; the arbitrary edicts of rulers the very
antonyms of justice. The very elements of
revolution sometimes inhere in civil enact-
ments.
	Under a republican form of government the
people are the sovereignty. When, therefore,
the will of the people crystallizes at the bal-
lot box its mandate is supreme.
	Liberty's urn is a safe repository of law.
	Section 7 of the Edmunds bill, now in the 
hands of the House Judiciary, Committee
reads:

	It shall not be lawful for any female to vote at any elec-
tion hereafter held in the Territory of Utah for any public
purpose whatsoever, and no such vote shall be received or
counted or given effect, and any and every act of the 
governor and legislative assembly of the Territory of Utah
providing for or allowing the registration or voting by fe-
males is hereby annulled.

	This attempt to repeal the Territorial act
which confers the franchise upon the women
of the Territory of Utah has been interpreted

[Column 2]
by its opponents as a subversion of the great
principles of civil equality—as retrogresion
in politics—as formulating civil disabilities on
the basis of religious belief.
	This construction, however, is a perversion
of facts, and can only be entertained by those
who have studied the problem outside the
Territory. The test of given principles, under
a disparity of conditions, must yield unex-
pected results. The civil polity of Utah Terri-
tory is unrepublican. It was framed by a 
people anti-American in sentiment and hostile
in purpose;a people unfamiliar with the genius,
of republican institutions; fresh from the des-
potisms of the Old World; saturated with its
spirit; whose initial and final intent is the es-
tablishment of a theocracy within the Repub-
lic; who, claiming its immunities while ignor-
ing its supremacy, borrow only so much of
its constitutional forms as best conserve their
independence; who locating the "kingdom to
come" in the fastnesses of the mountains, ce-
ment its foundation stones with an ecclesias-
ticism unknown in the annals of history.
Thrones and shrines were builded, crowns worn,
and oblations offered, ere the eye of the nation
caught the smoke of its incense. 
	Railroads are great civilizers. The snort of
the engine is the bugle note of progress. In
1869 the Union Pacific laid its last cable.
The tramp of the on-coming millions began
to echo along the canyons. A new civllization
began to creep into the valleys. The Moun-
tains of Utah, with their threads of silver
and dust of gold, called with a hundred
tongues "Come," and they came—loyal men,
men fresh from the victory of battle, from the
sacred atmosphere of Christian homes. They
came to stay.
	The pulse of national life began to be felt
in Zion. The hierarchy must resist its throb.
The national Government had blundered in
making its chief governor of the Territory, a 
man whose dictum in civil or ecclesiasti-
cal circles was absolute, whose slightest com-
mand was emphasized by a "Thus saith the
Lord" With intuitions which amounted to
prescience, this dictator foreshadowed the dis-
solution of church rule in civil administration
through the influx of Gentile residents and
the ballot in the hands of American citizens.
Diplomatic in action, resourceful in methods,
holding the keys of power, spiritual and tem-
poral, this magnetic brain evolved defensive
measures which were executed with celerity
and precision.
	The most audacious of these measures was
the proscription placed upon the mining inter-
ests. As often as the Gentiles opened the
mines, as often as Brigham Young went out
with a band of benighted dupes, and
under the light of the stars closed the mines
with imposing ceremonies and pronounced ana-
themas upon the adventurers. This failing, the
"shot-gun policy" was adopted. The com-
manding general at Fort Douglas was obliged 
to detail soldiers to protect the men engaged
in working the mines, for several successive
seasons. Still the miners multiplied.
	In October, 1869, Mr. E. L. T. Harrison, edi-
tor of the Utah (Mormon) Magazine, ventured
to publish an article advocating the mining in-
terests. Immediately the recalcitrant editor,
together with the publisher, Mr. W. S. Godbe,
who had endorsed the article, were summoned
before the high council to answer for violation
of the decrees of the infallible priesthood
George Q. Cannon conducted the trial. To
the test questions, "Do you believe President
Young has the right to dictate to you in all
things, temporal and spiritual?" a negative an-
swer was returned, for which both gentlemen
were excommunicated from the church. By
virtue of the same high authority the legisla-
ture passed laws taxing heavily the products
of the miners. Nothing daunted, capitalists

[Column 3]
and prospectors multiplied. The wily de-
ceiver then evoluted from his narrow soul the
magnanimous scheme of enfranchising the
women. The Mormon legislature passed the
bill. The Gentile miners were mostly un-
married men, or had left their families in the
East. Every Mormon citizen thus had
his civil power extended in correspondence
with his numerous alliances.
	Thus a man with half a dozen wives would
now have half a dozen votes besides his own.
"Brigham Young would have nineteen for his
nineteen wives, to say nothing of his daughters,
and the whole army of spiritual wives he might
produce."
	To the intelligent discussion of the justice
of this franchise it is necessary (1) to examine
the statute on which it is based: (2) to enquire
who were the law-making power; (3) to 
determine if statute itself is in harmony with
organic law.
	The act conferring upon women the elective
franchise, approved February 12, 1870, reads:

	Every woman of the age of twenty-one years, who has
resided in this Territory six months next preceding any
general or special election, born or naturalized in the United 
States, or who is the wife, widow, or the daughter of a
native-born or naturalized citizen of the United States,
shall be entitled to vote at any election in this Territory.

	Class legislation is hereby established, the
fundamental principle of republicanism vio-
lated, and the statute thereby made void.
	The qualifications of the male voter as fixed
by the Territorial legislature are as follows:
	First. If native born, he shall be six
months resident in the Territory. If foreign
born, he shall be a naturalized citizen, five
years resident in the country. He shall be
twenty-one years of age. He shall be a tax-
payer.
	No qualifications are defined for the female
voter except such as are overruled by the dis-
junctive "or"—"or who is the wife, widow, or 
daughter of a native-born or naturalized
citizen."
	It would puzzle an astute registrar of elec-
tions to discover who is thereby debarred the
exercise of the franchise.
	The woman or girl who is not the wife,
widow, or daughter of somebody might justly
be denominated "anomalous."  The naturali-
 zation provision was of no weight, as proven
in the immediate results which followed the
passage of the Edmunds bill. Under its regis-
tration clause aliens are required to produce
their papers of citizenship. In the two weeks,
preceding the first election thereafter, two
thousand (2,000)aliens most of whom had
habitually voted their wives and daughters as
well, were naturalized in the two district courts
of Ogden and Salt Lake City. The inequali-
ty of the statute and its license of power is
seen in its application to emigrants. The
trains freighted with recruits for Zion, which
arrived a day or two preceding elections, were
met at the depot by church officials. The
emigrants were taken to the tithing head-
quarters. Women and girls were then and
there selected by aspirants for sealing orders,
taken to the endowment house, and the mar-
riage ceremony performed. On the morrow
these wives of "native-born or naturalized
citizens," wholly ignorant of our language or
laws, or the significance of the franchise, with
the odor of the emigrant ship [-]till upon their
clothing, were full-fleged voters, and deposited
their ballots in full conformity with the statu-
tory requirements. Furthermore, the commit-
tees of the Emigration Fund Company desig-
nated the localities where newcomers should
find homes.
	In case of a doubtful election at a given
point, re-enforcements were dispatched thither
from the new colony. Mrs. Fanny Stenhouse, who
is the most gifted woman over identified with the


[Column 1]
Mormon church, whose statements are as re-
liable as truth itself, enunciates the spirit of 
this franchise act in these words: 
	I have often seen one solitary man driving into the city
a whole wagon load of women of all ages and sizes. They 
were going to the polls and their vote would be one. 
Many have voted two or three times. Men christened
their mules, conferring upon them the names of men, and
made them vote also. 
	"Again and again has it been charged in the
public prints of Utah, by eye witnesses, and 
unrefuted by the Mormon church, that the
genealogies of the dead, the unborn, and the
deceased had been represented at the ballot-
box. 
	"It is easy to see," says Mrs. Stenhouse, (by these meth-ods) "how the influence of the priesthood has been exerted
and the women themselves have been made the instruments
for riveting still more firmly their own fetters." 
	Judge Emerson, of Utah, in 1882, in a test
case said: 
	All regulations upon the subject (elective franchise) must
be uniforn and impartial. Any provisions which should
impose upon a particular class of citizens conditions and
requirements not required of all others is void. 
	Judge Twiss, of the same court, says: 
	Any construction of the statutes that would give one
class of persons the right of voting upon easier and differ-
ent terms than those given to another class would make an
inequality in the laws odious to every sense of justice and
every principle of republican government. Such statutes
would be monstrous and an offense to a government of
equal and just laws. 
	Yet here we find unnaturalized, non-tax-pay-
ing, minor females using the ballot. If the
daughter of a naturalized (?) citizen may vote, 
why not the son? If a minor, non tax-paying
girl may vote, why not the boy? Thus it is
seen that the daughter votes on the basis of
the eligibility of the father! Such is the
nature of the statute, under any just construc-
tion, void at the beginning. 
	We have seen that the "free and independ-
ent State of Desert" had been organized in
1850. During the lifetime of Brigham Young,
"on the day succeeding the adjournment of
the Territorial legislature, he, in the character
of governor of Desert, convened the same
body as a State legislature, and the business
of the State was then and there transacted."—
[Madame La Tour p. 344.] This State legis-
lature sat in judgment on the acts of the Ter-
ritorial legislature, confirming or repealing, as
the interests of the Mormon church dictated. 
This action was final. Nearly every member
of this Territorial or "State" legislature was a 
Mormon high priest, and was the possessor
of from two to seven wives. 
	Brigham Young had nineteen wives and an
indefinite list of daughters. 
	Such was the law-making power! 
	The postulate is easily sustained, that the
elective franchise was never legally conferred
upon the women of Utah. They were never
eufranchised by the Federal Government, or 
by any branch of it, or by its representatives. 
They were enfranchised by a church organiza-
tion in which the authority of the Federal
Government was not recognized even as a
factor. They were enfranchised for church
ends alone—to make the church impregnable
to outward attack, independent and hostile in
civil attitude. 
	To recognize the act is to recognize the su-
premacy of the church in civil administration. 
As proof we quote the following from Bishop
Lunt, of the Mormon Church, as published in
the San Francisco Chronicle: 
	Our vote is solid and will always remain so. It will be
thrown where the most good will be accomplished for the
church. The women of Utah vote, and they never desert
the colors of the Church; they vote for the tried friends of
the church. You can imagine the results which wisdom 
may bring about, with the assistance of a church organiza-

[Column 2]
tion like ours.  In some great political crisis the two pres-
ent political parties will bid for our support. Utah will be
admitted as a polygamous State, and the other Territories
we have peacefully subju[---]ed will be admitted also. We
will then hold the balance of power, and will dictate to the
country. We possess the ability to turn the political scale
in any particular community we desire. 
	This is the real animus of the movement.
This theory is made self-evident in the fact
that the same body of men who passed the
act of 1870 legislated away the right of dower
—an infamous scheme to give the husband the
leverage of power over the first wife. If she
refuses to give the husband a second, he in re-
taliation may will the property to the second, 
or the tenth, and the first wife be left penniless. 
	Oh! boasted magnanimity of the Mormon
hierarchy touching the civil status of woman! 
"Oh! Liberty, what deeds are done in thy
name!"
	Again, the Territories are the nation's de-
pendencies. We may term the States the na-
tion's children, the Territories are its minors 
still under subjection. The Territories have
no acquired sovereignty. 
	The organic act by which a Territorial gov-
ernment for Utah is established is the national
character of organization. The powers of the
legislative assembly are strictly defined and
limited by said act. Section 6 reads: 
	The legislative power of said Territory shall extend to
all rightful subjects of legislation consistent with the Con-
stitution of the United States and the provisions of this act,
All the laws passed by the legislative assembly and governor
snal be submitted to the Congress of the United States, 
and if disapproved shall be null and of no effect. 
	Section 5 limits the rights of citizenship to
"the free white male inhabitant," and confers
upon the legislative assembly the right of de-
fining the qualifications of voters, "provided,
the right of suffrage shall be exercised only
by citizens of the United States." 
	Therefore, the enfranchisement of the women
of Utah is in contravention of the organic act,
and is a repudiation of national supremacy. 
It has been so interpreted by the highest legal 
authority of the Territory. After the passage
of the Edmunds bill it was held by the judges
of the courts that registration could be denied
women on the basis of the invalidity of the
Territorial statute. The Utah Commission
were asked to so declare. But while listening
to the argument the Commission declined to
act. 
	Under these conditions, to construe the re-
peal of said statute as a violation of "vested
rights," is to subordinate the national Govern-
ment to a Church corporation. But it is
claimed that justice is met in the provision of
the Edmunds bill of 1882, which disfranchises
polygamists. The Edmunds bill, in effect,
corrected the abuses of the ballot by requiring
registration, by defining the age and date of
residence.
	Polygamists were disfranchsed; then the re
lations could be established—then the Mormon 
conscience repudiated perjury—then evasion
could not be sustained. The Mormons sent
circulars to their people declaring that
daughters of eligible fathers and the first
wives and widows of polygamists were entitled
to vote. The Commission were compelled to
make definite ruling on each point. After the
test of years it is found that the cohesion of
the Mormou Church has not been invaded by
the execution of this law, nor its spirit of re-
sistance to national authority restrained. What
then?  Shall the United States compromise
with this quasi sovereignty under the specious
plea of "vested rights." 
	The franchise is first a privilege conferred. 
It becomes a "right" by possession. "It can-
not be taken away except by revolution." 
This is a truism in political science under gen-
eral conditions. The parties enfranchised,
holding within themselves, by their own ballot, 

[Column 3]
the power to retain said franchise. If the
law-makers are false to their constituency and
attempt forceful withdrawal,revolution follows,
and an election of representatives who will
sustain the constituency may be had. Eng-
land and America are constantly extending
suffrage to new classes. They can add to the
list, but cannot withdraw. Not because the
ballot is a primal right. It is a privilege con-
ferred. But in its exercise inheres the power
to perpetuate itself. This is true in all the
States of this Union by virtue of State
sovereignty. 	
	A city government, however, might grant
municipal suffrage to women and the next 
legislature could pass a law prohibiting the ex-
ercise of the franchise in municipalities. The
city's power of self government is delegated
power. Exactly such is the status of the Terri-
tory. Moreover, if Congress had endorsed the
action of the Territorial legislature, Congress
has power to repeal its own action within the
limits of the National Constitution. The
Constitution of the United States has never
been amended so as to put the ballot in the
hands of women. Herein truth is stranger
than fiction! 
	The next argument of the opponents of re-
peal is—
	If you disfranchise the women why not disfranchise the
men?
	An interrogation is not a reason. It is not logic.
It is subterfuge in absence of argument. The
disfranchisement of the men is not equally just. 
The men were enfranchised by National 
authority and in consonance with constitution-
al provision. The women by an alien church
organization, which renders no allegiance to
the Government. Is it political wisdom to dis-
franchise the men? 
	The administration of a Republican govern-
ment is in the hands of the people. The authori-
ty must rest somewhere. The responsibility must 
be met by somebody. The question is simply that
of the practicability of the dissolution of the
local government. The Gentile sentiment in 
Utah favors such dissolution. Gentile
voters are willing to be disfranchised, that
civil order may be elvolved out of civil chaos.
	The Gentile policy to effect such result is
that of a legislative commission. 
	There is but one serious argument against it
—the probably transfer of Mormon political
representation to adjacent Territories, where,
in such event, the battle must be fought again. 
The disfranchisement of the women makes
possible the admission of the Gentile civil ele-
ment on an equality with the Mormon element;
gives the loyal Gentile the civil status of the
disloyal Mormon. It is claimed that this has
been accomplished already in the disfranchise-
ment of the polygamists. I deny it. Mormonism
and polygamy are in collusion. Polygamists
control the elections. The priesthood select
the candidates, and no man is put upon the
ticket, either for legislative or municipal office, 
who is not pledged to support "the divine
order." A "scratched" ticket is unknown.
At least two polygamous wives are members of
the Territorial central committee. 
	Said Dr. Ellen Ferguson, one of the Mor-
mon memorialists, at the theatre meeting at
which the Congressional memorial was drafted: 
	The ballot in the hands of the Mormon women is a
mighty power, and our enemies know it. When we raise
our voices to protest, it will be well for the Government to
listen. 
	With this "mighty power" in local politics,
county and municipal officers are entirely
Mormon. 
	The Territorial legislature had its first Gen-
tile member this year, sent from Park City, and
a bill passed both houses to redistrict the Terri-
tory in order to avert such calamities in
future. 
	Laws controlling municipal elections are


[Column 1]
such that the aldermen are placed on the gener-
al list. Thus no Gentile ward can elect a 
Gentile member of the city council. 
	Withdraw this "mighty power" in elections—
of 12,000 female votes—and very soon county
and municipal Gentile officers could be elected
and Gentile representatives chosen for the
legislature, This would be the immediate re-
sult in Ogden. Is the need of such representa-
tion commensurate with the cost? Judge ye. 
Under the Utah statutes no sexual crime is 
punishable save "rape," which law was evident-
ly framed for the Gentiles. Chastity has no
protection. The Mormon legislature, sitting
since the organization of the Territory, has
never framed a statute touching incest, seduc-
tion, adultery, bigamy, or polygamy, save once. 
That was inadvertently done through a hasty
adoption of the California code, and it was re-
pealed at the next session. 
	The legislature of the past winter convened
during the excitement of the Hampton conspir-
acy. This conspiracy was a carefully con-
structed Mormon plot to blacken the character
of Gentile residents; to arrest and punish vic-
tims under assumed municipal (Mormon) au-
thority. For the execution of this dastardly
outrage, lewd women were imported, at Mor-
mon expense; their rooms furnished by the
Mormons, and arranged so that witnesses could 
be secreted. Scores of letters were addressed 
to Gentiles, inviting them to call on important 
business. The names of two hundred Gentiles
were put upon the "black list." The scheme
was thwarted by Judge Zane's ruling that
municipal courts had no jurisdiction. 
	Public sentiment, thus intensified, demanded
Territorial laws. The one Genlile member in-
troduced a bill which should punish any viola-
tor Mormon or Gentile. The legislature scarce-
ly wished to incur the odium of repudiation. 
They attempted to defeat the measure by in-
troducing a disgraceful substitute which pun-
ished "unmarried men who should debauch un-
married women."
	A bill passed both houses, which amended
the Ogden charter by delegating power to 
the municipality to punish sexual crimes un-
touched by general statute. This was the ini-
tial step towards granting to all incorporated
cities the power to deal with such offenses. 
Why? If the legislature enacted such statutes, 
Mormons as well as Gentiles would be subject
to arrest. The city officials.are Mormon. If 
cities had exclusive jurisdiction, Gentiles alone
would be made to feel the penalty of violation. 
Moreover, under the Mormon Church regime
Mormons are amenable only to church rule; 
are tried in ecclesiastical courts, and sentenced
by the bishops to suffer church, not civil, penal-
ties. Governor Murray, always loyal and
heroic in action in vetoing the Ogden charter
bill, used this language: 
	While lust can blight the whole Territory without finding
any rebuke at the hands of the legislature, the attempt to 
confer power on a municipal corporation to punish local
transgressions against morality in the indefinite, inadequate, 
and inoperative way done in this bill cannot have my sanc-
tion. 
	Again, polygamy, even under United States
law, is rarely punished, as convictions are only
obtained under the "unlawful cohabitation" 
clause, with Judge Zane's ruling that "holding
out to the world as wives" constitutes the of-
fense. Why? The Utah statutes are silent on 
the subject of marriage. No marriage licenses, 
no certificates of marriage, no civil records of
marriage, are required. Marriage ceremonies
are performed in the endowment house, wit-
nessed only by Mormons. The statute of limi-
tation cuts off prosecution in three years, i. e.,
it is a crime to live in polygamy the first 
three years; it is a virtue thereafter. Laws for 
the supression of sexual crimes can only be
enacted when Gentile representation predomin-
ates in the legislature,and can only be executed 

[Column 2]
when the city and county officials are Ameri-
can citizens, not alien theorists. It is urged 
that the ballot of the Gentile women is as po-
tent as that of the Mormon women. The cen-
ters of Gentile representation are the mining
districts. Most of the miners are unmarried
men, or men who have left their families in the
East. The vote of the Mormon women nulli-
fies Gentile political power in these districts. 
Gentile women rarely vote in any part of the 
Territory. First, because their ballot signifies
nothing; it is labor lost. Second, the general
exercise of the ballot in the Territory is such
a travesty upon all that is sacred to woman
that Christian motherhood refuses to be com-
promised  by  recognition of the nefarious
scheme. Where, then, is the argument against
the passage of section 7 of the pending Ed-
munds bill? In the adverse sentiment of the
advocates of universal suffrage in the States? 
The sentiment of even this class of distinguished
women is not altogether adverse. "The Wo-
man's Home Missionary Society" of the Me-
thodist Episcopal Church, of which Mrs. 
Rutherford B. Hayes is the president, with
such ladies as Mrs. Governor Foraker, of 
Ohio, Mrs. Bishops Walden, Warren, Wiley, 
and Andrews, and many others of recognized
ability and social power, members of its execu-
tive committee, at its national session in Oct., 
1883, passed, without a dissenting voice, the
following resolution:
	Resolved, That a committee be appointed to confer with
the civil and religious authorities at Salt Lake City, and
make careful investigation of the relation of the ballot in 
the hands of the women of Utah to Territorial legislation, 
to Congressional representation at the national capital, and,
above all, to its power to perpetuate the religious bondage
and domestic slavery of the women so enfranchised: and if 
they shall find the facts to warrant, they shall draft peti-
tions to be circulated for signatures, which shall be pre-
sented to the Congressional committee at Washington, ask-
ing for the disfranchisement of the women of Utah, or for
a radical change of the whole form of government. 
	In pursuance of the resolution, the following
committee was appointed: Rev. T. C. Iliff. su-
perintendent of Methodist missions in Utah; 
Judge Boreman of the Lalt Lake bar; Mrs. 
Paddock, of Salt Lake, author of many books; 
and Mrs. Angie F. Newman, of Nebraska. 
Mrs. Newman went immediately to Utah. The
committee then faithfully complied with every
specification of the resolution as to conference
and investigation. In harmony with the judg-
ment of Federal officers and ecclesiastical au-
thorities, the petition was drafted by the com-
mittee and circulated by the W. H. M. S.
	Just as the petition emerged from the press
in Salt Lake City, a dinner was given to Rev. 
Joseph Cook, of Boston, at the house of Mrs. 
Paddock, of the committee, at which were
present Federal and ecclesiastical authorities
who had endorsed the movement. Its scope
and purpose were the theme of discussion. 
Mrs. Newman offered the distinguished guest a 
copy of the petition. Upon examination he
exclaimed, "Excellent, excellent! I will sign it
and inclose it in a letter to Senator Edmunds."
Such is the estimate of those who have studied
the Mormon problem within the Territory. 
Individual and official signatures of organized
bodies of Christian women were obtained, re-
presenting 250,000 women in the States. 
	On the 16th of June, 1884, these petitions
were read in the United States Senate by Sena-
tor Hoar. Congress adjourned before the bill 
passed the House. Many of these 250,000 
women have been champions of equal suffrage
from the rostrum and in the press for many
years.
	Such is enlightened Christian sentiment—
sentiment which holds the sanctity of Christian
marriage and the protection of virtue as at
least co-equal with civil liberty. It is, we be-
lieve, only a misapprehension of the fact terse-
ly expressed in convention by the lamented 

[Column 3]
Bishop Wiley, "that woman suffrage in Utah
only means woman's suffering," which has led
any true woman to advocate its sustentation. 
The franchise conferred by a theocracy, exer-
cised under its dominance, made to conserve its
power, to perpetuate polygamous life, and to 
clothe lawlessness with authority, is not the
franchise of a Christian republic. Let the
act be repealed in the grant of a legislative
commission, otherwise in the passage of sec-
tion 7 of the Edmunds bill, for righteousness' 
sake, for humanity's sake, and in defense of 
liberty protected by law, the only liberty un-
der the flag. 	MRS. ANGIE F. NEWMAN. 
	S. Mis. 122—2.

author: Unknown
title: SUMMARY.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol2 | 1873 | November | Issue: 11 | Title: SUMMARY. | Author: Unknown]

			SUMMARY.

	The American Woman Suffrage Association
held a very successful Anniversary and An-
nual meeting in New York and Brooklyn, Oct.
13th and 14th. The Convention was large. The
proceedings were spirited and harmonious.
Great enthusiasm prevailed. Col. T. Went-
worth Higginson presided and made the open-
ing address. Letters endorsing the movement,
were read from Hon. George Wm. Curtis, Wm.
Lloyd Garrison, Lydia Maria Child, Elizabeth
Stuart Phelps, Louisa M. Alcot and others.
Eighty-six delegates were present, representing
organized societies in fourteen States and Ter-
ritories. The Annual Report shows that Woman
Suffrage is established and works well in Wyo-
ming and Utah, that it was under discussion
last winter, in 21 State Legislatures, and received
a majority vote in those of Maine, Iowa and
Michigan. Written reports were read from 17
States. Julia Ward Howe was elected Presi-
dent for the ensuing year; Lucy Stone, Chair-
man Executive Committee. Among the Vice
Presidents at Large are Vice President Henry
Wilson. Hon. George Wm. Curtis, Senator Sar-
geant, Wm. Lloyd Garrison and Col. Higgin-
son. The Resolutions adopted are as follows.

author: Unknown
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | November | Issue: 8 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: Unknown]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	The Woman's Voice, in an editorial on the
women voters of Boston, says that from
present indications the woman's vote will
amount to at least 10,000 and will constitute
the balance of power in the coming school 
election.
	
	The ladies upon the Missouri State Board
of Commissioners of the World's Fair have
asked for $25,000 of the appropriation made
by the Legislature, to be applied in the
women's departments.  The supposition is that
the request will be granted, as the Com-
missioners favor the propisition.

	Ibsen is said to be contemplating a visit to
his native home, and will remain six muoths, 
either in the northern part of Scandinavia or
at a Danish watering place.  It is scarcely ex-
pected that he will return to Munich, but
that, on his return to Germany, he will take
up his residence in Dresden.

	Probably most people wonld be surprised to
learn that there is a spot in Africa where
women have even limited suffrage, but
under the government of the Cape of
Good Hope, which rules several hundred
thousand square miles of territory, women
have municipal suffrage.—Boston Daily
Journal.

	The spot which was selected for Lowell's
grave, and which, it is understood, was of his
own choosing, is under two large horn-beam
trees one of which is directly at the foot of
the grave and the other on the right of its head.
These trees are never trimmed, and their
cheif charm is natural and weird growth.
The lot is in a valley in the rear part of Mt.
Auburn cemetery, directly in the shadow of
the Longfellow lot, on Indian Ridge avenue.
Thus the two poets, who were neighbors in
life, may be said to occupy the same relation
in death.

author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT
title: DEATH OF MISS CARPENTER. — Miss
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | September | Issue: 8 | Title: DEATH OF MISS CARPENTER. — Miss | Author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT]

	DEATH OF MISS CARPENTER. — Miss
Mary Carpenter, who was well known for
the active part she has taken in the reform-
atory movement, and in the promotion of
ragged schools and of female education,
died a short time since at Redhedge, Bris-
tol. She was the daughter of the late Rev.
Dr. Lant Carpenter, of Bristol, and was
born in 1807. She had published several
works on the best methods of dealing with
youthful criminals, and on various other
philanthropic and educational subjects. She
read many papers before the Social Science
Association, and paid four visits to India
for the purpose of promoting female educa-
tion and prison reform in that country. her
last visit was made in 1875-76; and only
recently a parliamentary paper was issued
containing two letters which Miss Carpen-
ter had written to Lord Salisbury, at his
Lordship's desire giving her views upon
the questions of female education and prison
discipline in India.

	PETITIONS FOR a Sixteenth Amendment
for Woman, from 10,000 United States citi-
zens, from twenty-two States, have been
presented in open House and Senate by
thirty-one Representatives and twenty-nine
Senators since January 9th, 1877. The
friends of Woman Suffrage in both Houses
who intend to advocate this amendment in
the new Congress, desire its friends to sus-
tain them by mammoth petitions from
every quarter. It is earnestly desired that
all who have a desire to see woman in pos-
session of the right to advancement on an
equal footing with her brother man, will
sign these petitions, and thereby show to
the world that the shackles of superstition
and false education can be unriveted with-
out the  heretofore popular methods of war.
	Woman has fully demonstrated wherever
an opportunity has been given her, that
she is capable of taking her place side by
side with man, intellectually, and as the
ballot ought to be governed by intelligence
there is no reason why woman should be
deprived of its use.—Wolcottville (Ind.)
Gazette. 

	THE DEATH of Lady Sterling-Maxwell,
(better known as Hon. Mrs. Norton,) for-
merly Miss Caroline Elizabeth Sarah Sher-
idan, occurred recently; she was born in

1808, and was the daughter of the late Mr.
Thomas Sheridan, and granddaughter of 
the Right Hon. Richard Brinsley Sheridan.
At a very early age she shewed a taste for
authoriship, and produced "The Dandle's 
Rout," with illustrations from her own de-
signs. In 1821 she published "The Sor-
rows of Rosalie," and in 1831 a poem called
"The Undying One," based on the legend
of "The Wandering Jew." These were
followed by several volumes of poems and
works of fiction some of the latest of
which were "Lost and Saved," published 
in 1868. Miss Sheridan married, about
1829, the Hon. George C. Norton, a brother
to Lord Grantly and for many years a mag-
istrate at the Lambeth Police court. Mr.
Norton died in 1875; and on the 2nd of last
March, Mrs. Norton, who had for some time
been confined to her room, was married at 
her own residence to Sir William Sterling-
Maxwell, Bart. M. P.


	MRS. Virginia Barnhurst. Secretary of
the Natioal Woman Suffrage Association,
Philadelphia, has written some of the
strongest and mose able articles on the wo-
man question. In the "Ballot Box" of Sep-
tember she has an article entitled "Careless
Admissions," which strikes us as being
well-timed and to the point. We would
gladly publish it in full but can now only
qote part of it: "Women have reay noth-
ing to a of men in thi matter. no more 
than one man has to ask of another, exept-
ing to request that they will not interfere
with the exercise of their rights, and when 
they appeal to the government, as they now 
are doing, it is not for RIGHTS, but for pro-
tection in the exercise of rights." Mrs. B.
says, "I once heard one of our prominent
woman speakers from the platform, in a 
sweet, pathetic and quite imploring voice
appeal to the men in the audience with "It 
is so easy to give us the right; you can if
you will." All of this, however concilatory,
tame, and traditionally womanly, goes to 
make an admisses that is neither intended,
nor one that can be made false as it is with-
out drawing upon us ever ready to criticism.
It means nothing more nor less than that 
woman has not the right to vote-that man 
has, and, too, that he has the authoirty to 
give her the right, whenever his lordship
may, in his own good time, see proper to
be so agreeable and condescending.
	In the first place women are not asking
for rights. Rights are something that can
neither be given not taken away, and if it 
were otherwise she would not be likely to 
go to man for them, as according to Ameri-
can autonomy rights are equal in citizen-
ship, and thus one citizen has no more power
than another, and has nothing to give to
that other which that other, like him or
her, does not possess. If by some HOCUS-
POCUs it has come that man is in EXERCISE
of the franchise and woman is not, this 
does not weaken or destroy woman's right
to it, neither does it give him the least au-
thority over her right."


MRS. J. Southworth Spaulding will lec-
ture this winter on "How shall we Help
Ourselves" and "Popular Fallacies."


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

author: Unknown
title: WOMAN'S MASS MEETING IN SAN
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol18 | 1889 | August | Issue: 5 | Title: WOMAN'S MASS MEETING IN SAN | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN'S MASS MEETING IN SAN
PETE.

	According to previous call and appoint-
ment, a large number of the ladies of Manti,
assembled on the 20th of June, 1889, to or-
ganize themselves into a Suffrage Association.
Besides the ladies, a few influential citizens
were present who evinced a warm interest in
the proceedings, although they took no formal
part in them. Audience sang,

     "Guide Us, Oh, Though Great Jehovah."

Mrs. Rebecca Wareham offered prayer.
Audience sang, 

	"Earth With Her Ten Thousand Flowers,"

	Mrs. E. B. Wells of Salt Lake City, stated
the object of the meeting to be the organiza-
tion of an association of women of all ages,
for purposes of improving ourselves in matters
literary, formal, social and political; especially
in that or all branches of politics, which
touch on woman's condition and interests in
general, and her state of disfranchisement in
particular.
	Mrs. Zina D. Young of Salt Lake was
chosen temporary chairman, and Mrs. Lucinda
L. Dalton of Manti, temporary secretary.
	The name of San Pete Co., Woman Suffrage
Association was adopted, and the following
named ladies nominated and sustained as 
officers of the permanent organization.
	Mrs. Ellen Jakeman, of Manti, President;
Mrs. Esther S. Anderson of Manti, First
Vice-President; Mrs. Helen Armstrong of
Ephraim,Second Vice-President: Mrs. Tina M.
Erickson, of Mt. Pleasant, Third Vice Presi-
dent; Miss Mary Ann Frantzen, of Spring
City, Fourth Vice-President; Miss Clarrisa
Faux, of Moroni, Fifth Vice-President; Miss
Augusta Swalberg, of Gunnison, Sixth Vice-
President. Mrs. Sarah P. Vorhees, of Manti,
Secretary. Mrs. Lucinda L. Dalton of Manti,
Corresponding Secretary. Mrs. Margaret Kjar
of Manti, Treasurer.
	Executive Committee: Mrs. Catharine Brown
Chairman; Mrs. Belle Reed, Mrs. Margaret
Sloan, Mrs. Mary Luke, Mrs. Sarah P. Rey-
nolds.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells read the Constitution of
the National W. S. A., and it was moved
seconded and carried that the same be adopted
as the Constitution of the San Pete W. S. A.

[Column 2]

	Mrs. Wells read the By-Laws of the Utah
W. S. A., and it was moved, seconded and
