carried that the same, with some slight changes
be adopted as the By-Laws of the San Pete
Co., W. S. A.
	It was also moved seconded and carried,
that the San Pete Co., W. S. A., be an auxili-
ary of the Utah W. S. A.
	Mrs. Wells expressed an earnest hope that
this cause will be well supported in San Pete
Co., and that all members and friends will
work together in harmony and order. Re-
counted some of the noble efforts which had
been made by the friends of woman in the
east to prevent the outrageous proceeding of
robbing the women of Utah of the Franchise,
after they had been in peaceable possession for
seventeen years. Opined that so long as men
shall retain their magnificent physicial organi-
zations and proportions, and women their ma-
ternal instincts, there need be no fear that
women will ever take away any of the rights
of men. Said we only need our own on fair
and equal terms. Was satisfied that no less
than ten thousand men in Utah believe in
Equal Suffrage.
	Chairman, Mrs. Young, said the women of
Utah could not be accused of any stain of
wrong-doing in their use of the ballot. They
held, used, and valued that right, for the
good they could do themselves, their sons and 
daughters, and humanity in general. We
hope to regain this inestimable right, and in
some degree help other women to gain it.
	The first regular quarterly meeting was
appointed for Friday, June 28, 1889.
	No further business being presented, a mo-
tion to adjourn was carried, the audience sang
Doxology, and closing prayer was offered by
Mrs. Mary Ann Hyde of Spring City.
		LUCINDA L. DALTON, Sec., protem.


The roses of pleasure seldom last long
enough to adorn the brow of him who plucks 
them, and they are the only roses which do not
return their sweetness after they have lost their
beauty.
	Francis Minor, of the St. Louis, Mo., bar,
has brought out a pamphlet arguing that
women have the right to vote for members of
Congress under the U. S. Constitution. It is
entitled “An Argument in Support of the
Law of Federal Suffrage.”
	The International Congress of Woman’s
Rights, held in France recently, reported the
name of Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Honorary
President for America, Mrs. May Wright
Sewall, of Indiana, was present as one of the
delegates from America. Mrs. Belva A.
Lockwood, of Washington was in attendance
and also attended the Peace Congress.

author: side, with the yet few, but increasing,
title: PHILADELPHIA, PENN., Aug, 1877.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | September | Issue: 7 | Title: PHILADELPHIA, PENN., Aug, 1877. | Author: side, with the yet few, but increasing,]

	PHILADELPHIA, PENN., Aug, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	In receipt of your little paper, I can truly
say that I am pleased with it,—further I
am more than pleased. I find it a noble
effort in behalf of woman's advancement,
and it well deserves to take its place side
by side, with the yet few, but increasing,
earnest and effective exponents of the new
Evangel of this nineteenth century. In
the name of my sisters in the States, en-
gaged in this grand cause so dear to us all,
I send greetings and best wishes, for the
success of your worthy enterprise. It is
no small matter to keep a paper in existence
after it is born, and the effort to nurse it up
into the strong, healthful, potent life de-
sired for it, calls forth all the energy, perse-
verance and fertility of brain, in those who
have imposed upon themselves the noble
task.
	I am glad to have read the WOMAN'S EX-
PONENT, and am sorry not to have made an
earlier acquaintance with it. I am this
glad, and this sorry, for more reasons than
one,—the one particular reason being, that,
I have received light upon a subject in re-
gard to which, I was not only in the dark,
but in regard to which myself in common

[Column 2]

with the people generally in the States,
have been, as I now believe, deceived,—
that is, as to the condition of the women of
Utah, as woman and as citizen. After hav-
ing read the two articles in your July 15th
number "The Elective Franchise in Utah,"
and "Work for Women," on went my
thinking cap, and I assure you, I have come
frm under this utile piece of brain-gear
somewhat wiser than when I donned it. It
is the truth, and I am going to make a clean
breast of it, for there is no shame in being
misled, only in staying so in the face of
evidence, that the women of the States
have jumped at very unjust conclusions in
regard to their sisters in Utah. Upon a
"sealed book no one is able to pass judg-
ment" and certainly no one has a right in
public, where opinion is influenced, to com-
ment upon a subject in regard to which
they are ignorant. Who have been our
teachers I cannot say, but I confess that
the women here, in counting our victories
and in summing up the advance in re-
spect to the franchise of our sex, while
considering Wyoming as the first trophy
taken in the grand contest for the right, do
not look upon women suffrage in Utah as
worth a fillip. In fact we never speak of it
as we do of the first as a government for, of
and by the people, and we only and silently
once in awhile think of it as a government
wherein man is the tyrant and woman the
submissively tyrannized over—that each
man has not merely his own vote, but just
as many votes as he owns wives, and that
each woman is either an oriental doll or a
domestic drudge, with neither impulse nor
impetus towards an individualized exist-
ence, nor any other ambition than that of
pleasing her lord and rightful owner. In
short, that women are "driven like a flock
of sheep at the will of the driver."
	I have given you frankly an expose of
the opinion in the States of the position of
the women of Utah. May your able little
EXPONENT do the work of disproving what
I now truly believe to be erroneous in the
highest degree, and if well circulated, I
am confident that it has the power to do so.
	The question of woman's political status
is the first question—this once decided, her
other interests will regulate themseles;
and upon this question, should the women,
both of this land and over the seas, meet in
harmony and good faith.
	I hold that we Suffragists in the States
have nothing to do with the religious cou-
ditions, or the social tastes or condition of
our sisters in Utah, nor with those of each
other even, and it is to be regretted that
there are those, who do make it their busi-
ness to mix things up in this way.
	I will mail you samples of the tracts and
envelopes of the Citizen's Suffrage Associa-
tion an organization formed in 72—an off-
shoot of the older association, and the bold-
est, most uncompromising, most democrat-
ic of all—whose one tenet is Suffrage the
inherent right of the citizen of a Republic
—Universal Suffrage, irrespective of sex,
color, caste, creed, condition, the basis of a
true Democracy.
	I have just read your article in "Woman's
Words." Why not endeavor to show what
you are doing in Deseret through one of
our daily or Sunday papers, I am sure the

[Column 3]

would be very happy to publish anything
you might write.
	I shall look forward to the appearance of
your new book "The Women of Mormon-
dom."
	With the kindest wishes I remain your
sister in woman's cause,
	MRS. VIRGINIA BARNHURST.

author: Unknown
title: WASHINGTON CONVENTION.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol13 | 1884 | February | Issue: 17 | Title: WASHINGTON CONVENTION. | Author: Unknown]

WASHINGTON CONVENTION.
	The evening session  of Tuesday brought out
a larger attendance and there were several
members of Congress present. Matilda J.
Gage delivered an address on the political par-
ties of the country, showing they had been suc-
cessful or had failed according to their strict
adherence to or desertion of principle.
	Mrs. Stanton followed,speaking on "The
Disabilities and Limitations of the sex." She
glorified motherhood as the divinest function;
referred to Horace Greeley's limitation of
woman's usefulness, in which he said she could
neither navigate a ship across the sea, nor sing
bass in a choir, by stating that woman had
done both. She laid at the doctrine of celi-
bacy endless evils and ills, and asserted that
the religious devotees of the moral ages were
responsible for the darkness of that age. She
criticised St. Paul's strictures on marriage,and
condemned the practice and principle of celi-
bacy in pungent and unmeasured terms.
	Mrs. Laura de Force Gordon was the last
speaker. She gave a history of the work in
California together with an interesting account
of the contest over the admission of women to
practice at the bar. Women are now admitted
to all callings and professions in California, sex
being declared no disqualification by the Con-
stitution, by a clause which Mrs. Gordon had
herself drafted.
		SECOND DAY.
	The meeting was called to order by Miss An-
thony. She told her hearers not to be discour-
aged. There were but four bills in the Senate
ahead of the suffrage bill and that already the
friends of the measure were preparing their
speeches on it. She said if women were half
as sagacious as men they would note down
points in behalf of the bill and send them to
one of the senators favoring the measure, that
he might incorporate them in his speech.
	Dispatches were read from Helen M. Gou
gar and Mrs. Lockwood, and letters from Clara
Barton, Robert Purvis and Rachel Foster.
	Mrs. Perkins of Ohio then read a most able
and forcible address, quaint, touching and tell-
ing, showing full womanly and matronly in-
stincts, an unfaltering trust in the Creator, an
abhorence of intemperance and a resolute and
immovable conviction that the cause of woman
was just and should be secured.  “There had
been too many Jane Carlyles in the past,sitting
up all night to make bread  for a grumbling
husband. Victor Hugo had said the 19th cen-
tury belonged to women, and he, rather
than the bearish English philospher, should
be taken as the true representative of his
age."
	Caroline Gilkey Rogers then read a well-
prepared and interesting paper on the “Aris-
tocracy of Sex." In the course of her address
she gave a description of a speech made by
Old Sojourner Truth in a suffrage convention
years ago, by which the entire current of
thought and feeling was changed. "If de fust
woman that God eber made was strong ’nuff to
turn the worl" upside down," said the tall and
powerful ex-slave, "den dese heah women on
de platform oughter be able to set her right
agin."
	Miss Burr read an allegorical essay showing
the condition of a world where the distinction
of sex in the walks of life had been oblitera-
ted and that this world was surpassingly and 
painfully inferior.
		RESOLUTIONS.
	"Whereas a bill for an amendment to the fed-
eral constitution prohibiting the states from
disfranchising citizens on account of sex is now,
with reports for and against its passage, before
the Congress of the United States; therefore,
	Resolved, That we request our representa-
tives in congress to pass this bill, and submit it
to the legislatures of the several states.
	Whereas it is conceded to be the duty of
overnments to protect the persons and proper-
ty of their citizens against all aggressions by
land and sea; therefore,
	Resolved, That it is clearly the duty of the
United States, on this principle, to protect the
women of the several states, when robbed by
tyrannical custom or unjustlegislation of their
fundamental rights by extending to them the
rights of suffrage.
	Resolved,  That in the death of the Hon.
Henry Fawcett, Wendall Phillips, Senator An-
thony, Madam Mathilde Annekk, Frances D.
Gage, Sarah Pugh, Elizabeth T. Schenck,
Katherine Newell Doggett, Laura Giddings
Julian, Judge Folger, Bishop Simpson, and
William Heney Channing, the year 1884 has
been one of irreparable losses to our move-
ment, and as an association we would express
to their families and friends in England and
America our deep sympathy in their affliction
and our gratitude for their life long services for
the elevation of women.
	Resolved, That we note with great satisfac-
tion the growth of sentiment favorable to
woman suffrage in the W. C. T. U., and that
we thank them heartily for their recent memo-
rial to congress in behalf of the sixteenth
amendment.
	Whereas the dogmas incorporated in relig-
ious creeds derived from Judaism, teaching
that woman was an afterthought in creation,
her sex a misfortune, marriage a condition of
subordination, and maternity a curse, are con-


[Column 1]

trary to the law of God as revealed in nature
and the precepts of Christ; and
	Whereas these dogmas are an insidious poi-
son sapping the vitality of our civilization,
blighting woman, and laying their palsying
hand upon humanity; therefore,
	Resolved, That we denounce these dogmas
wherever they are enunciated, and we will
withdraw our personal support from any or-
ganization or person so holding and teaching,
and
	Resolved, That we call upon the Christian
ministry, as leaders of thought, to teach and
enforce the fundamental idea of creation, that
man was made in the image of God, male and
female, and, given equal dominion over the
earth, but none over each other. And fur-
ther, we invite their co-operation securing
the recognition of the cardinal point of our
creed that in true religion there is neither
male nor female, neither bond nor free, but all
are one."
	These resolutions, introduced the first day,
were read and adopted, save the last one, which
created an unusual sensation. Men and women
joined in the debate and the discussion took a
partly religious turn and threatened to result
disastrously. It was discussed pro and con
vigorously and was beginning to call forth
criminating attack upon special religious or-
ders, when Mrs. Stanton arose and spoke ear-
nestly, and conclusively in its behalf, and then
said further consideration would be postponed
till the morning following when a special ses-
sion would be held to consider it. The after-
noon meeting then adjourned.
	The evening session was devoted to ad-
dresses. Miss Hardman of Philadephia spoke
on the subject, "As the Rulers, so the People,"
stating that the only way to overcome mob
rule was to substitute virtue for vice, to give
the more moral half of the community—the
women—representation, and the result would
be attained. Women for the same work shoud
receive the same compensation, and yet the
only place where men did not cheat the women
was where women had the right to vote.
	Rev. Olympia Brown,for 20 years a preacher
in the Universalist Church, spoke to the text,
"All are created free and equal." The aris-
tocracy in power, she said, was that of the
moustache, whose insignia was the spittoon,
whose home was the smoking car, and whose
outward sign was the curling tobacco smoke,
and which was dependent upon an accident of
the body. 
	The concluding speaker of the evening was
Lillie-Devereux Blake. Her subject was the
"True Republic." She said the term "repub-
lic." was not fulfilled in this country. A young
woman here who desired to earn her living had
offered to her the alternative of starvation or
degradation. She hoped the Bartholdi statue
would never be erected until women had the
right to vote. 
	It was decided not to hold a morning session
to discuss the resolutions which created such a
tumult during the afternoon.

author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | June | Issue: 1 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT]

NOTES AND NEWS.
			     ----
 THE  NEW England Woman Suffrage As-
sociation held their ninth annual meeting,
commencing Monday evening May 28, at
Tremont Temple, Boston, Mass.  Mrs. Ju
lia Ward Howe the president of the Asso-
ciation being absent, Mr. Henry B. Black-
well called the meeting to order and laid the
business of the Convention before the as-
semblage.  Mr. I. T. Fields in the open-
ing appropriately, speech alluded to Sidney
Smith as being one of the earliest and
strongest champions of women’s rights.
Mrs. Lucy Stone in her remarks also allud-
ed to Sidney Smith, to whose disinterested
labors women all over the world were in-
debted.  She announced that Mrs. Mary A.
Livermore and Wendell Phillips would be
among the speakers at the convention on
Tuesday evening.  The business meeting
began in the Meionaon Tuesday morning,
with an audience of about five hundred.
The first business was the report of the
Chairman of the Executive Committee.
Mrs. Lucy Stowe read the report, which
stated in substance that the treasury had
been replenished through means raised by
a Festival and contributions so as to accom-
plish the work of the year without a debt.
 Many excellent speeches were made, and
reports from different societies brought for-
ward, appeals from various places to the As-
sociation in convention assembled, etc.  We
have not space to publish, or scarcely touch
on these speeches from distinguished people
of the world engaged in this public work,
but will give one paragraph from the speech
of Mrs. Mary A. Livermore concerning
Utah, which will show how falsely this
people are placed before the public, and
will call forth an article or two upon this
subject in our next issue.
 "In Utah women vote.  But both men
and women are ticketed, and receive from
the authorities a ballot corresponding with
the number they bear.  It is fair to say
that the women do not want to change them.
If men want women who always do exactly
what the men tell them, you will find them
in Utah.  These women command my sym-
pathy and respect.  They obey their reli-
gious convictions of duty.  The graveyards
of Utah are filled with women who have
died broken-hearted in obedience to their 
consciences; women who have given up
their husbands to other women because
they thought it to be their duty.”


Rebecca Andersen, of Seneca Falls N.
Y., is the oldest old maid in the world.
She is one hundred and twelve.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT


[Column 1]

author: ﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT
title: EDITORIAL NOTES.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol1 | 1872 | April | Issue: 21 | Title: EDITORIAL NOTES. | Author: ﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT]

﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.
	New York proposes to allow women own-
ing $250 in property to vote. Liberal New
York!
	Corrupt nations march to ruin with
more certain tread than they ever do to
victory or greatness.
	The Congressional whitewashing sea-
son is about over; but several darkened
reputations yet show discoloring stains.
	A very sensible lady, speaking of the
follies of fashion, remarks, concerning the
“Grecian Bend:” “Had the Creator desired
to see women with humps on their backs,
He would so have created them.”
	An eight year-old “country lassie” is re-
ported to have named one of her chickens
after a prominent Utah official, “’cause,”
she says, “that old hen is always cackling
when there’s nothing to cackle about.”
	Utah has received two new Associate
justices. If they are an improvement upon
their predecessors, the Territorial Supreme
Court quorum of three would not be injured
by another similar change.
	The friends of Woman Suffrage in
Massachussetts, are grieved at the action of
the Legislature of that State, in which, not-
withstanding the Republican platform, the
prohibition of equal political rights for
women was voted down by a large majority.
They declare themselves “defeated but not
conquered;” and they will eventually gain
the victory.
	“Strikes,” on a large scale, are threat-
ened again, in the East. Those of last
spring cost the employers and workmen of
New York over thirty-three millions.
That is the way capital and labor cut each
others’ throats. Both desire to make money,
and yet by these “strikes” prevent the
accomplishment of the object. Were there
less pride and stubborness among them,
arbitration might be successfully resorted to
for the settlement of their difficulties.
	The hunt for offices has been, so great
that President Grant has lamented the pres-
sure upon his time and patience from this
cause. It seems that the glory of the aver-
age masculine American, throughout the
States, is to become a politician, and the
aim of the politician is to secure office. The
country is viewed as a fat oyster which it is
the precious privilege of every member of
the winning party to open and enjoy.
	George Francis Train is confined in
the Tombs, the principal prison in New
York, for publishing the “Train Ligue,” on
the charge that it contained obscene litera-
ture; and a physical who has examined
him says he is insane. We have not seen
the “Train Ligue," and consequently cannot
speak as to its contents; but it is possible
Train’s peculiar style of telling the truth
has considerable to do with this medical
opinion as to his insanity.
	Six out of ten vacancies in the Internal
Revenue Department, at Washington, to be
filled by competition, were gained by
women, who doing the same work as men
receive the same pay. This is gratifying,
yet the right thus conceded should have
been recognized long ago. It was in Utah
that the right of women to support them-
selves as clerks, telegraph operators, and so
on, was first publicly and practically
acknowledged, and now it is being admitted
all over the country.
	The Editor of the San Francisco “Pio-
neer”does “not believe that Celestial mar-
riage; or polygamy, is an agreeable, natural
condition for men and women.” Let her
honestly and intelligently contrast polygamy,
as found in Utah, with that condition of
society impotently fought against, which
exists so largely and but semi-covertly in
San Francisco and elsewhere; and say which
social system she considers the more agree-
able and honorable.
	Foster, the “car-hook murderer,” was
hanged on Friday, March 21st, in New
York. His crime was the killing of one
Avery D. Putnam some two years ago, in a
street car, because Mr. Putnam endeavored
to protect two ladies from insult. On the
same day McElhaney, the Massachussets’
wife murderer, was also hanged; he had
brutally killed his wife. Charles Mortimer
has been found guilty of murder in the first
degree, in Sacramento, California, for the
killing of Sarah Gibson. These three cases
are enough for one issue of the Exponent,
to convince its readers how fast the world
that berates the Mormons for their “peculiar
views”’ on the woman question, is traveling
toawrds woman being protected and treated
as she should be.
	The special session of the United States
Senate closed on the 26th ult., not one of the
resolutions for expulsion of corrupt members
of Congress having passed either branch of
the national Legislature. Senator Patter-
son, of New Hampshire, accused of corrup-
tion and perjury, escaped because his term
of office had expired; Senator Pomeroy, of
Kansas, was declared innocent in the face of
very positive evidence of his guilt; Senator
Caldwell, from the same State, resigned,
although it was believed, that a vote to
expel him would have failed; Senator Clay-
ton, of Arkansas, was declared innocent;
while in the House, Ames and Brooks were
censured for admitted corruption, but not
expelled.
	If every, man in the country could be
paid as well for his services as the Rev. Dr.
Newman, palatial residences and comfortable
carriages should be more plentiful than they
are. Dr. Newman receives his salary as
pastor of the Methodist Episcopal Metropo-
litan Church, in Washington; he also re-
ceives $7,500 for being chapalin of the Senate
of the United States; and he has lately
received the appointment, from President
Grant, of Inspector of Consulates, as he pro-
posed a pleasure trip around the world, by
which he has the expenses of the trip paid,
and receives besides, $6,000, in coin. Un-
doubtedly he is a fortunate man, and his
good fortune is multiplied upon him because
he was defeated in discussion by Elder
Orson Pratt, in the New Tabernacle of this
city, when he came to overthrow Mormon-
ism, but was himself overthrown,
	The house of Representatives of the
New York Legislature, in session at Albany,
on Monday, March 24th, instructed the
Judiciary Committee to bring in an amend-
ment to the State Constitution, providing
that tax paying women citizens shall have
the right to vote. There are two anomalies
in this country that would bear explanation,
considering that “taxation without repre-
sentation” is held to be a grievous and
radical wrong. One is, why the people of
the Territories should be taxed by Congress
without a vote, by representative or dele-
gate, in imposing the tax, or disposing
of the money thus raised after it has been
collected. And the other is, why women
who own property and are taxed, all over
the United States, are refused representation
of any kind, except in two of the Territories
thus unjustly dealt with. The women and
the Territories only ask their just rights
from government and from the States.
	The question “are women citizens of
the United States within the meaning of
the Fourteenth Amendment to the Consti-
tution?” is expected to be shortly decided
by the Supreme Court of the United States,
in a suit brought by Mrs. Myra Bradwell
of Chicago, against the State of Illinois, for
refusing her permission to practice law.
Should the decision be in her favor, it will
make woman suffrage a fixed fact in every
part of the Republic, and will not then be
in the power of any demagogues to take the
right of suffrage from the women of Wyo-
ming and Utah, the only portions of the
United States where the elective franchise
is enjoyed by the sex. With this principle
sustained by the judicial branch of the
government, it might be said that the
builders budded more firmly than they
knew when framing that amendment, which
was intended to confer certain rights upon
the colored population but would be found
unwittingly to extend to women as well.
That this would work a greater revolution
in politics than the emancipation and enfran-
chisement of the blacks no one can doubt;
and some of the best jurists in the country
contend that the decision of the supreme
tribunal is certain to confirm it.
	“Was it a Ghost?” is the question agi-
tating a portion of Minnesota at present,
over the following rather curious item
which we clip from an Eastern paper: “In
the great storm which proved fatal to so
many people in Minnesota, one John
Weston, of Worthington, was overtaken on
his way home through the woods. Three
days after, his wife, who was anxiously
awaiting his coming, heard as she
thought at the door the voice of a neighbor
saying, ‘Mrs. Weston, John is frozen to
death.’ She went at once to the door, but
nobody was there. So, six days after, an-
other neighbor saw the missing man stand-
ing about fifteen feet from, the stable door.
He addressed him with, 'why are you
here, we thought you were frozen?’
When the ghost answered, ‘So I am, and
my body lies a mile and a half northwest of
Hersey.’ With this the ghost vanished.
The depth of the snow prevents searching
for the body at present.” Should the body
be found, at the place thus indicated, the
question now agitating that community will
be as far as ever from settlement; for the
world is unwilling to believe the truth in
regard to such phenomena, and prefers to
ascribe them to hallucination of the mind, or
accept the first theory that meets approval.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[For	the	Exponent.

author: Unknown
title: THE SUFFRAGE IN VICTORIA.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | August | Issue: 4 | Title: THE SUFFRAGE IN VICTORIA. | Author: Unknown]

THE SUFFRAGE IN VICTORIA.
Advocates of Woman Suffrage in this
country will learn with something like envy
that a measure similar to that which has so
often come to grief in the Imperial Parlia-
ment has become a Cabinet question in
Victoria. The Prime Minister of that
colony has submitted his project to his col-
leagues, and it will be interesting to watch
for the result. There is no intention to pro-
pose the enfranchisement of women at
large, for which change the time has not
arrived either in Australia or the United
Kingdom. The measure simply provides that
all women who obtain a place on the municipal
register shall be eligible also for the Parlia-
mentary register. These women may be
married or unmarried; all the requirement is
that they shall be of full age and entitled to a
municipal vote according to the laws of
Victoria, and the municipal roll shall furnish
the basis of that for Parliamentary purposes.
Colonials move faster than their kindred at
home. The roots of their institutions are not
yet deep; very few obstinate prejudices have
descended to them as inheritance, and they
can face problems unshackled by sentiments
and considerations which retain vitality in our
older system. They are setting a good example
in this endeavor to remove a harsh disability
from the gentler sex, and we trust their en-
lightened courage will be rewarded with suc-
cess.—Liverpool Mercury.

author: Unknown
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol5 | 1876 | June | Issue: 2 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: Unknown]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	THE DEBATE of Woman Suffrage in Par-
liament in England, has relatively far great-
er importance than any Congressional vote
here, because the decision of Parliament
must decide the question for the whole
kingdom, and in this country it is distribu-
ted and decided by Legislatures in the dif-
ferent states. This Bill is not only for Suf-
frage but to extend to such women as vote
in municipal elections, the right also to vote
for members of Parliament. Mr. John
Bright's speech in Parliament on this Bill
has called forth criticism from all persons
interested in the subject, and the Woman's
Journal says, "There is many a humble
home in England and America, where his
picture heretofore honored, will henceforth
be turned to the wall. Since Webster's 7th
of March speech there has been no down-
fall so conspicuous; and Whittier's grand
poem of "Ichabod," seems its only fitting
lament. The liberal press of England and
Scotland are profuse in their criticisms,
many of them very cutting and pointed.
There are many things which tend to prove
that public sentiment is with the extension
of Woman's Suffrage." One thing particu-
larly encouraging to those who are anxious
the Bill should pass, is that one half of the
minority are new members. It is not to be
supposed because the Bill has been rejected
that it will never pass. "In the Parliament
of 1841, Mr. Charles Pelham Villiers, asked
again and again that the Corn Laws might
be repealed, and over and over again the
House of Commons rejected the proposi-
tion. But in the year 1846 the same House
of Commons that had refused to listen to
him, passed a measure repealing the Corn
Laws." Mr Forsyth in his speech in the
house, moving the second reading of the
Bill said there had been no single meeting
against the principle of the Bill, but on the
contrary in favor of it crowded meetings
had been held in every large town in Eng-
land, Scotland and Ireland, and resolutions
by large majorities in favor of the Bill have
been enthusiastically adopted.

	THE WOMAN'S meeting in Boston closed
its session in May to open again in Novem-
ber. This association has for its object
more particularly "the unfolding of the aes-
thetic and spiritual elements of human na-
ture, than the mere literary and scientific,
though these are by no means ignored."
Reading the last sentence reminds us forci-
bly that these meetings of the Boston wo-
men are very similar to the organizations of
the women of Utah, the chief aim and ob-
ject of which is the higher cultivation of
the spiritual nature, though the physical,
moral and mental elements are not lost
sight of.


AN old detective at Troy says: "If I de-
sired to train my son for a detective, I
would first make him a newspaper reporter,
that beyond any other business is the busi-
ness that makes stupid men smart, ordinary
men able, and capable men brilliant; and
what an experienced reporter cannot detect
is not worth detecting."

author: Unknown
title: WOMEN'S SUFFRAGE.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol5 | 1876 | June | Issue: 2 | Title: WOMEN'S SUFFRAGE. | Author: Unknown]

		WOMEN'S SUFFRAGE.

	"Recently a public meeting in favor
of women's suffrage was held in St.
George's Hall, Langham plaee, Mr. Russel
Gurney, M. P., the Recorder of London,
presiding. The hall was quite full, and
among those on the platform were Miss
Becker, Miss Sturge, Miss Frances Power
Cobbe and Miss Tod."
	The chairman, after congratulating the
audience upon so large a meeting, after a
temporary discouragement in the House of
Commons, went on to enumerate some of
the items contained in the Bill which had
just recently been rejected. He thought the
Bill corrected the injustice which existed
and he argued that he could not see consist-
ency in allowing one landholder or a person
occupying house No. 13, and paying rates,
for that house, being allowed to vote, and a
person occupying No. 14, and bearing the
same burdens, not being allowed to vote
merely because there was a difference of
sex in the two occupants. He denied that
if women voted they would be enti-
tled to the right of sitting in Parliament,
any more than clergymen who had votes,
but had been prevented for more than a
century from sitting in Parliament. He
could not see why women should not have
their views represented: they could not al-
ways take the same view of questions as
men. He alluded to the complaints coming
mostly from wives, sisters, and daughters;
the subject of the injustice done to younger
sons was also brought up, that the eldest
sons carried off all the property, yet young-
er sons were not excluded from the fran-
chise.
	Miss Becker rose to propose the first reso-
lution as follows:—"That this meeting
hereby records its approval of the assimila-
tions of the conditions of the Parliamen-
tary, to those of the Municipal franchise in
so far as regards the disabilities of sex, and
pledges itself to support the principle by
every constitutional means." Before speak-
ing to it, however, she wished to read the
following letter:—
			"39, Sloane-street, May 13.
	"Madam,—I regret that I cannot comply
with your request in attending the meeting
at St. George's Hall. You must not on that
account, however, infer that I am unfriend-
ly to its object, I have voted in its favor on
more than one occasion, and doubtless I
shall do so again. It may be, as some think,
that no great advantage at this time would
accrue to the country by extending the
electoral suffrage, but I cannot see any prin-
ciple or consistency in withholding it, es-
pecially from those whose cause you are
pleading. The Legislature has conferred
the right on women of voting for their mu-
nicipal representatives, of voting for, (and,
indeed, acting as) Poor Law Guardians, and
the same with regard to School Boards, and
they have always been entitled to fill cer-
tain parochial offices, and it is never alleged
that in the discharge of these duties they
have acted less worthily than men. In
most respects, their rights as owners of
property are concurrent with those of men.
They can inherit peerages, and, as we know
to our satisfaction, they can occupy the
Throne. I cannot see, therefore, on what
ground it is that they can reasonably be said
to be disqualified from determining upon
the claims of candidates who seek to repre-
sent their interests in Parliament, especi-
under the peaceful condition in which they
would now record their votes.
	"As far as I am acquainted with the ob-
jections usually alleged, I am bound to say

[Column 2]
that they appear to be those which have al-
ways been offered whenever fresh extension
of liberty to the subject has been proposed,
and which in former years I have myself
heard urged against personal freedom in
the Colonies, religious liberty in this coun-
try, the enfranchisement of the working
classes, and against the abolition of every
monoply (political or commercial) whenever
it has been assailed. We have seen, how-
ever, with what little success resistance up-
on these great matters has of late years been
attended, and with what satisfaction the
results of past changes are now regarded by
the country. You, then, have little reason
to feel discouragement at the present posi-
tion of your cause. You have only to per-
severe, making it clear to the public that
your claim is just and possible to be enfore-
ed—and that political rights are now with-
held where there is intelligence and capaci-
ty to use them wisely and where the posses-
sion of them would afford protection and
benefit to those who demand them.
	"With great respect, dear Madam, I re-
main your obedient servant,

author: Unknown
title: CONSTITUTION.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol17 | 1888 | January | Issue: 16 | Title: CONSTITUTION. | Author: Unknown]

CONSTITUTION.
	ART. 1. This organization shall be called
the National Woman Suffrage Association.
	ART. 2. The object of this Association shall
be to secure national protection for women in
the exercise of their citizen's right to vote.
	ART. 3. All citizens of the United States
subscribing to this Constitution, and contribut-
ing not less than one dollar annually, shall be
considered members of this Association.
	ART. 4. The officers of this Association


[Column 1]

﻿shall be a president, vice-president from each
of the states and territories, corresponding and
recording secretaries, a treasurer, and an exe-
cutive committee of not less than five.
	ART. 5. A quorum of the executive com-
mittee shall consist of nine, and all officers of
this Association shall be exofficio members of
this committee, with power to vote.
	ART. 6. All woman suffrage societies
throughout the country shall be welcomed as
auxiliaries, and their accredited officers or duly
appointed representatives shall be recognized as
members of the National Association.
	Chairman: "You have heard the Constitution
of the N. W. S. A., what is the pleasure of
the meeting?" Mrs. Alder: "I move it be
adopted." This motion was seconded by a large
number of ladies present, and the  Constitu-
tion was adopted.
	Chairman suggested that some one nominate a
Committee on By-Laws. Mrs. Dougall moved
that the Chairman appoint the Committee.
Chairman appointed Mrs. Lottie Young, Mrs.
Julia C. Taylor, Edith Knowlton, Committee
on By-Laws. Committee retired.
	While the Committee were out Mrs. C. I.
Kirby addressed the audience. She spoke of
the advantages to be derived from woman
suffrage, and presented some of her views and
the information she had obtained at home and
abroad in regard to woman's work and influence.
When Mrs. Kirby concluded, Mrs. Jane S.
Richards was called for, but declined; and Mrs.
Z. D. H. Young responded to her name, and
addressed the meeting. Her speech was elo-
quent and pathetic; she appealed to the heart:
her subject was, "Woman and Motherhood,"
and her ideas were so gracefully conveyed, that
every heart was touched in a tender place.
	The Committee came in and reported, and
Mrs. Julia C. Taylor read the "By-Laws and
Regulations." After the reading some one
moved that they be read and voted upon separ-
ately. The Secretary read as requested, and
the By-Laws and Regulations were adopted
separately. The following is a copy:

author: Unknown
title: HIGGINSON SPEAKS FOR HIMSELF.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol17 | 1888 | March | Issue: 19 | Title: HIGGINSON SPEAKS FOR HIMSELF. | Author: Unknown]

HIGGINSON SPEAKS FOR HIMSELF.
	Col. T. W. Higginson having been, as usual,
quoted by the attorney for the remonstrants,
as suggesting practical difficulties, the follow-
ing letter was read to the committee:
	CAMBRIDGE, Feb. 6, 1889.
DEAR MR. BLACKWELL:—I am sorry that I
must be in New York on Friday; but I will
authorize you to say to the committee, if you
think best, that I hope that they will report in
favor of extending suffrage to women. I think
there can be now no doubt that they will exer-
cise it freely, if they have the opportunity.
That they will always exercise it wisely, espe-
cially at first, is not to be expected. Men do
not always vote wisely, though they have had
much more practice than women. All great
extensions of the suffrage bring in a new and
inexperienced class, and the immediate results
are not always good. But the ultimate result
is always to educate and enlighten the emanci-
pated class, and to benefit the whole community
in the end. Women will probably vote, on
the whole, quite as judiciously as men do; and
they need the ballot for self-respect and for
self-protection.
	Very truly yours,
THOMAS WENTWORTH HIGGINSON.

author: Unknown
title: CONVENTION.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol5 | 1876 | May | Issue: 24 | Title: CONVENTION. | Author: Unknown]

			CONVENTION.

	The Tenth Annual Convention of the
National Woman's Suffrage Association,
will be held in Masonic Hall, corner 6th,
Ave. and 23d St. New York, May 24, 1877.
	"The great effort of the National Wo-
man's Suffrage Association this year, will
be for the 16th amendment which shall
specifically declare woman's right to the
ballot. The right of petition is woman's
only recognized method of influencing leg-
islation; a million names to the 45th Con-
gress would have great moral influence up-
on Congress and the nation. The United
States already has three classes of voters,
the black man, naturalized male citizens,
and amnestied rebels; each of these has
right to the ballot under United States au-
thority. Though by decision of the Su-
preme Court, in case of Virginia L. Minor,
of Missouri, all women were remanded to
the States, yet as the National Government
had power to invest with the ballot in the
above instances, it has like power to en-
franchise woman.
	"Let us therefore meet and discuss the
best methods of attaining this great object."
	The circular is signed by Matilda Joslyn
Gage, Ch. Ex. Com., and the information is
respectfully given also that all contributions
to this convention should be sent to Ellen
C. Sargent, 1733 De Sales St., Washington,
D. C. Letters should be addressed to Mrs.
Gage, Fayetteville, N. Y.

author: Unknown
title: SALT LAKE COUNTY.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | March | Issue: 16 | Title: SALT LAKE COUNTY. | Author: Unknown]

SALT LAKE COUNTY. 
	The regular monthly meeting of the Salt
Lake Co. W. S. A. was held in the 13th ward
Assembly Rooms, Feb. 16, 1892. Pres. Eliza-
beth Howard in the chair. Opened by sing-
ing from the Suffrage Song Book "O come,
come away." Prayer by Mary A. Freeze.
Roll called. After which 14 new members
were voted in; as no programme had been
prepared Pres. Howard said she would be
pleased to hear from the ladies present.
Dr. E. S. Barney read a sketch,—Some of
the reasons why it is necessary for women to
be interested in obtaining the power of the
ballot.
	Elizabeth McFarlane said she would be
pleased to see our young ladies come out and
educate themselves to hold the positions that
await them; experience gives us education.
Women are the peacemakers of the world,
they have fought many bloodless wars. Spoke
of the work accomplished by the great women
in India in settling the slave question; and the
blood that might have been spared had our
own Government accepted the advice of our
women. It has been estimated that had the
bodies of men killed in the late war been put
into coffins and placed end to end they
would have made a complete band around the
world. Spoke of the steps being taken by our
women to prevent war with Chili, and related
other interesting instances of history on the
same subject.
	Miss Louie Poulten favored us with a song
which was beautifully rendered; and for which
she received a hearty vote of thanks.
	M. Isabella Horne said, I desire all to under-
stand I am in favor of the elevation of woman.
Since the death of our Savior men have
oppressed women more and more and tried to
keep them in ignorance. We can see how
rapidly she advances when she is allowed some-
thing of the same opportunities and privileges
as men. Spoke of the honor shown to women
of the Scriptures; women have more real
courage, as strong minds and intellects as their
brothers. A gentleman once told her that God
made man first, "I answered, yes but He im-
proved very much in his second manufacture.
I was invited to attend a debate on this subject
by a party of young men and women, the argu-
ments were very interesting." Also referred
to an article in our last EXPONENT.
	Vice Pres. Louise K. Spencer made a few
pleasing and encouraging remarks.
	Bathsheba W. Smith, "I hope you will
steadily increase in numbers and intelligence;
let us continually bear in mind that we have
duties to others as well as ourselves, if we have
all the rights we care, for there are many who
have not.
	Elizabeth Price made some remarks in re-
gard to the suffrage entertainment of the pre-
vious evening.
	Vice Pres. Phebe C. Young, "I think suf-
frage ladies should be minute women and al-
ways prepared to say or do something when
called on," related in a very interesting man-
ner a sketch of the life of Empress Josephine.
	Emma Finch 20th, and Julia Ridges 22nd
wards, were appointed to act as agents of the
suffrage society in their own wards.
	Pres. Howard, had been greatly pleased


[Column 1]

with the exercises of the day and with the en-
tertainment on the previous evening. "The
other day in looking over one of my old
journals, it was recalled to my mind that I had
the privilege of dining with Acting Governor
Mann on the day he had signed the bill grant-
ing suffrage to the women of Utah; when he
told me he had done so, I arose to my feet and
in my feeble way thanked him in the name of
the women of Utah for having done so.
	Meeting adjourned until the third Tuesday
in March at the same hour and place.
			M. A. CAINE, Ass't. Sec.

author: Unknown
title: TRYING TO SOLVE THE PROBLEM.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | December | Issue: 10 | Title: TRYING TO SOLVE THE PROBLEM. | Author: Unknown]

TRYING TO SOLVE THE PROBLEM.

	Republicanism, what is it? is it today what
it was a century ago? Webster’s unabriged
Dictionary does not give the definition of what
it is today. The next great author and de-
finer will be under the necessity of coining a
new word to give the full meaning of what
Republicanism is at present. I used to think
when a little girl listening to my father in
the exciting times when Dick Johnson of
Tenn., was a candidate for the Presidency and
the Editors were resorting to all kinds of
slander, for Johnson had a colored woman for
a wife which ’twas said was the cause of his
defeat some of his enemies published a poem
defining the political strife that was raging
as follows: that class of politicians that could
be bought and sold were mongrel puppy, whelp,
and curs of low degree. I thought then Re-
publicanism was the father of the Constitution
and the Declaration ot Independence covered
the whole platform of freedom and equal
rights to all of her citizens, are women citizens
if not what are they? Are they classed with
lunatics and crimnals, no wonder women are
contending for their rights when the adminis-
trators of this glorious government have en-
tered the opening wedge. They use political
chicanery pass unconstitutional bills, send out
Commissioners to confiscate Church property,
administer test oaths; those who have bought
their seat in the sacred halls of this govern-
ment with that demon alcohol for the purpose
of violating one single clause in the Constitu-
tion will pass away in dishonor and when they
meet the patriots gone before, their counten-
ance, will be fallen. Geo. Q. Cannon was
the first Apostle that ever graced the halls of
Congress, I feel safe in saying he never bought
one single vote, when votes can be bought and
sold such men are not qualified to keep
sacred and inviolate that heaven born inspired
Constitution that guarantees to all its citizens
the right to worship God according to the
dictates of their own conscience; when the iron
heel of oppression stood heavily upon the neck
of our Puritan Fathers, our Mothers brought
forth patriots and statesmen such as never
graced the earth, who gave their lives to be-
queathe that sacred boon liberty to their pos-
terity who are now in exile. Judge Zane
reports thirteen hundred American citizens
having been imprisoned and even women with


babes in their arms for their religion.
In the days of Herod when Jesus was
arraigned before rulers and Judges He opened
not His mouth like a sheep before the shearers,
He was dumb; when reading the impertinent
questions asked the Apostles I was forcibly re-
minded of the humility of the Savior. Women
can tell the Government how to solve the prob-
lem: let Congress pass a law requiring every
man to marry a wife and the great majority
of women petition that honorable body to
make it lawful for them to have a husband.
Motherhood is the brightest crown that woman
wears, for she not only fills the halls of Legis-
lation with her own sons, but the ranks of the
battlefield with the offspring of her own body and
yet she has not the franchise nor the privilege
of answering the end of her creation.
					E PLURIBUS UNUM.

author: Unknown
title: MILLARD CO., W. S. A.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol19 | 1890 | May | Issue: 21 | Title: MILLARD CO., W. S. A. | Author: Unknown]

MILLARD CO., W. S. A.

	The W. S. A.  of Millard Co., over which
I have the honor to preside; and which I was
delegated to represent at the N. A. W. S.Conven-
tion, is one of which I am proud, and I think
justly so, for we have a large membership list,
and many interesting and instructive meetings
are held, where subjects are discussed of deep
interest to the higher education, and better
social status of woman.  In the two years of
my presidency I have met but four individuals
belonging to the county who expressed them-
selves, opposed to woman having the full right
to the suffrage and equal rights in all social
and political matters with men.  And while
we call ourselves the Woman Suffrage Asso-
ciation, we take a broader, more liberal view,
and ask that the same right should be ex-
tended to every citizen of every country, in-
dependent of creed or color.  And while much
is written, and said about the Indian, and his
right to the suffrage of which many would de-
bar him, we would freely accord to him all
the rights, we as women, demand for our-
selves.  Is he not the rightful owner of the
soil of America?  are we the intruders, or is he?
surely we are the usurpers; he was here when
Columbus landed, and for how long before
who shall attempt to say.  It is fashionable to
laugh at his ignorance and sneer at his primi-
tive manners.  How long is it since the un-
tutored ancestors of our vaunted civilization
were as rude and uncultured as he?.
	Immense sums are annually expended upon
foreign missions; let some of this mission work
be done at home; devote some of the funds
for the clothing and educating of the red
man.  Have him taught a trade or otherwise
employ him,and he will soon be able to clothe
himself, and provide for his own wants, be-
come a useful member of society and repay
us many times over for what we have done
for him.  The negro is another stumbling
block, and eye-sore to many.  Has he not al-
ready suffered enough at the hands of the
white man?  should not his freedom be full
and complete?  it was dear enough purchased,
let us not curtail it.  Freedom should be given
with no niggard hand.  Make the qualifica-
tions for the suffrage an educational one, to
the extent Wyoming has, if you will; keep it
from the criminal if you choose, otherwise ex-
tend it to ALL, especially to woman.
	How is it that men, who love freedom so
well, withhold it from their mothers, wives
sisters and daughters?  Is it from jealousy or
cowardice?  They know that woman's percep-
tion is quick to discern, that she will soon dis-
cover the "right man, for the right place;" they
know that drink will not corrupt, or money
purchase her vote; do they fear to subject
themselves to the test?  I have never yet heard
a man give a valid reason for witholding the
suffrage from woman, nor do I think they can
find one.
	The time is not far distant when the con-
cession will be forced from them, why not give
it at once, gracefully, and earn the thanks of
the women of all nations, rather than wait,
and be told, "to you no thanks are due, for
you were compelled to be just to our sex."

author: Unknown
title: THAT BILL
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol2 | 1873 | January | Issue: 16 | Title: THAT BILL | Author: Unknown]

			THAT BILL
	The perusal of the new Frelinghuysen Bill
has filled me with indignation; and being,
for the present at least,an American Citizen,
I take the liberty to express my opinion on
the subject.
	First, I would like to know how any man
dares to call himself a son and citizen of
this liberal Republic while advocating such
arbitrary measures. Not content, it seems,
with trammeling the free action of the Ter-
ritorial Legislature, he would even enslave
the future State by having it enacted by
Congress that “no laws shall hereafter be
passed conflicting with the provisions of this
Act.” One Act, at least, in the code of the
future State must remain immutable as the
laws of the Medes and Persians! Then this
Sovereign Slave, Deseret, may fold her arms
and content herself with observing the pro-
gress of Sister States in this direction until
lost to her view, and she left alone in her
chains.
	How pure and exalted is the friendship,
how tender and winning the sympathy
which this bill holds out to a large class of
virtuous and high-minded women in this
Territory!
	If they will repudiate the honorable name
of wife,and, by their own admissions, stamp
their children as illegitimate, they may
have—a suitable proportion of their hus-
band's property! How magnanimous the
offer! how charming the prospect!!
	Does Mr. Frelinghuysen, or any person of
sound mind, for one moment imagine that
there is money enough even represented by
the National Debt to buy from one of Utah’s
right-minded daughters her children’s birth-
right? her own proud sense of honor? or her
steadfast faith that she is walking in the
straight and narrow way which leads to life
everlasting? I fear he judges her by report
and not from personal acquaintance.
	Then, again can any unprejudiced person
call it just that we be deprived of our fran-
chise at the whim of interested or disinter-
ested politicians who make not even a pre-
tense of showing a reason for it? And that 
too, not only for a season, with the hope of
regaining, at some future day,the great loss,
but since “No Act shall hereafter be passed
&c.,” though all sister States might enfran-
chise their daughters, Utah might not do
the same.
	Although woman suffrage is here yet a
bold and unsupported fact, though no wo-
man has yet been called to serve on a jury
nor elected by vote to any civil office, I
doubt if Mr, Frelinghuysen himself, values
his vote more than I do mine, if I am a wo-
man; for I look upon it as the concession of
the government that daughters are human
as well as sons, and as a stepping stone to
all other privileges; so I maintain, not only
my inherent right to a voice in the laws I
must obey, but that the very least the“Pow-
ers that be” can do for us is to give us a fair
chance and plenty of time to prove whether
we are not at least as well qualified to make
good use of the ballot as a freedman or a
foreigner. Then as for the insinuation some
cast upon us that we dare not vote other-
wise than as our husbands dictate, or that
neither male nor female voters dare vote ex-
cept as the Church authorities think best,
I can say for the former that so far as my
information extends, there is not a shadow
of truth in it; and for the latter I have struck
off an objectionable name from the ticket
which most church members voted entire and
witnessed the numbering of it, without the
remotest idea that any disagreeable con-
sequences to myself would follow.
	It would be hard indeed, if after having

[Column 2]
tasted the sweets of liberty, we were to be
remanded to the level of idiots and felons.
And by the provisions of this most unre-
publican bill we are to be subjected to the
old common law which gives all a wife’s in-
herited property, earnings, children, person,
all but life itself to her husband’s absolute
control; thus in all save that one reservation,
setting her on a level with domestic ani-
mals. What have we done to merit such
overwhelming kindness as this?
	But, as I said in the beginning,I am only
surprised and indignant at the effort being
made, not alarmed about the result. we poor
Minor Children of the Union have too many
friends among those who have attained to
their majority, and Woman Suffrage and
equal rights as principles have too many
friends for such measures to be counten-
anced. God speed the day when not only
we, but all women may enjoy franchise and
all kindred privileges unquestioned.
							L.L.D.

author: Unknown
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol13 | 1884 | November | Issue: 11 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: Unknown]

NOTES AND NEWS.
				______

	A woman suffrage convention will be held in
Lexington, Ky., Nov. 5 and 6, under the aus-
pices of Mrs. Mary B. Clay, of Whitehall, Ky.,
president of the American Woman Suffrage
Association.  Mrs. Gougar and Mrs. May
Wright Sewall,of Indiana, and Mrs. Elizabeth
Boynton Harbert, of Illinois, are among the
speakers.—Ex.
	Woman Suffrage is being discussed in the
Normal Institutes in Iowa.  Two Hundred
teachers in Des Moines, offer a discussion of
the question, decided in favor.  P of. Akers,
State Superintendent of Public Instruction,
said recently in a lecture, “The equal citizen-
ship of women is coming—coming rapidly—
and no man can stop it!”  The sentiment was
highly applauded.—Ex.
	“A monument in the shape of a simple obe-
lisk bearing the relief portraits of Dr. Martin
Luther and his Katie—“Dame Zolsdoerferin,”
as he was wont to call her—was recently erec-
ted on the site of the homestead in which she
passed the years of her widowhood.”

author: Unknown
title: NOTES AND NEWS.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | December | Issue: 13 | Title: NOTES AND NEWS. | Author: Unknown]

NOTES AND NEWS.

	THE PRESENT term of Congress opened
at Washington on the 7th inst. President
Grant sent in an unusually lengthy message,
but so many subjects had to be presented
that "woman suffrage" or the "Mormon
question" were neither of them referred to.
	THE PROPOSITION to grant women the
right of suffrage, which was lately submit-
ted to the vote of the people in Michigan,
was lost. But its defenders are not in the
least awed in consequence of this failure; a
correspondent to the "Woman's Journal,"
heads an article upon the subject, "Defeated
but not Subdued."
	MRS MALLOY, connected with the press
at Elkhart, Indiana, says she loves her
present line of business so well that she
can work sixteen hours out of the twenty-
four at it, and her work does not seem bur-
densome. She considers that journalism is
especially woman's work, and that any wo-
man who naturally takes to it will succeed
where most men would utterly fail. Presi-
dent Young entertains much the same
opinions and has several times illustrated
them from the stand.
	A HOME for widows and single women
has lately been opened in Philadelphia by 
ladies of the Presbyterian church in that 
city. Woman may be regarded as a worth-
less inconvenience by some of the unappre-
ciative "sons of Adam"—and alas! it must
be acknowledged, sons of Eve too—but
since she has begun to comprehend how
much there is for her to do in the reform-
ing of the world, she has gone to work with
a will and a purpose, and is accomplishing
a vast amount of good.
	IT IS asserted that in ancient Egypt
women were honored and respected equally
with men; their opinions were considered
quite as worthy of notice; and their names,
on such occasions as weddings were as likely
to precede those of the gentlemen as other-
wise.  An extraordinary high degree of
culture is also said to have been fairly
claimed by the race.  These two statements
hang together well, and may be regarded as
another evidence in favor of equal rights
being extended to men and women.

author: Unknown
title: WOMAN'S RIGHT OF DOWER.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol11 | 1882 | December | Issue: 13 | Title: WOMAN'S RIGHT OF DOWER. | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN'S RIGHT OF DOWER.

	The right of dower in Utah has been the
theme of much discussion in private and public,
in the press of the country and among the offi-
cials of the Territory. They acknowledge they
are sore on this subject, and think it an outrage
to the people that the right of dower should have
been abolished by the Legislature of Utah. Any
rational person who examines the ground care-
fully, without prejudice, would say at once that
the statutes of Utah as regards married women
and widows, is far in advance of the right of
dower as existing in most of the States of the
Union.
	In Utah woman holds her own property—her
husband cannot touch it; in most of the States
the husband can not only squander his wife's
income, but he can collect her earnings and
squander it at the gaming table or in the grog-
shop, just as he pleases or chooses. Not long
since, a lady related to the writer a little incident
illustrative of the very deplorable condition of
woman's right to her own earnings. The lady
was traveling on the train. She was a student
of human nature, and had noticed a very pale,
delicate-looking girl, sensitive and shrinking,
with a book in her hand; the lady was interested
in the appearance of the child, and opened a
conversation with her by inquiring where she
was going? She answered to the next village;
her father was with her, going on to collect her
week's earnings. He was a dissipated, brutal-
looking man. The child was deformed, had a
hunch on her poor back, and she looked weary
of life. She said her mother earned a little
money towards supporting the family by taking
in washing, but her father was cruel, and there
was no law to prevent his taking her hard earn-
ings from the factory and using them for his
own gratification. Is it any wonder that the in-
telligent women of the world want the ballot,
in order to get the laws remodeled, and better
the condition of the industrious working-classes,
that they may not be the prey of the indolent,
the dissipated or cruel?
	This is only one instance of the thousands
that occur every day, and yet women whose
"lives are cast in pleasant places," sit at their
ease, in blissful ignorance of the laws and gov-
erments, because, forsooth, a strong arm shelters
them from care and hardship, and so they ex-
claim, "We have all the rights we want."
	The right of dower is no more nor less than a
sort of vassalage, and a relic of the old common
law of England, and through this came the ap-
pelation relict, as applied to the wife after her
husband's decease; a word which, from its as-
sociation in that sense, is specially distasteful to
independent women. In the statutes of Utah
woman is a person; she can hold and convey
property, and she has the same right to do so
that her husband has. We give below the sec-
tions as laid down in the Revised Statutes of
Utah, and let every woman consider and judge

[Column 2]
for herself whether this is not preferable to the
right of dower, which strictly speaking means
one-third of her husband's estate, for she has
none of her own in law, and that very estate
may have come as a legacy to her from her
father or other relative, or it may be much of it
her own accumulated hard earnings. Here is
the enactment:
	(1020.) SEC. 1. Be it enacted by the Governor
and Legislative Assembly of the Territory of
Utah: That all property owned by either spouse
before marriage, and that acquired after by gift,
bequest, devise or descent, with the rents, issues
and profits thereof, is the separate property of
that spouse by whom the same is so owned or
acquired, and separate property owned or ac-
quired as specified above, may be held, managed,
controlled, transferred and in any manner dis-
posed of by the spouse so owning or acquiring it
without any limitation or restriction by reason
of marriage.
	(1021.) SEC. 2. Either spouse may sue or be
sued, plead and be impleaded, or defend and be
defended at law.
	(1022.) SEC. 3. No right of dower shall exist
or be allowed in this Territory.—Compiled Laws,
page 342.
	In the laws governing the estates of decedents,
it is provided that:
	"Any married woman may dispose of all her
estate by will, and may alter or revoke the will
in like manner as a person under no disability
might do." (Ibid p. 271.)
	We have not time to enumerate all the points
involved, but, added to the law as it stands re-
corded, the women of Utah have the elective
franchise, and the privilege of voting at all elec-
tions in the Territory. If either of the late
Governors of the Territory had been as "liberal"
as the party title they have taken suggests,
women would have been ere this eligible to
offices of emolument and trust, and some might
for aught we know heve already been elected.
But even under the present conditions, the
women of Utah have more liberties and privi-
leges than those of other States and Territories.
In the "despised Mormon Church" women vote
on all questions the same as men do. This
scarcely accords with the current idea outside,
that Mormon women are oppressed and kept in
bondage.


[Column 1]

author: Unknown
title: FORTIETH ANNIVERSARY OF THE
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol16 | 1887 | February | Issue: 18 | Title: FORTIETH ANNIVERSARY OF THE | Author: Unknown]

    FORTIETH ANNIVERSARY OF THE
	 WOMAN SUFFRAGE MOVE-
				MENT.
	The International Council, which will be held
next month in Washington, D. C., and of which
we have already published several notices, is to be
a more elaborate sort of Jubilee for women than
was at first anticipated, even by the ladies who
planned the campaign. Miss Susan B. Anthony
has been interviewed by Washington reporters,
and has spoken quite wittily upon the subject.
	Dear Miss Anthony, whom all suffragists love
very dearly, has the most ready wit at her com-
mand, and reporters or interrogaters either never
get the best of her. We have always noticed,
whether on the platform or in the reception or
drawing-room, she always has an answer ready
for every satirist, and so cool and collected as to
preserve her womanly dignity under the most
trying circumstances Her courage and equan-
imity are unsurpassed. Such women as Miss
Anthony are rare, and in genuine originality she
equals any of the leaders in the suffrage cause.
Mrs. Stanton and Lucy Stone are, perhaps, more
motherly in their views and thoughts, but Miss
Anthony is a leader born, and carries both the
mothers and daughters along with her, fearlessly,
independently and without hesitation. Young
women look up to her as an oracle almost, and
her very manner gives prestige to her work. No
one who has ever seen Miss Anthony can help
noticing her peculiar face and figure, so positive
in its character, so remarkable in its outline; and
so emphatic is she in her declaration of right, and
denunciation of wrong, that she cannot fail to
impress her convictions upon the public.
	We venture to say this movement will forward
the suffrage for women that has been delayed al-
most everywhere for forty years, and that States
and Territories will, with renewed energy, take
up the subject and accomplish something credit-
able in favor of the ballot for women. The asso-
ciations to be represented are to be mostly of a
national character. Miss Anthony said to a Wash-
ington reporter, “When first we thought of hold
ing this woman’s jubilee none of us imagined we
should find more than half a dozen Nationally
organized associations of women, and the Secre-
tary tells me we have sent out to associations
nearly forty official invitations.”
	The question then asked Miss Anthony was.
“Have you invited only nationally organized bodies
of women?” Miss Anthony’s reply was, “That
has been our rule so far as invitations are con-
cerned. Besides these, we have invited a number
of distinguished women to speak on various kinds
of unorganized work. For instance take the various
professions. We have women physicians, lawyers
ministers, publishers, editors. No associations
of women in these lines of work exist, yet they
must be heard of at our council if we wish to
make it anything approaching a complete picture
of what women have done and are doing. So we
[column 2]
have invited women who are acquainted from
practice with these professions.” From this one
may gather a little what the nature of the Council
will be.
	By letter from Mrs. Spofford, the Treasurer, we
learn that the price for the use of Albaugh’s Opera
House will be $1,800, but she says, “The largest
churches here will not hold the people that come
to our regular meetings.”
	The programme, of the International Counci
was not quite complete at the latest information
we received, but among the delegates from nation-
ally organized bodies of women in this country
are Mrs. Mary A. Livermore, Mrs. Lucy Stone,
Miss Francis Willard, Mrs. Caroline Buell, Mrs.
Julia Ward Howe, Miss Mary F. Eastman and
Miss Clara Barton. The Western Association of
Collegiate Alumnae send their President, the
eminent microscopist of the Michigan University,
Louise Reed Stowell. Mrs. Frazier will represent
the Moral Education Society. Mrs. Laura Mc-
Neir, the ladies of the G. A. R. Rev. Antoinette
Brown Blackwell, the first woman ordained to the
ministry, will be present; also Rev. Anna Shaw,
the only ordained Methodist women. Pendita
Ramabai will speak for the women of India. Fin-
land, Denmark and Sweden are to send representa-
tives. France has elected Madame Bagelot of
Paris as a delegate,and England sends to the
Council Miss Helen Taylor, step daughter of
John Stuart Mill. Miss Henrietta Muller, a mem-
ber of the London school board, will come to the
Council. and also Mrs. Alice Scratcherd, a promi-
nent woman suffragist in  England, Helen
Campbell, author of the “Prisoners of Poverty,”
who, on account of her work in England, will not
be able to attend in person, will send a paper,
which will be read, as also a report by Fanny
Zampini Salazaro of Rome. This lady is editor 
of the Review of the interests of women pub- I
lished in Rome, and will write upon the work of
Italian women.
	This altogether new movement on the part of
tbe N. W. S. A. is distinctive in itself, and will
do more towards making woman suffrage popular
than any work that has yet been done, or any
convention that has been called. It is bringing
together in a General Assembly the brightest and
ablest leaders, and the most intellectual women of
the age that are before the public. It is an inspira-
tion in the advancement of the cause of woman
that will promote all the several interests of the
great subjects under consideration by both men
and women of progressive thought.
	Industrial enterprises that will give employment
to woman and to girls, by which they can gain a
livlihood in the over-crowded cities of the old
and new world, is one of the most important mat-
ters to be hoped for from the ballot in the hands
of women, or from the powerful influence of a
popular body of interested workers in this direc-
tion. That much can be done in suppressing vice
by industry there is little doubt. When pure-
minded women move earnestly and in unity upon
some of these momentous questions at issue, and
when noble, lion-hearted men are willing to join
harmoniously in these great and high en-
deavors for the bettering of the condition of
those who are powerless to lift themselves, in-
stead of buying votes for whiskey then there will
be something permanent accomplished. Mean-
time let us hope that Congress will treat no other
class of women as its members have treated
“Mormon” women after they had been given the
ballot, and voted for seventeen years, to
take it away without cause or provocation
of any kind and only this frivolous pre-
sumption, “We have the power and you can’t help
yourselves.’
	The worst excuse for not granting suffrage to
other women is that “Mormon” women voted a
“Mormon” tictket. “It was quoted as 
trong argument why none of the women in
[column 3]
the United States should be permitted to vote.”
This does seem ridiculous but nevertheless the
statement is published in the papers.

author: A PLURAL WIFE
title: STILL WE WILL PROTEST.
text:
[WomansExponent | Vol15 | 1886 | September | Issue: 7 | Title: STILL WE WILL PROTEST. | Author: A PLURAL WIFE]

	STILL WE WILL PROTEST. 

	The enemies of the Latter-day Saints will
persist in keeping them continually in the at-
titude of defense. We are not an aggressive
people; most gladly would we walk in the
quiet paths of peace and improvement, but
the attacks constantly made upon our people, 
practices and religious institutions compels us
to defend them against the most bitter and un-
just assaults from all quarters. 
	Were we really the vile and abominable peo-
ple that our enemies would have us appear—
why is not the truth, plain, indisputable truth, 
sufficient to arouse a nation's hatred without
the everlasting resort to the manufacture of
malicious lies by the wholesale. The women
of Utah had not recovered from the shock of
disgust and indignation occasioned by reading
a living abomination, called by Mrs. Angie F. 
Newman of Nebraska, "A petition to Congress
to deprive the women of Utah of the right of
franchise," in which she displays the most in-
tense hatred and ignorance of the subject
which she essays to handle with such eloquence
—her charges are a burning insult to thousands
of her sex, as intelligent, virtuous and noble
women as ever graced this fair earth, and for 
the second time I felt it my duty to refute her
malicious misrepresentations, but ere I began 
the task. the visit of the G. A. R. to Salt Lake
City, and some of the speeches made at the
Pavilion has furnished other demands for the
women of Zion to defend themselves against
another volley of cruel indignities and vile 
insults, proving that the efforts of such works 
as Mrs. Newman's have not been in vain, and 
the most unjust prejudice has found fruitful
soil in the hearts of ignorant and thoughtless
people who apparently have been made willing
victims of falsehood and deception. That
people at a distance should be deceived by so
many false and unscrupulous teachers, is not
amazing, but that such people as fill the ranks
of the G. A. R., professing the principles of
Christianity, and representing the patriotism of
this great nation should come here to the capi-
tol of our Territory and display their ill-gotten 
information and entire ignorance of the people
and situation is truly marvelous. 
	They talked about liberating the "slaves," 
the women—" Mormon" women, of course. 
Until the "Mormon" women acknowledge this
position and assume the attitude of slaves, 
pleading deliverance, such sentiments are a
gross insult to our womanhood, and we de-
cidedly resent it. We have not been trained to
ignobly submit to insult from man, having
always been treated with the greatest respect 
and deference by the brotherhood of this
Church, and we will not quietly submit to in-


[Column 1]
dignities offered under the guise of patriotic
gallantry.
	I was born on the once barren soil of Salt
Lake City, a child of freedom, proud of my
birth right, trained to freedom of thought,
freedom of speech and liberty of action con-
sistent with the rights of others. Some of my
earliest recollections are of the celebrations of
Independence day, when I learned to love the
Stars and Stripes and honor the Constitution,
which guaranteed to all "life, liberty and the 
pursuit of happiness, and to every creed the 
right to worship God according to the dictates
of conscience." These ideas are indelibly
stamped upon my soul, and I emphatically
deny ever being taught disloyalty to the govern-
ment of the United States.
	And from the principles of the Gospel as
taught by Jesus Christ in former days and
again established on earth through Joseph
Smith in this dispensation, I have received ex-
tended views on the rights and privileges of
woman, and I know that the Church of Jesus 
Christ of Latter-day Saints proclaims the
greatest freedom and broadest charity for
woman. She is regarded as man's equal, and
his duty to protect, honor and cherish her is
clearly defined.
	Let those who would learn of the true status
of woman in this Church, throw away all no-
tions obtained from the ravings of anti-Mor-
mon zealots. Let reason govern all your in-
vestigations, and make a memoranda of a few
facts, furnished by the people upon whose
cause you are to sit in judgment.
	The women of this Church are charged with
being slaves. In face of the fact that she has
an equal vote in all ecclesiastical affairs, which
prevents the possibility of tyranny from the
Church, she still holds the right of suffrage
(despite the determined efforts of our pre-
tended emancipators to wrest it from us,) and
as the ballot is secret, political despotism is a 
myth. Again, women have perfect freedom
of speech, and are thoroughly organized and
trained in public speaking. I don't think
there is a community on earth where women
have greater privileges in this respect.  Then
she has full liberty of the press; the columns
of every newspaper are at her service, and
women even publish a paper, in which every
woman is urged to express her views and senti-
ments on all subjects.
	Women have on several occasions embraced
the privilege of holding mass meetings, when
thousands of women throughout the Territory
have assembled and emphatically protested
against the wrong, injustice and oppression—
not of men holding the priesthood, or any
Church measure, but against the national de-
fenders and protectors of women (?) They
have drawn up memorials, framed protests and
sent intelligent, educated ladies to represent
them at the capitol of the nation, besides sign-
ing petitions in the usual order, with 50,000
signatures. Are these the acts and privileges
of slaves? Let common sense answer. I will
go farther, and state that we have other rights,
which the Gospel and the Constitution have
conferred upon us. Each woman has the
right to the love, care and protection of the
husband of her choice, embracing the right to
fulfill the first great law of her being, to be-
come an honorable wife and mother. Where
is there a community of women more blest?
We have the right to hold land in our own
title, and pay taxes on it, too, but not as many
others, without representation. We have the
right to accept any proposal of marriage, also the
perfect right to refuse, and girls are not driven
to Zion like dumb brutes and married by the
dozen to men in authority. Nothing but fic-
tion will sustain such statements.
	Mrs. Nichols, of the Relief Corps, invited
the oppressed women of Utah to come to New 
York and they should receive help, for there she

[Column 2]
had every right that any woman ought to have;
personally speaking perhaps she has, but can
she say this truthfully of thousands of her
sex? We know she cannot. Many of the
Saints have been rescued from the bondage,
grinding poverty and sins of New York and
other American cities, and they know that
their present condition is infinitely improved
in these pleasant vales; besides, we are a read-
ing community. Were the statistics of New
York never published we might have a little
better opinion of society there. They record
30,000 prostitutes, and as many so-called "sur-
plus women"—(a libel on the economy of her
Creator.)
	By the time Mrs. Nichols and her philan-
thropic sisters have reclaimed and provided
for all there, and the thousands of homeless,
neglected, half-starved and over-worked child-
ren besides, she may consistently come to the
rescue of "Mormon" women. In the meantime
we will enjoy our perfect freedom, our happy
homes, noble husbands, our bright, healthy,
well-cared-for children, and pray for deliver-
ance from outside tyranny and oppression.
